reference: – Tennessee Virtual Academy, a for-profit, online public school heavily supported by state Republicans, has found a novel way of boosting student performance—just delete bad grades, reports News Channel 5 in Nashville. A leaked December email from the school's VP appears to tell its middle-school teachers to erase some scores that don't measure up. "If you have given an assignment and most of your students failed that assignment, then you need to take that grade out," it reads. "To me, this appears like it's grade fixing," said one Democratic state representative. Virginia-based parent company K12 is refusing to talk, but the TVA principal said the deletions were intended to "more accurately recognize students' current progress." But as the two-year-old school will receive $7.5 million in funding from the state this year, many Tennessee lawmakers are outraged. The Knoxville News Sentinel reports that a House bill that proposed shutting the school altogether was killed yesterday; a second was passed by the House Education Subcommittee. It would allow the state to shutter a virtual school that records sub-standard student performance two years in a row. (Meanwhile, a Lehigh University student is suing over a bad grade.)

final_summary:
On November 5, 2017, a devastating mass shooting occurred at the First Baptist Church in Sutherland Springs, Texas, resulting in the deaths of 26 individuals and injuries to 20 others. The perpetrator, identified as Devin Patrick Kelley, 26, opened fire on the congregation during Sunday morning services, targeting a small, tight-knit community where everyone knew each other. Kelley, a former Air Force member, had a violent past, having been court-martialed in 2012 for assaulting his spouse and child, and subsequently serving a year in prison before receiving a bad conduct discharge in 2014.

At approximately 11:20 a.m. local time, Kelley entered the church wearing all-black tactical gear and a ballistic vest. Witnesses reported hearing gunfire and seeing people running for cover as Kelley continued to shoot. A local resident, Stephen Willeford, who was at the scene, grabbed his rifle and engaged Kelley, chasing him in a truck driven by Johnnie Langendorff. Kelley eventually crashed his vehicle and was found dead, though the exact cause of death remains unclear.

The victims ranged in age from 5 to 72 years old, with eight patients being treated at Connally Memorial Medical Center in nearby Floresville, Texas. Four of these patients were transferred to University Hospital in San Antonio for further treatment, with one requiring airlift due to critical condition. Three patients were released from Connally Memorial Medical Center, while one remained in stable condition.

The First Baptist Church, a hub for community services and outreach programs, offered free home-cooked meals to anyone who walked through the door every Thursday. The church's pastor, Frank Pomeroy, and his wife, Sherri, suffered a personal loss, as their 14-year-old daughter, Annabelle, was among the victims. Pastor Pomeroy described Annabelle as "one very beautiful, special child."

As the community struggled to cope with the tragedy, residents expressed shock and grief, grappling with the question of why such an event could occur in their peaceful town. "You'd never think something like this would happen out here – it's so small," said Nick Uhlig, a lifelong resident of Sutherland Springs. The town's residents came together to mourn the loss of their loved ones, holding vigils and prayer sessions, and setting up a makeshift memorial with 26 crosses to honor the victims.

President Donald Trump condemned the shooting as an "act of evil" and called it "horrific," while Texas Governor Greg Abbott declared it the deadliest mass shooting in Texas' modern-day history. The incident sparked widespread outrage and calls for stricter gun control measures, with many questioning how Kelley was able to obtain firearms despite his violent past.

As the investigation continues, authorities are working to piece together the events surrounding the shooting and determine the motives behind Kelley's actions. The FBI and local law enforcement agencies are conducting a thorough investigation, collecting evidence and interviewing witnesses to understand the circumstances leading up to the tragedy.

reference: – There are 26 people confirmed dead in America's latest horrific mass shooting—including the 14-year-old daughter of Frank Pomeroy, pastor of the First Baptist Church of Sutherland Springs, Texas. Pomeroy tells ABC that Annabelle, his youngest daughter, was a "very beautiful, special child." Pomeroy says he was in Oklahoma Sunday in a rare absence from the church. He says all the people killed were good friends. At least three other children were killed in the attack and others are among the wounded. Sutherland Springs resident Nick Uhlig tells the Houston Chronicle that his cousin, Crystal Holcombe, was eight months pregnant and died along with three of her five children. The two others were badly injured. Uhliq says the church provides community services, including free meals, for the tiny town, where the shooting is believed to have killed or injured close to 10% of the population. "Everybody can come, it doesn’t matter what religion you are," he says. Local business owner Dana Fletcher tells CNN that the community about 30 miles east of San Antonio is very small and very tight-knit. "There's two gas stations, the church, a community center, post office, a Dollar General, a tire shop," she says. Amanda Mosel tells the San Antonio Express-News that the congregation numbers around 50. She says her 13-year-old goddaughter was among those killed. The gunman, identified as Devin Kelley, was found dead in his vehicle after the mass shooting. His motive is still unclear.

final_summary:
In the aftermath of the 2012 Sandy Hook Elementary School massacre in Newtown, Connecticut, Sandy Hook Promise, a national non-profit organization founded by family members of the victims, was established to address the issue of gun violence affecting children. The organization aims to turn their tragedy into a moment of transformation by providing programs and practices that protect children and prevent senseless loss of life.

To raise awareness about the warning signs of potential shooters, Sandy Hook Promise launched the "Know the Signs" campaign, featuring a powerful public service announcement (PSA) titled "While You Were Watching Evan." The PSA, released in 2018, tells the story of Evan, a high school student who becomes infatuated with a mysterious classmate through secret messages exchanged on a school library desk. As Evan's romantic escapades unfold, the viewer is distracted from the subtle yet ominous presence of another student in the background, displaying concerning behavior indicative of potential violence.

This second student is seen engaging in concerning activities, including browsing gun-related content on YouTube, being bullied by peers, and sharing images of guns on social media. Meanwhile, Evan remains unaware of the danger lurking behind him, only to discover his secret admirer's identity on the final day of school. Suddenly, an ominous figure emerges, armed with an assault rifle, highlighting the devastating consequences of ignoring warning signs.

Sandy Hook Promise emphasizes that recognizing one warning sign alone does not necessarily indicate a person is planning violence; instead, multiple connected signs over time may signal a trajectory towards harm. The organization encourages viewers to become aware of these signs and take proactive steps to prevent such tragedies. Notably, research suggests that 80% of school shooters and 70% of individuals who took their own lives shared their intentions with someone before acting, yet no one intervened.

Through the "Know the Signs" campaign, Sandy Hook Promise aims to educate the public on identifying at-risk behaviors and empowering them to take action, ultimately preventing further tragedies.

history.draft:
In the aftermath of the 2012 Sandy Hook Elementary School massacre in Newtown, Connecticut, Sandy Hook Promise, a national non-profit organization founded by family members of the victims, was established to address the issue of gun violence affecting children. The organization aims to turn their tragedy into a moment of transformation by providing programs and practices that protect children and prevent senseless loss of life.

To raise awareness about the warning signs of potential shooters, Sandy Hook Promise launched the "Know the Signs" campaign, featuring a powerful public service announcement (PSA) titled "While You Were Watching Evan." The PSA, released in 2018, tells the story of Evan, a high school student who becomes infatuated with a mysterious classmate through secret messages exchanged on a school library desk. As Evan's romantic escapades unfold, the viewer is distracted from the subtle yet ominous presence of another student in the background, displaying concerning behavior indicative of potential violence.

This second student is seen engaging in concerning activities, including browsing gun-related content on YouTube, being bullied by peers, and sharing images of guns on social media. Meanwhile, Evan remains unaware of the danger lurking behind him, only to discover his secret admirer's identity on the final day of school. Suddenly, an ominous figure emerges, armed with an assault rifle, highlighting the devastating consequences of ignoring warning signs.

Sandy Hook Promise emphasizes that recognizing one warning sign alone does not necessarily indicate a person is planning violence; instead, multiple connected signs over time may signal a trajectory towards harm. The organization encourages viewers to become aware of these signs and take proactive steps to prevent such tragedies. Notably, research suggests that 80% of school shooters and 70% of individuals who took their own lives shared their intentions with someone before acting, yet no one intervened.

Through the "Know the Signs" campaign, Sandy Hook Promise aims to educate the public on identifying at-risk behaviors and empowering them to take action, ultimately preventing further tragedies.

reference: – Meet Evan. He's a bored high school kid who starts defacing school property, er, doodling on a table in the library one day. Someone writes back. Messages are swapped as days pass, mystery and romance bloom. Evan wanders the halls, eyeing his classmates, wondering who his mystery penpal is, and as AdWeek puts it, "there ensues a back-and-forth that's pretty captivating. Perhaps too captivating." (To avoid spoilers, stop reading and watch the video.) The end result has been described as "jarring" (Kansas City Star), "chilling" (Mic), and "powerful" (People). While you and Evan are busy being captivated—especially as Evan meets his mystery penpal—one of Evan's classmates is lurking in the background providing warning signs that he's planning a shooting, which in fact interrupts that very meeting we've been waiting for. The ad comes courtesy of Sandy Hook Promise, run by family of the victims of the school shooting in Newtown, Conn. "When you don't know what to look for, or can't recognize what you are seeing, it can be easy to miss warning signs or dismiss them as unimportant," says Nicole Hockley, whose 6-year-old son, Dylan, died in the massacre. The organization, which says 80% of shooters tell someone what they're planning, aims to shine a light on the need to watch for those signs. (In Utah, a teen's own parents disarmed him inside a school.)

final_summary:
In Pennsylvania, inheritance tax is levied on the transfer of a deceased person's assets to beneficiaries through their will, heirs by intestacy, or transferees by law. The tax rate varies based on the recipient's relationship to the deceased individual. According to the state's regulations, no inheritance tax is applied to transfers made to a surviving spouse or to a parent from a child under the age of 21. Transfers to direct descendants and lineal heirs are taxed at 4.5%, whereas transfers to siblings are taxed at 12%. Other heirs, including those who are not charitable organizations, exempt institutions, or government entities exempt from taxation, are subject to a 15% tax rate. Notably, property held jointly by spouses is exempt from this tax.

A notable change occurred for estates of individuals who passed away after June 30, 2012, which introduced an exemption for certain farm land and agricultural properties from inheritance tax when transferred to eligible recipients. Estates affected by this change should review Inheritance Tax Informational Notice 2012-01 for details on the applicable conditions and requirements.

Pennsylvania inheritance tax payments are due upon the death of the decedent and become delinquent nine months after the individual's death. A 5% discount is available when the payment is made within three months of the decedent's death. For additional guidance and frequently asked questions regarding inheritance tax, residents can refer to the brochure "Pennsylvania Inheritance Tax and Safe Deposit Boxes." Those seeking more detailed and historical information on Pennsylvania inheritance tax should consult the Tax Compendium.

reference: – Roland Bosee Jr., 68, and Nino Esposito, 78, have been inseparable since they first met in 1970. "You meet someone and it just clicks," Esposito told the Pittsburgh Post-Gazette last month. So with the SCOTUS decision over the summer to legalize gay marriage, the men looked forward to finally tying the knot. But one issue is holding them back: Esposito is technically Bosee's father, because he adopted him in 2012. "We thought never in our lifetime—or in 20 lifetimes—...same-sex marriage [would] happen," Bosee said, adding to CNN that the adoption "gave us the most legitimate thing available to us" at the time. Even though they petitioned to have the adoption annulled, Allegheny County Judge Lawrence O'Toole —known for being progressive on LGBT issues—rejected that plea, noting that adoptions are typically only dissolved when fraud is involved and that nixing theirs "would place in jeopardy and imperil adoption decrees generally." Adoptions of this sort are often sought to cement legal status for financial and other reasons, as well as to strengthen emotional ties. The men said it was both: Esposito told the Post-Gazette "we felt we lacked a family of our own," but they also discovered that any money they inherited from each other would be subject to a much greater inheritance tax if they weren't considered relatives. One person on the couple's side: Pennsylvania Sen. Bob Casey, who tells the Post-Gazette, "I wasn't aware that LGBT couples were turning to this method. I can't even imagine having to get an adoption petition approved … to visit a loved one in the hospital." Casey has written a letter of support to AG Loretta Lynch, asking her office to "consider issuing guidance" in cases like this; her office is reviewing it, a rep tells CNN. (The gay marriage decision held up a heterosexual couple's divorce.)

final_summary:
In a significant development for survivors of child sexual abuse in Utah, the state legislature has passed a bill eliminating the statute of limitations for lawsuits against perpetrators of such crimes. The legislation, known as HB277, aims to provide victims with more time to heal and gather the courage to seek justice, rather than being forced to confront their abusers within a limited timeframe. 

Deondra Brown, co-founder of the non-profit Foundation for Survivors of Abuse, welcomed the passage of the bill, stating that it allows victims to "breathe, take their time in healing, and come forward and tackle that big legal case when and if they are every ready." Brown's own experiences as a survivor of childhood abuse inform her advocacy for the bill. Her sisters, who were also victims of their father's abuse, pursued criminal charges against him in 2010, leading to his conviction and sentencing to 10 years to life in prison for sodomy on a child, a first-degree felony, and two counts of sex abuse of a child, second-degree felonies.

Under current Utah law, civil actions against perpetrators of child abuse are limited to four years after the victim turns 18. However, HB277 removes this limitation, providing victims with a four-year window from the time of discovery or remembrance of the abuse to file a civil lawsuit. This change is intended to give victims the opportunity to overcome emotional obstacles that often prevent them from seeking justice.

The bill's sponsors, including Rep. Ken Ivory and Sen. Aaron Osmond, emphasized the importance of holding perpetrators accountable for their crimes, regardless of the time elapsed since the abuse occurred. According to Osmond, approximately 1 in 6 boys and 1 in 4 girls in Utah will suffer or have suffered from sexual abuse, and most children do not realize or cope with the trauma until after the age of 40. The bill's passage provides a crucial step towards supporting these victims and ensuring accountability for perpetrators.

Notably, the bill faced opposition from some lawmakers who raised concerns about potential false accusations and the impact on defendants. Despite these concerns, the measure ultimately passed the Senate with a 24-2 vote.

In a related case, a former high school teacher, Michael Layne Williamson, was recently sentenced to prison for sexually abusing a student 22 years ago. During the sentencing hearing, the victim, now 35, shared a powerful testimony detailing the lasting effects of the abuse and the guilt and shame she carried for decades. Williamson apologized to the court, the community, and the victim, acknowledging his actions as "heinous" and pleading for forgiveness. Judge Thomas Willmore delivered a stern rebuke to Williamson, emphasizing that his actions were a deliberate breach of trust and the law, rather than a simple mistake. The judge sentenced Williamson to one to 15 years in prison, with an additional term of up to five years to run concurrently, and ordered him to complete a sex offender program while incarcerated. The victim expressed relief and gratitude for the justice served, but also acknowledged the ongoing struggle to protect her own children from similar harm.

reference: – A retired Utah school teacher was sent to prison for having sex with one of his teenage students 22 years ago, reports the Deseret News. The woman came forward last year and told police that former teacher Michael Layne Williamson, 60, had repeatedly assaulted her over a period of years. He was sentenced to one to 15 years in prison by a judge who berated him for disgracing the "noble profession" of teaching. Williamson, who pleaded guilty in May to aggravated sexual abuse of a child and another count of attempted sexual abuse, apologized in court. "This indiscretion of mine was of the most heinous type," he said. "Let me stop you right there," retorted Judge Thomas WIllmore. "It is not an indiscretion. You broke the law." The unidentified victim, now 35, displayed a photo of herself as an eighth-grader and said Williamson ruined her life. "You put it all on my shoulders, those of a 13-year-old, to carry," she said. "I always blamed myself." She said she left the state and kept silent but on a trip back home, the pain was too much. Supporters of Williamson, however, wrote that he was a respected teacher and golf coach who “fell victim to the persuasions of a girl,” reports the Herald Journal. It's possible this isn't the end of Williamson's legal trouble: Last year, the state changed the law to allow victims of sexual abuse to file civil lawsuits years after the fact, notes the Deseret News. (In Texas, this teacher was accused of having a longtime sexual relationship with an eighth-grade student.)

final_summary:
On October 27, 2018, a devastating attack occurred at the Tree of Life synagogue in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, leaving 11 people dead and six injured. However, the actual number of fatalities was initially reported as 11, but subsequent information revealed that four police officers were also among the wounded. The shooting is considered one of the deadliest attacks on the Jewish community in the United States, although it does not surpass the 1985 killing of Charles Goldmark, his wife, and their two sons by David Morse Rice, which remains the deadliest attack specifically targeting Jews in US history. The Anti-Defamation League (ADL) stated that the attack is "unconscionable" and "unthinkable" given the current state of affairs.

Robert Bowers, a 46-year-old man, has been charged with 29 federal counts, including 11 counts of using a firearm to commit murder and multiple counts of hate crimes. According to the police affidavit, Bowers expressed hatred towards Jews and stated that he wanted all Jews to die, as well as claiming that Jews were committing genocide against his people. Bowers traded gunfire with police and was shot multiple times but survived.

The attack occurred during Sabbath services, when the synagogue's front door was left unlocked, allowing Bowers to enter freely. This vulnerability is indeed common in many houses of worship across the country. Bowers was armed with a rifle and three handguns and targeted a building that housed three separate congregations. The synagogue's staff had not received any prior threats, but security was a concern, and efforts were underway to improve it.

The shooting sparked widespread condemnation and expressions of sympathy from politicians and religious leaders. President Donald Trump ordered flags at federal buildings to be flown at half-staff in respect for the victims and planned to visit Pittsburgh. Thousands gathered for a vigil in the city, with some expressing shock and grief. The incident raised alarm in Jewish communities nationwide, prompting authorities to send extra patrols to synagogues and other houses of worship.

The FBI is leading the investigation, which is ongoing. The agency reported an overall increase in hate crimes in 2016, with anti-Semitic incidents accounting for 684 reported cases, more than any other type of hate crime. The ADL found a significant rise in anti-Semitic incidents last year, with a nearly 60% increase in 2017 compared to the previous year. The organization attributed this surge to a decline in civility and an emboldening of fringe elements in society.

In related news, the FBI reported a 4% increase in hate crimes from 2015 to 2016, with anti-Islamic crimes seeing the largest percentage rise. African Americans were targeted the most in racial hate crimes, followed by incidents motivated by bias against victims based on sexual orientation or gender identity.

The incident raised alarm in Jewish communities nationwide, prompting authorities to send extra patrols to synagogues and other houses of worship.

The FBI is leading the investigation, which is ongoing. The agency reported an overall increase in hate crimes in 2016, with anti-Semitic incidents accounting for 684 reported cases, more than any other type of hate crime. The ADL found a significant rise in anti-Semitic incidents last year, with a nearly 60% increase in 2017 compared to the previous year. The organization attributed this surge to a decline in civility and an emboldening of fringe elements in society.

In related news, the FBI reported a 4% increase in hate crimes from 2015 to 2016, with anti-Islamic crimes seeing the largest percentage rise. African Americans were targeted the most in racial hate crimes, followed by incidents motivated by bias against victims based on sexual orientation or gender identity.

reference: – The Anti-Defamation League says the shooting at the Tree of Life synagogue in Pittsburgh is thought to be the deadliest attack specifically targeting Jews ever in the US—by far. CNN reports the previous attack to have that label occurred in 1985, when Seattle lawyer Charles Goldmark, his wife, and two sons were killed. (The Washington Post notes it "was actually a case of mistaken religious identity," as the family was not Jewish.) The toll in Saturday's shooting stands at 11. The AP reports police on Sunday said the victims were eight men and three women. In an arrest affidavit made public, police say that while treating alleged shooter Robert Gregory Bowers for injuries he sustained after being shot by police, he said Jews were "committing genocide to his people" and that he wanted them all to die. The AP separately reports on a "vulnerability" the shooter took advantage of: an unlocked door. While the Tree of Life synagogue required guests to ring a bell to gain access during the week, on the Jewish Sabbath the building was open to all guests for Shabbat services. The Post quotes the FBI special agent in charge as saying it was "the most horrific crime scene I’ve seen in 22 years." Federal hate crime charges were filed against Bowers on Saturday: 29 charges in all, including 11 counts of using a firearm to commit murder. The Pittsburgh Post-Gazette reports Pittsburgh police also filed charges on Saturday: 11 counts of criminal homicide, six counts of attempted homicide, six counts of aggravated assault, and 13 counts of ethnic intimidation. (Read what President Trump had to say about the shooting here.)

final_summary:
Love bombing is a complex psychological manipulation tactic where an individual showers excessive attention and affection on another person, often with the intention of gaining control or exploiting them. This behavior is commonly exhibited by individuals with narcissistic tendencies, although it can also be observed in others who seek to dominate or manipulate others. 

The process of love bombing typically begins with an initial phase of intense flattery and admiration, which can create a false sense of security and attachment in the victim. However, this is often accompanied by a hidden agenda, where the perpetrator seeks to establish a sense of dependence or control over the victim. Children of narcissistic parents are particularly vulnerable to love bombing, as they have been conditioned to seek external validation and approval to compensate for the lack of emotional support in their childhood.

When love bombing is combined with intermittent reinforcement, a technique where the abuser provides occasional rewards and affection to keep the victim hooked and dependent, it can create a sense of uncertainty and longing. This can make the victim more susceptible to the abuser's demands, as they become increasingly reliant on the abuser for emotional validation.

Psychologist Dale Archer defines love bombing as a form of conditioning tool used to maintain power and control over the victim. He notes that this behavior is often characterized by an initial flood of words and affection, followed by a sudden shift to unreasonable, controlling, and manipulative behavior. This can include public displays of affection, such as posting photos on social media, only to later punish the victim for perceived transgressions.

Love bombing can lead to a range of negative consequences, including the development of Stockholm syndrome, where the victim develops a strong emotional bond with their abuser, often perceiving them as a savior or a source of comfort. To identify love bombing, experts recommend paying close attention to the consistency and sincerity of the abuser's behavior, as healthy relationships build gradually, with a focus on actions rather than words.

Victims of love bombing often experience a rapid escalation of emotions, including feelings of euphoria and attachment, which can be misinterpreted as a sign of a deep and meaningful connection. Individuals who have experienced love bombing in the past can develop immunity by seeking internal validation and surrounding themselves with people who provide genuine support and recognition. They should be cautious of over-the-top flattery and praise, recognizing that it may be a tactic to manipulate and control.

By developing a stronger sense of self-worth and learning to differentiate between connection and flattery, individuals can protect themselves from falling prey to love bombing and cultivate healthier, more fulfilling relationships. This requires a critical evaluation of the abuser's behavior, as well as a greater awareness of one's own needs and boundaries.

reference: – Love at first sight may actually be a warning sign. That's according to experts cautioning people in new relationships about so-called "love bombing." Not familiar with the term? Elle UK "delve[s] into the worrying behaviors of a 'love bomber,'" explaining that it takes time for people to truly get to know each other as their relationships deepen. When someone is over-the-top awesome in the beginning of a relationship—always sending flowers and offering up compliments and affection—it could actually be a "conditioning tool (otherwise known as a form of abuse)." PsychCentral calls it a "process of grooming." Constant flowers themselves aren't the worrisome sign—the shift is; ie, if the attention goes from positive and affectionate to controlling and cruel. If the person being "bombed" falls fast and hard it can be harder to see that shift. Love bombers, by the way, are narcissists, and they're usually men. In Psychology Today, psychiatrist Dale Archer dates the term "love bombing" to the 1970s, when Unification Church of the United States (aka the "Moonies") first used it. Cult leaders like David Koresh and Charles Manson would employ this tactic to woo followers and then con them into nefarious acts. Archer argues that idealization isn't necessarily harmful when it grows over time, but that the "common thread" in love bombing is intense courtship and idealization over just a matter of days or weeks. Soon, "love bombers exploit the natural human need for self worth, and turn it into shame, regret, and self-loathing." (One study finds that men tend to feel more entitled and be more narcissistic than women.)

final_summary:
Verizon's new "Share Everything" plan, set to launch on June 28, aims to simplify the way families manage their data usage by sharing a common pool of data across all devices on a single plan. However, upon closer inspection, it appears that this shift may not necessarily benefit individual customers, despite the touted convenience.

Under the new plan, families can indeed enjoy significant savings by pooling their data, but individuals who opt for the shared data plan will face increased costs due to the mandatory inclusion of unlimited voice and text messages, regardless of their actual usage. This means that customers who rely heavily on alternative communication methods, such as data-based apps, will be forced to pay for features they no longer need.

A closer analysis of the pricing reveals that customers who currently pay for limited voice and data plans will see their rates increase under the new plan. For instance, a customer who currently pays $80 per month for 450 voice minutes, 1,000 text messages, and 2GB of data will be upgraded to a $100 plan with unlimited voice and text messages, along with 2GB of data. Similarly, a couple who currently enjoys a share plan with 700 minutes, 1,000 text messages per phone, and 2GB of data each will have to give up their unlimited data plans in exchange for unlimited voice and text messages, resulting in a $150 plan.

The introduction of access fees for devices adds to the complexity, with prices ranging from $10 for tablets to $40 for smartphones. This makes it challenging for individuals who want to sign up multiple devices under one plan, especially since the lowest rate – $10 for a tablet – seems excessive.

Analysts suggest that Verizon's primary goal is to concentrate family plans around a single provider, making it difficult for family members to switch to a different carrier. This strategy could ultimately lead to AT&T offering a similar plan, potentially harming individuals on that carrier.

While the shared data plans may appeal to large families who want multiple devices with data, individuals who don't use their phones extensively for voice calls will see their plan rates increase significantly. For example, a customer who currently pays $69.99 for 450 minutes and 2GB of data will have to pay $90 for just 1GB of data under the new plan.

Critics argue that wireless carriers should offer compelling data-only packages with per-minute voice options, as data usage has surpassed voice usage on smartphones. However, the new shared plans prioritize unlimited voice and text messages, forcing customers to pay for features they may not need.

Some customers, like those who value Verizon's excellent coverage, may consider switching to the carrier due to the potential savings offered by the shared data plans. Others, like Sprint customers, may find themselves attracted to the carrier's unlimited everything plans for $80 per smartphone once its LTE network is fully operational.

reference: – Verizon yesterday unveiled its new "Share Everything" plans, in which families will share one data allowance on a family plan rather than each member having his or her own data allowance on separate plans. Despite the fact that Verizon is touting this as a money-saving opportunity for families, critics run the numbers and say it's actually not: Dan Rowinski of ReadWriteWeb finds that one hypothetical family of four will likely pay exactly the same amount per month, or may save at most $10 per month. The problem? Data may be cheaper under the new plans, but "you pay more per device," he explains. "Verizon's new plan does not give families a discount. The difference amounts to an administrative change." Troy Wolverton goes a bit further in the Mercury News, calling the new plans "a crummy deal for many consumers." He finds that many users will actually end up paying more, since the only real price breaks are on talk minutes and text messages—"services [consumers] are already using less." "Overall, the move appears to be a price hike tarted up to look consumer friendly," he writes. All the critics concede that the new plans are certainly simpler to understand, but on ZDNet, Matthew Miller is "disappointed that convenience comes at such a high price." If Sprint gets its LTE network running, it will be an "attractive" option for individual unlimited plans at $80 per phone, he notes. On CNET, Roger Cheng points out that Verizon is now "forcing" unlimited voice and text messages with these plans. "Under the change, customers will have to give up their old grandfathered unlimited data plans right as people use more data than ever," he writes. "It's a classic case of a carrier giving you more of what you don't need, and taking away what you do—all for a higher price." The new plans roll out on June 28, and the only way to keep your existing plan is to upgrade to new phones at full cost, with no discount.

final_summary:
Maria Rose Belding, a 20-year-old American University sophomore, is the co-founder of MEANS Database, a revolutionary online platform that connects food donors with food banks and pantries in need. Born out of her own frustration with food waste while volunteering at a local soup pantry in Iowa, Belding's initiative has grown exponentially since its inception, saving approximately 4,000 pounds of food in its first year and expanding to over 200 donor and recipient accounts across 27 states. However, the issue of food waste remains a pressing concern in the US, with an estimated 40% of the country's food being discarded, despite 48 million Americans experiencing food insecurity.

Belding's innovative approach addresses this paradox by facilitating communication between food providers and those in need, reducing the likelihood of surplus food going to waste. Unlike larger organizations like City Harvest, which operate at a larger scale and handle logistics themselves, MEANS empowers small organizations and individuals to participate in food recovery efforts. According to experts, including Mathy Stanislaus from the Environmental Protection Agency, initiatives like MEANS play a significant role in addressing food waste and promoting sustainability.

Belding's journey began as a high school freshman, when she witnessed the struggle of a local soup pantry to distribute donated food due to lack of communication. Initially, she attempted to build a website to address this issue but lacked technical expertise. Meeting Grant Nelson, a self-taught programmer and law student, marked a turning point in the development of MEAS. Together, they refined the concept, leveraging data science and mathematics to optimize the platform's functionality.

Since its launch, MEANS has gained recognition, including a $10,000 prize from L'Oréal Paris's Women of Worth campaign and a $42,500 award from the GW New Venture Competition. The organization now employs 11 staff members, 10 interns, and volunteers, with plans to introduce a system enabling companies to claim tax credits for their donations, generating revenue for MEANS. Belding aims to expand the platform, create a public directory of food pantries, and tackle the issue of incorrect contact information among food pantries.

Food production contributes significantly to the national energy budget, utilizes substantial amounts of water, and generates greenhouse gases, highlighting the importance of addressing food waste. By bridging the gap between food providers and those in need, MEANS has the potential to make a meaningful impact in reducing food waste and alleviating hunger in the US. With a growing network of over 200 donor and recipient accounts, MEANS is poised to continue its mission of reducing food waste and improving access to nutritious food for those in need.

history.draft:
Maria Rose Belding, a 20-year-old American University sophomore, is the co-founder of MEANS Database, a revolutionary online platform that connects food donors with food banks and pantries in need. Born out of her own frustration with food waste while volunteering at a local soup pantry in Iowa, Belding's initiative has grown exponentially since its inception, saving approximately 4,000 pounds of food in its first year and expanding to over 200 donor and recipient accounts across 27 states. However, the issue of food waste remains a pressing concern in the US, with an estimated 40% of the country's food being discarded, despite 48 million Americans experiencing food insecurity.

Belding's innovative approach addresses this paradox by facilitating communication between food providers and those in need, reducing the likelihood of surplus food going to waste. Unlike larger organizations like City Harvest, which operate at a larger scale and handle logistics themselves, MEANS empowers small organizations and individuals to participate in food recovery efforts. According to experts, including Mathy Stanislaus from the Environmental Protection Agency, initiatives like MEANS play a significant role in addressing food waste and promoting sustainability.

Belding's journey began as a high school freshman, when she witnessed the struggle of a local soup pantry to distribute donated food due to lack of communication. Initially, she attempted to build a website to address this issue but lacked technical expertise. Meeting Grant Nelson, a self-taught programmer and law student, marked a turning point in the development of MEAS. Together, they refined the concept, leveraging data science and mathematics to optimize the platform's functionality.

Since its launch, MEANS has gained recognition, including a $10,000 prize from L'Oréal Paris's Women of Worth campaign and a $42,500 award from the GW New Venture Competition. The organization now employs 11 staff members, 10 interns, and volunteers, with plans to introduce a system enabling companies to claim tax credits for their donations, generating revenue for MEANS. Belding aims to expand the platform, create a public directory of food pantries, and tackle the issue of incorrect contact information among food pantries.

Food production contributes significantly to the national energy budget, utilizes substantial amounts of water, and generates greenhouse gases, highlighting the importance of addressing food waste. By bridging the gap between food providers and those in need, MEANS has the potential to make a meaningful impact in reducing food waste and alleviating hunger in the US. With a growing network of over 200 donor and recipient accounts, MEANS is poised to continue its mission of reducing food waste and improving access to nutritious food for those in need.

reference: – There are 48 million Americans who, at some point each year, may be unable to get enough food. Meanwhile, a third of the country's food goes to waste. This isn't as easy an issue to solve as it sounds—something Maria Rose Belding realized while volunteering at a soup pantry as a teen. At one point, the pantry received 10,000 boxes of mac 'n cheese, but couldn't find enough people in need before the stuff expired, reports Quartz. "We were throwing away all of this food just because we couldn't communicate," Belding tells the Washington Post. Now a 20-year-old American University student, Belding hopes the app and website she co-created can help: Overflowing pantries list what extra food they have available on the MEANS (Matching Excess and Need for Stability) Database, and neighboring pantries get notifications, Belding writes at the Huffington Post. Grocery stores and restaurants with food to unload can make use of the site, too. Belding remembers watching the first donation of canned beans pop up after MEANS launched in February. "We were all anxiously sitting by our computers hitting refresh," she says—the anxiety intensified by the fact that beans are donated so regularly they aren't a hot commodity. Suddenly, "it just disappeared." Since then, the database has kept 4,000 pounds of food from the trash. While a similar service saved 50 million pounds of food in New York in 2015 by matching donations with food kitchens, MEANS allows smaller organizations to do the ground work themselves, and currently has 200 accounts across 27 states. While grants and awards keep the site running for now, Belding has plans: to track large retailers' donations from a tax-benefit perspective, something most don't currently do since those donations are made on such a local level. MEANS would charge a small fee for doing all the tracking. (On the food front, this young chef has left one of the world's best restaurants to try to save America's school lunch program.)

final_summary:
On July 6, Asiana Airlines Flight 214 crashed at San Francisco International Airport, resulting in significant damage and injuries. In the aftermath of the accident, local station KTVU announced the names of the flight's pilots on live television, claiming they had obtained the information from the National Transportation Safety Board (NTSB), although the NTSB had not released the names. The names provided - Ho Lee Fuk, Sum Ting Wong, Wi Tu Lo, and Bang Ding Ow - were entirely fictional and caused offense due to their resemblance to ethnic stereotypes.

KTVU initially stated that it had verified the names with an NTSB official, but later apologized for the error, citing that the names were not read aloud before being broadcast and that the station had not confirmed the individuals' positions within the organization. The NTSB subsequently released a statement acknowledging that a summer intern had acted outside their authority in providing the false information to KTVU.

The NTSB emphasizes that it typically does not release or confirm the names of crew members or individuals involved in transportation accidents to the media, and expresses deep regret for the incident. This incident highlights the importance of verifying information before broadcasting it to the public. Asiana Airlines responded to the incident, stating that the reputations of the pilots and the company had been severely damaged by the report and considering taking legal action against KTVU and the NTSB. The Asian American Journalists Association condemned the use of the fictional names, criticizing their insensitive nature.

In the days following the incident, KTVU issued multiple apologies, including one on its noon and 6 pm newscasts, explaining that the names were not read aloud before being broadcast and that the station had not properly verified the information. The NTSB took steps to address the issue, promising to review its procedures to prevent similar errors in the future.

Asiana Airlines responded to the incident, stating that the reputations of the pilots and the company had been severely damaged by the report and considering taking legal action against KTVU and the NTSB. The Asian American Journalists Association condemned the use of the derogatory names, calling them "grossly offensive."

In the days following the incident, KTVU issued multiple apologies, including one on its noon and 6 pm newscasts, explaining that the names were not read aloud before being broadcast and that the station had not properly verified the information. The NTSB took steps to address the issue, promising to review its procedures to prevent similar errors in the future.

reference: – There's not fact checking, and then there's ... not even using your brain. A newscast on Bay Area Fox affiliate KTVU apparently fell for a (racist and offensive) joke, incorrectly reporting that the pilots of the plane that crashed at San Francisco airport were named "Sum Ting Wong," "Ho Lee Fuk," "Bang Ding Ow," and "Wi Tu Lo," SF Weekly reports. The network later apologized, but said an NTSB official in Washington had "confirmed" them. It wasn't exactly an official. Per the NTSB: "Earlier today, in response to an inquiry from a media outlet, a summer intern acted outside the scope of his authority when he erroneously confirmed the names of the flight crew on the aircraft." The kicker, as Mediabistro notes, is that KTVU had just been boasting about its "100% accurate" coverage of the disaster earlier this week.

final_summary:
Anthony Scaramucci, the newly appointed White House Communications Director, has faced controversy surrounding his financial ties to his investment firm, SkyBridge Capital. According to his financial disclosure form, filed with the Office of Government Ethics, Scaramucci earned $4.9 million from his ownership stake in SkyBridge in the period between January 1, 2016, and June 30, 2017, in addition to his $5 million in salary during the same time frame. This disclosure highlights the significant wealth Scaramucci has accumulated throughout his career, similar to many of President Trump's top advisors and cabinet members.

Scaramucci's financial filing values SkyBridge Capital at over $50 million, with him owning nearly 44% of the firm. Upon completion of the company's sale, expected in the third quarter of this year, Scaramucci will receive the agreed-upon purchase price for his share of the sale proceeds. Bloomberg valued the SkyBridge deal at between $200 million and $230 million when it was announced in January. Despite stepping down as managing director of SkyBridge in January, Scaramucci remains an employee of the firm, collecting a salary, while awaiting the sale's completion. His continued involvement with SkyBridge has raised concerns about potential conflicts of interest, particularly given the scrutiny of business dealings with China, as the sale involves a Chinese conglomerate, HNA Group. The Committee on Foreign Investment in the United States (CFIUS) is reviewing the deal to ensure it poses no risk to national security.

Scaramucci's financial disclosure also reveals his extensive personal wealth, including assets worth up to $85 million, real estate investments, a minority stake in the New York Mets, and investments in various companies such as Juice Press. Additionally, he has taken out multiple mortgages totaling $2.5 million to $7 million with U.S. Bank. On July 19, just five days after being named White House Communications Director, Scaramucci tweeted that he would contact the FBI and the Justice Department regarding the leak of his financial disclosure information, labeling it a felony. He tagged White House Chief of Staff Reince Priebus in the tweet, suggesting Priebus may be involved in the leak. Scaramucci had initially deleted the tweet before posting a follow-up message stating that Priebus was assisting in efforts to identify the leaker.

reference: – New White House communications director Anthony Scaramucci was not happy about what he believed was a leak of information from his financial disclosure Wednesday—though it later emerged that the form was available to the public. "In light of the leak of my financial disclosure info which is a felony. I will be contacting @FBI and @TheJusticeDept #swamp @Reince45," Scaramucci said in a now-deleted tweet, per the Daily Beast. Scaramucci later denied suggestions that his tagging of Reince Priebus was a declaration of war on the White House chief of staff. "Wrong! Tweet was public notice to leakers that all Sr Adm officials are helping to end illegal leaks," Scaramucci said in a second tweet. The disclosure information believed to have enraged Scaramucci appeared in a Politico report late Wednesday. According to the report, Scaramucci earned $4.9 million from his ownership stake in the SkyBridge Capital plus more than $5 million in salary from Jan. 1, 2016, to the end of June this year, when he began a short-lived stint at the federal Export-Import Bank. The New York Times notes that Scaramucci filed the disclosure form June 23 and under federal law, it would have been available to the public 30 days later. "They aren't in process yet," Scaramucci wrote to the Times after being asked why he thought the report had been leaked. He didn't respond after being told it would have been available July 23.

final_summary:
Tensions surrounding the Kurdish region of Iraq's bid for independence reached a boiling point in the months leading up to the September 25, 2017, referendum. Proponents of the vote, such as Masoud Barzani, the former President of the Kurdistan Region, expressed enthusiasm for the referendum, emphasizing its importance in determining the future of the Kurdish people. Similarly, Nawshirwan Mustafa stated on his Facebook page that the referendum offered a chance for the people of Kurdistan to express their desire for freedom and self-determination.

Notwithstanding these sentiments, several key figures raised concerns about the timing of the vote. Qubad Talabani, Jalal Talabani's son, suggested that it might be wiser to hold the referendum after the defeat of ISIS, while the Patriotic Union of Kurdistan (PUK) party, led by Bafel Talabani, advocated for a delay due to security concerns and the need for further negotiations with Baghdad. 

The Kurdistan Democratic Party (KDP), however, maintained its commitment to holding the referendum as scheduled. According to a statement published on their website, they believed the referendum was essential for determining the future of the Kurdish people and establishing a clear stance for negotiations with Baghdad. Although they acknowledged that the referendum would not be binding, they viewed it as a crucial step toward achieving independence.

International organizations also weighed in on the matter. On September 20, 2017, the United Nations Assistance Mission for Iraq (UNAMI) urged the Kurdistan Regional Government (KRG) to reconsider the referendum, citing concerns about its potential impact on regional stability and the ongoing fight against terrorism. However, the KRG chose to proceed with the vote, with Prime Minister Nechirvan Barzani affirming that the referendum would indeed take place as planned.

In the run-up to the referendum, clashes erupted between Peshmerga forces and Turkish-backed militias along the border with Turkey. The Turkish government had long been opposed to the idea of an independent Kurdistan and had threatened military intervention if the referendum proceeded. Despite these tensions, the KRG pushed forward with preparations for the vote, which ultimately occurred on September 25, 2017.

The outcome of the referendum revealed widespread support for independence, with over 92% of voters backing secession from Iraq. Nevertheless, the international community swiftly condemned the vote, with the UN Security Council calling for dialogue and negotiation between Erbil and Baghdad. The US, UK, and EU also expressed concern about the potential repercussions of the referendum, whereas Turkey and Iran outright denounced the vote.

Following the referendum, the KRG faced intense pressure from neighboring countries, particularly Turkey and Iran, which perceived the vote as a threat to their territorial integrity. In response, the Iraqi government announced plans to seize control of Kurdish airports and borders. As the situation continued to worsen, the KRG was compelled to suspend flights at Erbil International Airport and close its borders with Turkey and Iran.

history.draft:
As the Kurdish region of Iraq prepared for its historic referendum on independence, tensions ran high among the various factions vying for power. On September 12, 2017, Masoud Barzani, the former President of the Kurdistan Region, tweeted "The people of Kurdistan will decide their future through a free and fair referendum on September 25th." This statement was echoed by other pro-independence leaders, including Nawshirwan Mustafa, who wrote on his Facebook page that "the referendum is a chance for the people of Kurdistan to express their desire for freedom and self-determination."

However, not all voices were in favor of the referendum. Jalal Talabani's son, Qubad Talabani, expressed concerns about the timing of the vote, stating on Twitter that it would be better to hold the referendum after the defeat of ISIS. Similarly, the Patriotic Union of Kurdistan (PUK) party, led by Bafel Talabani, called for a delay in the referendum, citing security concerns and the need for more time to negotiate with Baghdad.

Meanwhile, the Kurdistan Democratic Party (KDP), led by Masoud Barzani, remained committed to holding the referendum as scheduled. In a statement published on their website, the KDP argued that the referendum was necessary to determine the future of the Kurdish people and to establish a clear position for negotiations with Baghdad. They also emphasized that the referendum would not be binding, but rather a step towards achieving independence.

On September 20, 2017, the United Nations Assistance Mission for Iraq (UNAMI) issued a statement urging the Kurdistan Regional Government (KRG) to postpone the referendum, citing concerns about the potential impact on regional stability and the ongoing fight against terrorism. However, the KRG rejected this call, with Prime Minister Nechirvan Barzani stating that the referendum would go ahead as planned.

In the days leading up to the referendum, there were reports of clashes between Peshmerga forces and Turkish-backed militias along the border with Turkey. The Turkish government had long been opposed to the idea of an independent Kurdistan, and had threatened military action if the referendum went ahead. Despite these tensions, the KRG pressed ahead with preparations for the vote, which ultimately took place on September 25, 2017.

The results of the referendum showed overwhelming support for independence, with over 92% of voters supporting secession from Iraq. However, the international community was quick to condemn the vote, with the UN Security Council issuing a statement calling for dialogue and negotiation between Erbil and Baghdad. The US, UK, and EU also issued statements expressing concern about the potential consequences of the referendum, while Turkey and Iran condemned the vote outright.

In the aftermath of the referendum, the KRG faced significant pressure from neighboring countries, particularly Turkey and Iran, which saw the vote as a threat to their own territorial integrity. The Iraqi government also responded strongly, with Prime Minister Haider al-Abadi announcing plans to take control of Kurdish airports and borders. As the situation continued to deteriorate, the KRG was forced to suspend flights at Erbil International Airport and close its borders with Turkey and Iran.

Tensions surrounding the Kurdish region of Iraq's bid for independence reached a boiling point in the months leading up to the September 25, 2017, referendum. Proponents of the vote, such as Masoud Barzani, the former President of the Kurdistan Region, expressed enthusiasm for the referendum, emphasizing its importance in determining the future of the Kurdish people. Similarly, Nawshirwan Mustafa stated on his Facebook page that the referendum offered a chance for the people of Kurdistan to express their desire for freedom and self-determination.

Notwithstanding these sentiments, several key figures raised concerns about the timing of the vote. Qubad Talabani, Jalal Talabani's son, suggested that it might be wiser to hold the referendum after the defeat of ISIS, while the Patriotic Union of Kurdistan (PUK) party, led by Bafel Talabani, advocated for a delay due to security concerns and the need for further negotiations with Baghdad. 

The Kurdistan Democratic Party (KDP), however, maintained its commitment to holding the referendum as scheduled. According to a statement published on their website, they believed the referendum was essential for determining the future of the Kurdish people and establishing a clear stance for negotiations with Baghdad. Although they acknowledged that the referendum would not be binding, they viewed it as a crucial step toward achieving independence.

International organizations also weighed in on the matter. On September 20, 2017, the United Nations Assistance Mission for Iraq (UNAMI) urged the Kurdistan Regional Government (KRG) to reconsider the referendum, citing concerns about its potential impact on regional stability and the ongoing fight against terrorism. However, the KRG chose to proceed with the vote, with Prime Minister Nechirvan Barzani affirming that the referendum would indeed take place as planned.

In the run-up to the referendum, clashes erupted between Peshmerga forces and Turkish-backed militias along the border with Turkey. The Turkish government had long been opposed to the idea of an independent Kurdistan and had threatened military intervention if the referendum proceeded. Despite these tensions, the KRG pushed forward with preparations for the vote, which ultimately occurred on September 25, 2017.

The outcome of the referendum revealed widespread support for independence, with over 92% of voters backing secession from Iraq. Nevertheless, the international community swiftly condemned the vote, with the UN Security Council calling for dialogue and negotiation between Erbil and Baghdad. The US, UK, and EU also expressed concern about the potential repercussions of the referendum, whereas Turkey and Iran outright denounced the vote.

Following the referendum, the KRG faced intense pressure from neighboring countries, particularly Turkey and Iran, which perceived the vote as a threat to their territorial integrity. In response, the Iraqi government announced plans to seize control of Kurdish airports and borders. As the situation continued to worsen, the KRG was compelled to suspend flights at Erbil International Airport and close its borders with Turkey and Iran.

reference: – This year’s Indy 500 found its instant replay moment, and it looks like it could have come straight from a Michael Bay film. Driver Scott Dixon walked away Sunday from the nightmare crash captured in this video. (Replay starts about 47 seconds in.) It also can be seen in this tweeted video. The LA Times reports that the accident began when driver Jay Howard lost control of his vehicle a little more than a quarter into the 200-lap race. He collided with Dixon, whose car was sent flying through the air before smashing into an inner retaining wall. Though Dixon’s vehicle was demolished, both drivers survived the fiery accident and were released after a check-up at the track's medical center. Afterward, Dixon said he was “a little bit beaten up” and “glad everybody was OK," calling the incident "definitely a wild ride.” The race continued on, with Takuma Sato later becoming the first Japanese driver ever to finish first, reports the AP. Sato held off Helio Castroneves, who was going for his fourth Indy win.

final_summary:
During the Macy's Thanksgiving Day Parade in New York City, spectators witnessed something unexpected amidst the festive atmosphere - confetti containing confidential personal information, including social security numbers and banking details of police employees, some of whom are undercover officers. According to eyewitnesses, the sensitive information was found on confetti strips scattered throughout the viewing area. Ethan Finkelstein, a Tufts University student on Thanksgiving break, stumbled upon the confetti strips stuck to his friend's coat and nearby surfaces while watching the parade. Upon closer inspection, the confetti strips revealed a range of documents, including arrest records, incident reports, and details about Mitt Romney's motorcade from the final presidential debate at Hofstra University in Nassau County last month.

Investigation revealed that the confetti strips originated from shredded documents belonging to the Nassau County Police Department. It appears that the documents were not properly disposed of, resulting in the exposure of sensitive information such as names, social security numbers, dates of birth, and other personal details. The Nassau County Police Department has initiated an internal investigation to determine how this occurred and is reviewing its protocols for handling sensitive documents.

Meanwhile, federal authorities have been working on reassembling documents shredded by the Stanford Financial Group, which is linked to a $7 billion Ponzi scheme operated by R. Allen Stanford. To accomplish this task, technicians utilize advanced software that scans the shredded fragments, assigns unique identifiers, and examines characteristics such as size, color, and font. This process enables operators to identify potential adjacent fragments and reassemble the documents.

Different types of shredders exist, including strip shredders, cross-cut shredders, and those that reduce paper to dust. Strip shredders generate larger fragments, making them more susceptible to reassembly, whereas cross-cut shredders produce smaller, more secure pieces. For top-secret information, the Department of Defense mandates that shredded particles not exceed 5 square millimeters in size. Historically, documents were reconstructed manually, often requiring considerable time and effort, as exemplified by instances such as the 1979 Iranian Revolution and the reassembly of a shredded dictionary on Good Morning America in 2002.

final_summary:
In a move that may seem like a step out of the spotlight, Steve Jobs' resignation as Chief Executive Officer (CEO) of Apple Inc. on August 24, 2011, could potentially have little impact on the company's operations. As CEO, Jobs was accountable to the board of directors and shareholders, but he was also a member of the board himself. Following his resignation, Jobs took on the role of chairman of the board, succeeding co-lead directors Andrea Jung and Arthur D. Levinson, who had previously shared the position. This shift in leadership came after Jobs had stepped down from day-to-day operations in January, handing over responsibility to Tim Cook, the former Chief Operating Officer (COO), who was subsequently elected as the new CEO by the board.

Although Jobs was on a medical leave of absence, he remained involved in key decision-making processes throughout the year, including the unveiling of the iPad 2 in March. His exact medical condition has not been publicly disclosed. The question of whether Jobs or Cook will unveil the upcoming iPhone 5 or iPad 3 remains uncertain. With Cook's promotion to CEO, he also joined the board of directors, increasing the total number of members from seven to eight. The existing directors include Jung, Levinson, William V. Campbell, Millard S. Drexler, Al Gore, and Ronald D. Sugar.

The timing of Jobs' resignation has sparked speculation, with some attributing it to health concerns, while others believe it was a strategic move to ensure a smooth transition. Apple's recent financial performance has been impressive, with record quarterly earnings reported despite a challenging period for competitors like Dell and HP. The company is poised to release new products, including the iPhone 5 and potentially an affordable iPhone model, further solidifying its position in the market.

However, the departure of Jobs marks a significant turning point for Apple, marking the end of an era and the beginning of a new chapter under Cook's leadership. While Jobs will continue to serve as chairman of the board, his reduced involvement raises questions about the company's future direction and innovation. Apple's success has been built on multiple fronts, including its ability to produce innovative products, control its supply chain, and engage in strategic partnerships. As Apple moves forward, it faces challenges in areas such as cloud computing, innovation, and competing with lower-cost alternatives. The company's focus on marketing and strategic partnerships may hinder its ability to innovate and adapt to changing market conditions. Ultimately, Apple's future depends on its ability to find a new path forward, a concept embodied by Jobs' constant introduction of new products and technologies.

reference: – Steve Jobs revolutionized Apple in his second go-round with the company, taking the stock from a split-adjusted $6.05 per share in 1996 to $376 today. But with Jobs resigning as CEO, everyone is wondering what the future holds for Apple. Some reactions: What will change? Not much, write Nathan Olivarez-Giles and Thomas Suh Lauder for the LA Times. In his role as CEO, Jobs had to answer to two groups: shareholders, and the board of directors. Now he's at the helm of that board. It was the perfect time to go, writes Brian Barrett for Gizmodo, noting that the coming release of the iPhone 5 and more affordable iPhone 4 should buoy the company and give CEO Tim Cook a chance to step into the spotlight. "If they'd waited any longer, the iPhone 5 announcement would've been fully shrouded in memories of Jobs. ... This feels calculated, in the best possible way, to happen at the best possible time. Now." Apple has lost its core, writes Rupert Goodwins for ZDNet, and only time will tell how this plays out. The iPhone and iPad can only be updated so many times, and with Jobs "there was always a 'next.'" But "Apple as innovator without Jobs is Apple unknown." Its future "depends on the new next, and the longer we have to wait to see what happens, the less likely it will happen at all."

final_summary:
Robert Mueller's appointment as special counsel to investigate alleged ties between Russia and the Trump campaign marked a pivotal moment in the ongoing saga. One year on, the investigation remains shrouded in secrecy, despite yielding significant developments. Mueller, a decorated war veteran and former FBI director, was tasked with probing any links and/or coordination between the Russian government and individuals associated with President Donald Trump's campaign following Trump's decision to fire FBI Director James Comey, who was leading the initial inquiry. 

In the past year, Mueller's team has secured five guilty pleas and multiple indictments, including those of former Trump campaign officials Paul Manafort and Rick Gates, and former national security adviser Michael Flynn. Additionally, Mueller indicted 13 Russian nationals and three Russian entities on charges related to the 2016 US presidential election. These developments demonstrate the investigation's progress, despite facing intense scrutiny and criticism from Trump and his allies. Many experts argue that the president cannot claim immunity from a subpoena or indictment, citing the Supreme Court's ruling in the case of President Bill Clinton, which allowed a civil suit to proceed against a sitting president. Furthermore, the court's unanimous decision in the Watergate-related case United States v. Nixon suggests that presidents are not exempt from subpoenas in a criminal investigation.

Regarding the possibility of Mueller indicting Trump, there is no clear-cut answer, as there has never been a binding judicial opinion on this matter. However, many experts believe that courts would rule against the idea of prosecuting a sitting president, citing the Office of Legal Counsel's opinions from 1973 and 2000, which concluded that a president cannot be criminally prosecuted due to concerns about undermining the executive branch's ability to function. Trump's potential removal of Mueller is also a complex issue, with various scenarios possible. According to Justice Department regulations, only the attorney general can fire Mueller, currently Acting AG Rod Rosenstein, who was appointed by Trump. However, Rosenstein could refuse to fire Mueller if he believes there has been no misconduct. Alternatively, Trump could fire Rosenstein and continue replacing Justice Department officials until someone agrees to terminate Mueller. Another possibility is that Trump could attempt to rescind the Justice Department regulations governing Mueller's appointment, although this would require a lengthy process allowing Mueller to continue working in the interim.

Moreover, Trump's use of pardons to evade the investigation is also uncertain. While the Constitution does not explicitly prohibit a self-pardon, experts argue that there are compelling reasons why a self-pardon would be impermissible, including the concept of mercy and the principle that no one should be a judge in their own cause. Pardoning presidential associates could potentially hinder the investigation, but it would not provide a complete solution, as the probe focuses on Russian interference in the election, not individual wrongdoing. To effectively shut down the investigation, Trump would need to issue a blanket amnesty, which has never been used to protect a presidency.

In the past year, Mueller's team has secured five guilty pleas and multiple indictments, including those of former Trump campaign officials Paul Manafort and Rick Gates, and former national security adviser Michael Flynn. Additionally, Mueller indicted 13 Russian nationals and three Russian entities on charges related to the 2016 US presidential election. These developments demonstrate the investigation's progress, despite facing intense scrutiny and criticism from Trump and his allies. Many experts argue that the president cannot claim immunity from a subpoena or indictment, citing the Supreme Court's ruling in the case of President Bill Clinton, which allowed a civil suit to proceed against a sitting president. Furthermore, the court's unanimous decision in the Watergate-related case United States v. Nixon suggests that presidents are not exempt from subpoenas in a criminal investigation.

Regarding the possibility of Mueller indicting Trump, there is no clear-cut answer, as there has never been a binding judicial opinion on this matter. However, many experts believe that courts would rule against the idea of prosecuting a sitting president, citing the Office of Legal Counsel's opinions from 1973 and 2000, which concluded that a president cannot be criminally prosecuted due to concerns about undermining the executive branch's ability to function. Trump's potential removal of Mueller is also a complex issue, with various scenarios possible. According to Justice Department regulations, only the attorney general can fire Mueller, currently Acting AG Rod Rosenstein, who was appointed by Trump. However, Rosenstein could refuse to fire Mueller if he believes there has been no misconduct. Alternatively, Trump could fire Rosenstein and continue replacing Justice Department officials until someone agrees to terminate Mueller. Another possibility is that Trump could attempt to rescind the Justice Department regulations governing Mueller's appointment, although this would require a lengthy process allowing Mueller to continue working in the interim.

Moreover, Trump's use of pardons to evade the investigation is also uncertain. While the Constitution does not explicitly prohibit a self-pardon, experts argue that there are compelling reasons why a self-pardon would be impermissible, including the concept of mercy and the principle that no one should be a judge in their own cause. Pardoning presidential associates could potentially hinder the investigation, but it would not provide a complete solution, as the probe focuses on Russian interference in the election, not individual wrongdoing. To effectively shut down the investigation, Trump would need to issue a blanket amnesty, which has never been used to protect a presidency.

reference: – Thursday is the one-year anniversary of the start of Robert Mueller's investigation, and President Trump is among those marking the occasion. His tweet, however, drips with sarcasm. "Congratulations America, we are now into the second year of the greatest Witch Hunt in American History ... and there is still No Collusion and No Obstruction," he writes. "The only Collusion was that done by Democrats who were unable to win an Election despite the spending of far more money!" In another tweet, he calls the investigation "disgusting, illegal and unwarranted" and said his administration has nonetheless had "the most successful" first 17 months of any White House, "by far!" Related coverage: The basics: Mueller was appointed special counsel to investigate "any links and/or coordination between the Russian government and individuals associated with the campaign of President Donald Trump," explains PolitiFact. Mueller quickly expanded his investigation into whether Trump obstructed justice, notably by firing FBI chief James Comey. The piece hits other nuts and bolts of the investigation.

final_summary:
After nearly four decades of armed struggle for independence, the National Liberation Front of Corsica (FLNC) has announced its decision to abandon its armed resistance and transition into conventional politics. The move marks a significant shift for the group, which has been waging a low-intensity military campaign against French targets on the island since 1976. 

Located in the Tyrrhenian Sea, part of the Mediterranean, between the coasts of France and Italy, Corsica has experienced a tumultuous history marked by terrorism, including bombings, robberies, and assaults. These violent acts have resulted in numerous attacks carried out by the FLNC over the years. Notable incidents include the 1997 coordinated bombings of multiple banks and government offices, as well as the assassination of a top French official in Ajaccio in 1998, prompting a severe crackdown by the French government. Despite a 2003 announcement by a branch of the FLNC to suspend military actions, attacks continued, albeit at a reduced frequency.

The decision to disarm and engage in conventional politics comes after several recent developments, including the approval of a bill restricting land and property purchases to Corsican residents, aimed at addressing speculation-driven real estate price inflation on the island. This measure, along with others, has contributed to a sense of progress toward a resolution. The FLNC views these advancements as positive steps toward a resolution, signaling a shift from a period of conflict to a focus on building a true Corsican political presence.

This development follows a similar path taken by ETA, the Basque separatist organization in Spain, which recently announced plans to decommission some of its arsenal and explosives. The FLNC's decision is a pivotal moment in the history of the Corsican separatist movement, which has maintained a strong following among many of the island's residents. With over 100 members, the group aims to continue its efforts through peaceful means, marking a new chapter in the complex relationship between Corsica and France.

The FLNC's decision is seen as a significant turning point in the history of the Corsican separatist movement, which has enjoyed support among many of the island's 320,000 residents. With over 100 members, the group aims to continue its fight through peaceful means, marking a new chapter in the complex relationship between Corsica and France.
