reference: – There might be a reason that college sexual assaults make the news more often these days. A new Department of Education report has found that reports of "forcible" campus sexual assaults shot up 50% between 2001 and 2011, jumping from 2,200 to 3,300, Time reports. At the same time, every other category of college crime decreased. The numbers mean that 2.2 out of every 10,000 college students in 2011 reported an assault, Business Insider points out. Still, the spike doesn't necessarily mean that more assaults are occurring; it might just mean that victims have become more comfortable coming forward. "It's a good thing that more victims are reporting," one advocate told NPR in response to a similar finding in April. "For far too long, they've been left on their own. And now they're getting the help they need."

final_summary:
A bill signed into law by Oklahoma Governor Mary Fallin bars cities and counties from setting their own minimum wage standards, effectively blocking a proposed initiative in Oklahoma City to raise the minimum wage to $10.10 per hour. Critics argue that the legislation specifically targets Oklahoma City, where a grassroots effort was underway to align with President Barack Obama's national push for a $10.10 hourly wage. 

Organizers in Oklahoma City were collecting signatures for a petition to put the issue to a city-wide vote, which would have allowed residents to decide whether to increase the minimum wage above the federal requirement of $7.25 per hour. However, the bill's passage has dealt a significant blow to this effort. David Slane, the lawyer behind the petition, expressed disappointment over the state legislature's decision, stating that it undermines the people's right to decide on minimum wage. Despite the setback, Slane vowed to continue the campaign, aiming to educate the public about the importance of raising the minimum wage.

Governor Fallin's office released a statement defending the bill, citing a Congressional Budget Office report that suggested raising the minimum wage nationwide could result in a loss of half-a-million jobs. However, experts dispute this claim, pointing out that nearly 90% of workers affected by a federal minimum wage hike are over 20 years old, with an average age of 35, and more than a quarter have children to support, working full-time. This challenges Fallin's assertion that most minimum-wage workers are young, single individuals working part-time or entry-level jobs.

Supporters of the bill argue that it will aid local businesses, claiming that an artificially raised minimum wage could harm local economies and lead to job losses. State Representative Randy Grau and Senator Dan Newberry, co-authors of the bill, contend that it creates a safer business environment, allowing Oklahoma to remain economically competitive and foster job growth. However, opponents, including State Representative Eric Proctor, believe that the current minimum wage is "immoral" as it leaves individuals working full-time below the poverty line.

The passage of the bill has sparked debate about the effectiveness of raising the minimum wage in combating poverty, with some arguing that it would disproportionately affect small business owners and consumers. As the issue remains contentious, proponents of the Oklahoma City initiative are exploring alternative avenues, including a potential constitutional challenge to the new law and possibly pursuing a statewide initiative petition.

reference: – An initiative to raise the minimum wage in Oklahoma City has been thwarted by a new law banning cities in the state from setting their own minimum wages or requirements for vacation days. A petition being circulated in the city called for raising the local minimum wage to $10.10—the level President Obama is pushing—from the current federal requirement of $7.25, the Oklahoman reports. The coalition behind the petition says it is considering a constitutional challenge to the new law. Gov. Mary Fallin, who signed the bill into law, rejected the argument that allowing cities to raise the minimum wage would bring people out of poverty, saying having different minimum wages across the state could scare off businesses and a higher minimum wage would cost jobs. "Most minimum wage workers are young, single people working part-time or entry-level jobs," she said. "Many are high school or college students living with their parents in middle-class families." Numerous studies, however, have found this to be untrue, Al Jazeera notes. A recent Economic Policy Institute report found that almost 90% of minimum wage workers are over 20, a quarter have children to support, and more than half work full time.

final_summary: US Representative Anthony Weiner exchanged explicit messages with a young woman, later identified as Sydney Elaine Leathers, a field organizer for Obama for America, following his resignation from Congress. Initially, the conversations were shared anonymously on TheDirty.com, but they were quickly linked back to Leathers through her social media profiles on Facebook and Formspring. Leathers used the handles "Sydney Leathers" and "sidneyelainexo," and her online interactions with Weiner were characterized by flirtatious and intimate exchanges. In these exchanges, she expressed admiration for his legislative work and stated that he could continue sending explicit photos without consequence. A high school friend confirmed that the two had raunchy online chats last summer, during which Weiner allegedly promised Leathers job opportunities in either Chicago or New York. This friend also noted that Leathers felt she was the most important person in the relationship, even surpassing Huma Abedin in her esteem. Weiner's wife, Huma Abedin, publicly defended her husband in a press conference, describing him as "a smart, caring, and dedicated person" who has become "a better man." In a previously published essay, Abedin explained that she chose to stay with Weiner because of his dedication to public service and his personal growth.

reference: – BuzzFeed has identified Anthony Weiner's newly revealed sexting partner as Sydney Elaine Leathers, a progressive activist in her early 20s who lives in Indiana. Pictures on her Facebook and Formspring accounts look pretty close to the blurred images offered up on The Dirty when it broke the sexting story, and she's talked about Weiner quite a bit online (often on accounts that are now deleted). For example, in a June 2011 Facebook post, she declared: "Rep. Weiner can continue sending dick pics every single day for the rest of his life as long as he continues to legislate like he does. I decided." Her TwitPic account identifies her as an Obama for America field organizer. She also listed Weiner as one of her heroes, wrote at least one blog post about his resignation, and said on Facebook that he shouldn't have resigned. An anonymous source confirms BuzzFeed got the ID right and says he or she saw the messages exchanged a year ago, but a BuzzFeed reporter asked Weiner last night if he knew Leathers and—shocker—got no response. The source, who says he or she has known Leathers since high school, says she "felt she was the important one" to Weiner, even more important than wife Huma Abedin. As for Abedin, Daily Intel notes that she came to a "prescient conclusion" in September's Harper's Bazaar: "People have said many things about my husband—some nice, some not so nice. And that will surely continue. Launching this campaign was not an easy decision for our family to make. Putting yourself out there comes with a cost." (Meanwhile, "cubicle guy" has become a viral sensation after Weiner's press conference last night.)

final_summary:
President Donald Trump removed his chief of staff, Reince Priebus, on July 21, 2017, replacing him with Homeland Security Secretary John Kelly in a sudden move that ended Priebus' six-month tenure. According to Priebus, he had submitted his resignation to the President privately on Thursday, but sources close to him initially denied this, claiming he was not resigning. However, after Trump announced Kelly as his new chief of staff via Twitter upon returning to Joint Base Andrews outside Washington, sources later confirmed that Priebus had indeed resigned the previous day.

Priebus' removal was widely anticipated, as he had faced intense criticism for failing to contain leaks within the White House and struggling to establish order amidst the administration's numerous controversies. Despite his efforts to implement a sense of stability, Priebus bore significant responsibility for the administration's legislative setbacks, particularly the failed attempt to pass a healthcare replacement bill.

Tensions within the White House had escalated in recent weeks, culminating in a public feud between Priebus and newly appointed Communications Director Anthony Scaramucci, who publicly disparaged Priebus in a profanity-laced rant. Sources close to the President revealed that Trump's family members, including Ivanka Trump and Jared Kushner, had lost confidence in Priebus and encouraged the President to effect a change.

In contrast, White House Chief Strategist Steve Bannon is expected to remain in his position, despite rumors of his potential dismissal. Trump consulted with various conservatives, including Representative Mark Meadows, to gauge their opinions on keeping Bannon, ultimately deciding to retain him.

Kelly, a decorated Marine veteran with extensive experience in government service, will assume the role of chief of staff on Monday, participating in a meeting with Trump's cabinet. His appointment marks a significant shift in the White House's leadership dynamics, as Kelly brings a wealth of experience and a reputation for discipline to the position.

Priebus' tenure as chief of staff lasted only 189 days, making it one of the shortest in modern presidential history. During his time in office, he struggled to assert authority amidst a complex web of competing interests and alliances within the administration. His departure leaves behind a legacy of controversy and challenges, including the ongoing struggle to contain leaks and maintain order within the White House.

final_summary:
On April 10th, a Dutch TV crew was filming a documentary at the Royal Burgers Zoo in Arnhem, using a camera-equipped drone to capture footage of the chimpanzees in their 1.7-acre enclosure. During the filming, a female chimpanzee named Tushi demonstrated a remarkable display of problem-solving skills and tool use. She carefully selected a 6-foot-long branch from a nearby willow tree and carried it to a location where the drone could be easily targeted.

As the drone approached, Tushi prepared herself for the attack, displaying a grimace, a facial expression typically associated with assertiveness and determination in humans. Her deliberate actions suggest that she had planned the attack, much like a human would. Tushi's first attempt at swatting the drone did not succeed, but she persisted, making a second, more forceful strike that successfully knocked the device to the ground.

The camera captured the entire incident, providing valuable insight into Tushi's behavior and the complex cognitive abilities of chimpanzees. Researchers Jan van Hooff and Bas Lukkenaar, who studied the incident, believe that Tushi's behavior demonstrates a level of forward planning and deliberation, characteristic of tool use in primates. The chimps at the Royal Burgers Zoo have learned to use various tools, including sticks, through observation and experimentation, without explicit instruction from humans.

Tushi's actions highlight the innovative and adaptable nature of chimpanzees, showcasing their ability to assess the suitability of tools for specific tasks. By studying the behavior of Tushi and her fellow chimps, researchers can gain a deeper understanding of the evolution of human cognition and the development of advanced tool use in our primate cousins.

reference: – A chimpanzee that famously knocked a flying drone out of the air with a stick earlier this year wasn't reacting out of fear or annoyance but rather executing a pre-planned and deliberate attack. That's according to a new study in Primates. "This episode adds to the indications that chimpanzees engage in forward planning of tool-use acts," researcher Bas Lukkenaar says in a press release. The Christian Science Monitor reports a Dutch TV crew was using the drone to film a documentary on the chimps at the Royal Burgers' Zoo in April. After a test-run with the drone, the chimps started collecting sticks and climbing scaffolding. When the drone returned, a chimp named Tushi was ready, swiping it out of the air with a 6-foot branch. While studying footage from the busted drone, researchers focused on Tushi's facial expression during the attack, NBC News reports. They found the grimace she makes before and during the incident is similar to what humans do while carrying out a predetermined use of force. According to the press release, the lack of fear in Tushi's expression means she wasn't simply attacking out of reflex. The Monitor explains it's the difference between a calculated plan and swatting at a mosquito. This isn't unusual behavior for the chimps at Burgers' Zoo; they've taught themselves to use 13 different types of tools, the press release states. (Now find out how chimps are more advanced than humans.)

final_summary:
Charlo Greene, a former Alaskan TV reporter, is facing up to 54 years in prison for allegedly violating state laws related to her cannabis club in Alaska, a state where recreational marijuana was legalized in 2014. Greene's case has garnered significant attention and raised questions about the ongoing war on drugs and its impact on individuals and communities. 

In 2014, Greene, then a reporter for KTVA, unexpectedly quit her job on live television to reveal her ownership of the Alaska Cannabis Club, a business that provided marijuana to members through donations. This decision was met with widespread media attention, but it also drew the ire of authorities. Despite the state's new laws, the Anchorage Police Department conducted two raids on the club, resulting in Greene being charged with 14 felony and misdemeanor offenses, including misconduct involving a controlled substance.

Greene's case centers on the Alaska Cannabis Club's business model, which operated under a loophole in the state's laws that allowed for the transfer of up to an ounce of pot. However, prosecutors argue that the club and Greene are one entity, and therefore, she can be held criminally responsible for its actions. Greene disputes this claim, stating that the club was a sole proprietorship and that she was not directly involved in any transactions.

Greene's lawyer claims that the district attorney misled the grand jury about the business structure of the club, and that the charges are unjustified. Critics argue that the prosecution is overly aggressive and that Greene's treatment is a modern-day lynching. Many in the cannabis community have failed to rally around Greene, despite her high profile and dedication to the cause.

Greene's trial is set to begin in the coming months, and she faces the possibility of spending decades in prison if convicted. Alaska's struggle to establish clear regulations for the retail market has contributed to the confusion surrounding Greene's case. The state has only recently begun issuing licenses for retail businesses, and many operators have been operating in a gray area.

Greene's experience highlights the challenges faced by entrepreneurs in the cannabis industry and the need for clearer guidelines and protections for those who operate within the law. Her case also raises concerns about racial disparities in the application of marijuana laws, as a black woman, Greene is part of a group disproportionately affected by the war on drugs. Her prosecution has sparked outrage among advocates who argue that the government's tough-on-crime policies have led to mass incarceration and perpetuated systemic racism.

Despite the uncertainty surrounding her case, Greene remains committed to her advocacy work and continues to speak out against the injustices she believes she has faced. Her story serves as a reminder of the complexities and challenges inherent in the transition from prohibition to regulation in the cannabis industry.

reference: – Charlo Greene made headlines in 2014 when the then 26-year-old memorably quit her TV reporting gig on air while announcing her intention to push for the legalization of marijuana in Alaska: She said, "F--- it, I quit," before walking out of view. But as the Guardian reports, her ensuing off-screen plight has been largely ignored, even though she faces more than a half-century in prison. That's because Greene, whose legal name is Charlene Egbe, isn't just a cannabis advocate but the owner of the Alaska Cannabis Club, which she created on April 20, 2014, a full six months before Alaska voted to legalize the adult use of cannabis. In other words, she was receiving "donations" for marijuana through club "memberships" before it was legal to do so. Police raided the club twice and detectives made several undercover purchases, and though Greene wasn't directly involved in them, state prosecutors are charging her because the club is registered under her name. She's pleaded not guilty to charges of "misconduct involving a controlled substance," and the original indictment listed eight offenses that amounted to a possible 24 years in jail. The Guardian broke the news to Greene that six offenses have since been added, raising the total to 54 years. In the Weed Blog, Greene calls this a "modern-day lynching," and tells High Times that she hopes the cannabis community rallies behind her. "I need help more than ever," she says. "Now that I could lose the rest of my life because of cannabis, it feels like the people I fought for have abandoned me." (Alaskans can't smoke weed in public.)

final_summary:
In July 2009, Shane Bauer, Josh Fattal, and Sarah Shourd embarked on a hiking trip in the Kurdish mountains, having read online that the area was safe and been recommended by a friend. Unbeknownst to them, the region bordered Iran, and they soon found themselves detained by Iranian authorities. According to Shourd, they had no intention of crossing the border and were unaware of any warnings or restrictions.

The trio was apprehended and eventually sent to Evin prison, where Bauer and Fattal remained incarcerated. Shourd was released on $500,000 bail in September 2010 and returned to the United States. Her release was delayed until just before President Mahmoud Ahmadinejad attended the UN General Assembly in New York, reportedly due to concerns about her health.

The Iranian government initially planned to put Bauer and Fattal on trial, citing espionage charges against them. However, the trial was delayed due to Shourd's absence, with Judiciary Spokesman Qolamhossein Mohseni-Ejei stating that the court needed time for Shourd to either return to Iran or for her case to be handled separately. Their lawyer, Masoud Shafiee, expressed surprise at the postponement, claiming he had not received any notification from the authorities.

The delay coincided with efforts by the US and other nations to persuade Iran to resume talks about its nuclear program, which Iran insists is for peaceful purposes. The US State Department stated that it did not know how the hikers had entered Iranian territory and had not made any claims about their intentions. In contrast, an anonymous US military official suggested that the hikers' actions may have raised suspicions, but this assertion was disputed by Shourd, who described their experience as a simple hiking trip gone awry due to their proximity to the border.

The incident sparked a diplomatic row between the US and Iran, with some speculating about a potential prisoner swap involving Iranians held in US custody. One such individual, Shahrazad Mir Gholikhan, claimed to have experienced difficulties while imprisoned in the US, though the specifics of her situation were not disclosed.

reference: – The three American hikers detained by Iran weren’t captured in Iraq, says Sarah Shourd: They were beckoned over the border by an armed soldier, she tells the New York Times in an effort to set the WikiLeaks version of the story straight. “We did not actually enter Iran until he gestured to us. We were confused and worried and wanted to go back." In Shourd's fullest account of her ordeal to date, she goes into detail about their fateful overnight camping trip, describing "scores of campfires" near the waterfall they were hiking to—which abutted Iran. “I think we were extremely unlucky,” says Shourd. “I guess I never believed there would be so many hundreds of people close to a border.” Her interview with the Times comes ahead of a court date for her fellow hikers, which was set for Saturday but has been delayed by Iran until Shourd “can return to Iran or,” if she refuses, her case can “be dealt with differently,” said an Iranian official. But her lawyer says the trial should go forward; neither he nor Shourd, he tells Reuters, were notified of the change.

final_summary:
The film adaptation of Elizabeth Gilbert's bestselling memoir "Eat Pray Love" follows the story of Liz Gilbert, a successful New York writer struggling with her empty personal life. Played by Julia Roberts, Liz embarks on a year-long journey to rediscover herself, traveling to Italy, India, and Indonesia in search of spiritual enlightenment. While the film boasts stunning visuals, courtesy of cinematographer Robert Richardson, and Roberts shines in her portrayal of Liz, the movie ultimately falls short due to its inability to capture the essence of Gilbert's distinctive writing style, which relies on private, silent moments between the writer and her reader.

The screenplay, written by Murphy and Jennifer Salt, relies heavily on familiar tropes and clichés, failing to delve deeper into the complexities of Liz's emotional journey. This approach results in a film that feels shallow and touristy, lacking the nuance and depth of the original memoir. Despite its visual appeal, the movie's reliance on sentimental and predictable plot devices detracts from its overall impact.

In Italy, Liz learns to appreciate the simple pleasures of life, indulging in delicious food and wine, and meeting a handsome language coach, Luca Argentero. However, the film's portrayal of Liz's relationships, particularly her romance with Felipe, played by Javier Bardem, feels contrived and lacks depth. The supporting cast, including Viola Davis as Liz's best friend and publisher, is underutilized, and the film's pacing suffers from a lack of tension and conflict.

One notable exception is the scene with Richard Jenkins, who plays a fellow meditation student at Liz's Indian ashram. His passionate and naturalistic performance brings out the best in Roberts, creating a memorable moment in an otherwise forgettable film. Overall, the movie feels like a surface-level exploration of Liz's journey, rather than a genuine attempt to capture the essence of her transformative experience. As a result, the film receives a C+ grade, indicating that while it is enjoyable in a superficial sense, it lacks substance and depth, leaving viewers wanting more.

history.draft:
The film adaptation of Elizabeth Gilbert's bestselling memoir "Eat Pray Love" follows the story of Liz Gilbert, a successful New York writer struggling with her empty personal life. Played by Julia Roberts, Liz embarks on a year-long journey to rediscover herself, traveling to Italy, India, and Indonesia in search of spiritual enlightenment. While the film boasts stunning visuals, courtesy of cinematographer Robert Richardson, and Roberts shines in her portrayal of Liz, the movie ultimately falls short due to its inability to capture the essence of Gilbert's distinctive writing style, which relies on private, silent moments between the writer and her reader.

The screenplay, written by Murphy and Jennifer Salt, relies heavily on familiar tropes and clichés, failing to delve deeper into the complexities of Liz's emotional journey. This approach results in a film that feels shallow and touristy, lacking the nuance and depth of the original memoir. Despite its visual appeal, the movie's reliance on sentimental and predictable plot devices detracts from its overall impact.

In Italy, Liz learns to appreciate the simple pleasures of life, indulging in delicious food and wine, and meeting a handsome language coach, Luca Argentero. However, the film's portrayal of Liz's relationships, particularly her romance with Felipe, played by Javier Bardem, feels contrived and lacks depth. The supporting cast, including Viola Davis as Liz's best friend and publisher, is underutilized, and the film's pacing suffers from a lack of tension and conflict.

One notable exception is the scene with Richard Jenkins, who plays a fellow meditation student at Liz's Indian ashram. His passionate and naturalistic performance brings out the best in Roberts, creating a memorable moment in an otherwise forgettable film. Overall, the movie feels like a surface-level exploration of Liz's journey, rather than a genuine attempt to capture the essence of her transformative experience. As a result, the film receives a C+ grade, indicating that while it is enjoyable in a superficial sense, it lacks substance and depth, leaving viewers wanting more.

reference: – Eat, Pray, Love looks lovely but lacks both the spice and the depth of the best-selling memoir it's based on, say critics. Julia Roberts stars as a jaded New York divorcee traveling the world to seek enlightenment. Roberts' Liz says she wants to move outside her comfort zone, writes Kirk Honeycutt at the Hollywood Reporter, but as she journeys through Italy, India, and Indonesia, "the film never ventures, even once, into a situation that does not reek of comfy familiarity." The movie "is shameless wish-fulfillment, a Harlequin novel crossed with a mystic travelogue," complains Roger Ebert at the Chicago Sun-Times, though he notes that the overwhelmingly female audience he watched it with appeared quite moved. Roberts manages to make the "2 hours and 15 minutes of eating, praying and loving pleasant enough," writes Roger Moore at the Orlando Sentinel, who decides Eat, Pray, Love "isn’t a bad movie, just a spiritually dead one." Roberts is a great choice to play Liz but "her warmth, coupled with Javier Bardem's scruffy sexiness" as love interest Felipe, aren't enough to compensate for "the folded-map flatness of this production," writes Lisa Schwarzbaum at Entertainment Weekly.

final_summary:
Tom Clements, the 58-year-old executive director of the Colorado Department of Corrections, was shot and killed at his home in Monument, Colorado, on March 20, 2013. The shooting occurred around 8:30 p.m. when Clements answered the front door of his house, prompting a search for the gunman and raising questions about whether the attack was related to his position as the state's top corrections official. Authorities have not yet ruled out any possible motives, including the possibility that the shooting was linked to Clements' job.

Clements had been serving as the head of the DOC since January 2011, overseeing state and private prisons and parole operations. Prior to joining the Colorado Department of Corrections, he spent over three decades working in the Missouri Department of Corrections. The FBI and the Colorado Bureau of Investigation have joined the case, and authorities are searching for a dark-colored "boxy" car seen near Clements' home at the time of the shooting. A witness reported seeing one person driving away in the car, which had its engine running.

In the aftermath of the shooting, Governor John Hickenlooper expressed sadness and shock, describing Clements as "unfailingly kind and thoughtful." Hickenlooper also stated that he did not believe the killing was part of a larger attack against his cabinet. Clements' killing is reminiscent of the 2008 murder of Adams County prosecutor Sean May, who was shot and killed as he returned home from work; however, May's killer was never found.

The shooting also raises questions about the safety of high-profile officials in Colorado, particularly given the recent controversy surrounding the case of Homaidan al-Turki, a Saudi national who was convicted of sexually assaulting a housekeeper and keeping her as a virtual slave. Al-Turki had requested to be transferred to his home country to serve out his sentence, but Clements denied the request due to state law requiring sex offenders to undergo treatment while in prison. Al-Turki's case drew attention from Saudi officials and the US State Department, leading to a meeting between Colorado Attorney General John Suthers and King Abdullah of Saudi Arabia; however, it is unclear whether this case is directly related to Clements' killing.

In response to the shooting, the Colorado Department of Corrections has increased security measures for top officials, including the governor's mansion. This move is considered precautionary, with officials stating that there is no specific threat against other state leaders. Meanwhile, Governor Hickenlooper is set to sign new gun control laws in Colorado, which include expanded background checks and limits on ammunition clips. These measures come in response to the mass shooting at a movie theater in Aurora, Colorado, in July 2012, which left 12 people dead and dozens injured.

Hickenlooper's decision to support gun control measures marks a significant shift for the governor, who initially expressed skepticism about the effectiveness of stricter gun laws. However, after conducting research and gathering input from various stakeholders, including gun owners and advocates, Hickenlooper became convinced that the measures would help reduce gun violence. The new laws are expected to face opposition from gun rights groups, including the National Rifle Association, which has condemned the measures. However, supporters argue that the majority of Coloradans support the new laws, with a recent survey showing that 80% agree with expanded background checks and 61% support magazine limits.

Hickenlooper's decision to push for gun control measures has sparked controversy, with some critics accusing him of overreaching. However, the governor remains committed to finding common ground and moving forward with the new laws, citing the need for practical solutions to gun violence.

reference: – Tom Clements, the executive director of Colorado’s department of corrections, was shot dead last night—but the who and why remain a mystery. Clements was shot fatally in the chest as he answered the door of his home in Monument, Colo., reports NBC, but nobody else in the house was harmed. Although there's no clear connection, the timing is notable: the shooting comes on the eve of tough and controversial new gun laws in the state, following the movie theater massacre in Aurora last year. Colorado Gov. John Hickenlooper is expected to sign new legislation today that will restrict magazine capacity and expand background checks on private and online firearms sales. But his first duty this morning was instead to pen a letter to Department of Corrections employees. "I can hardly believe it, let alone write words to describe it," wrote Hickenlooper. "I am so sad. I have never worked with a better person than Tom, and I can't imagine our team without him."

final_summary:
Grocery stores have developed a strategy to minimize waste and maximize profits by repurposing unsold products. One example of this practice is the creation of rotisserie chickens, which are made from unsold chicken nearing its expiration date. These chickens are then sold at a lower price point than buying and cooking a raw chicken. This approach is not unique to chicken, as grocery stores also repurpose unsold produce and meat into various prepared foods, such as salads and deli items.

This practice allows grocery stores to maintain a diverse range of offerings without incurring significant losses. By utilizing unsold products, stores can provide customers with a variety of options, including hot bars, salad bars, bakeries, and deli counters. Consumers benefit from lower prices on raw chicken and access to affordable prepared foods.

Many large retailers, including Costco, employ dedicated rotisserie chicken programs, and similar strategies to minimize waste and optimize production. High-end stores like Whole Foods and Trader Joe's also engage in this practice, with the former's CEO confirming that unsold meat and produce are reused in prepared foods.

Some consumers may find the idea of consuming food near its expiration date unsettling, but it's essential to note that sell-by, use-by, and best-by dates are primarily guidelines for optimal quality, not indicators of food safety. Research suggests that these dates are often inaccurate, resulting in unnecessary waste and environmental consequences.

Despite being cautious about food safety, grocery stores discard a substantial amount of usable inventory each year, estimated to be around $900 million annually. This waste ultimately translates to higher costs for consumers in the form of increased prices. By choosing to buy rotisserie chickens and other prepared foods, consumers can contribute to reducing waste, supporting the environment, and keeping prices low for themselves and others.

reference: – Those who like chicken might have noticed a seemingly weird quirk in the grocery store: It's generally cheaper to buy a fully cooked rotisserie chicken than to buy a raw chicken and prepare it yourself at home, writes Megan McArdle at Bloomberg View. Cooking food yourself is typically cheaper, so why the exception here? McArdle cites a post at KCET that explains what's going on: Supermarkets use chickens that are about to hit their sell-by expiration date for those rotisseries. In fact, they do it not just with chicken but with many of the offerings in the prepared-food department. Underhanded? No, writes McArdle. It's actually kind of brilliant. Instead of taking a loss on the still-fine food, the stores cook it up and serve it hot. "This is the sort of thing that no one talks about when they talk about innovation—and yet, it’s a major way in which our economy has become more efficient over the last few decades," she writes. The practice benefits both the store and the consumer, and it reduces spoilage to boot. Click for her full post.

final_summary: On Wednesday evening around 8 p.m., a bizarre incident occurred in Mendon when a Toyota Corolla, driven by Nabila Altahan, became suspended from a telephone guy wire after she reacted too quickly to a GPS unit's instructions to turn around. According to State Police, Altahan was traveling westbound on Route 4 in Mendon when her GPS device suddenly directed her to make a U-turn, as she had apparently passed her intended destination. As a result, Altahan lost control of her vehicle, which came to rest suspended from the telephone pole's supporting wire. Fortunately, no one was injured in the incident. Witnesses reported seeing the driver jump out of the car as it hung suspended from the pole. The scene drew a response from both State Police and the Rutland City Fire Department, which arrived shortly after the incident.

The scene drew a response from both State Police and the Rutland City Fire Department, which arrived shortly after the incident. Local resident Ray Ault, who happened to be driving by at the time, also witnessed the aftermath of the accident.

final_summary:
The Ass Kicker, a unique coffee concoction, has garnered attention in Adelaide, Australia, due to its exceptionally high caffeine content. Developed by Steve Benington, the owner of Viscous Cafe, the drink contains the equivalent of 80 standard cups of coffee and is designed to be savored over a three- to four-hour period. Initially, Benington created the Ass Kicker for an emergency department nurse who required a potent beverage to stay alert during extended shifts. 

The drink comprises four espresso shots, two different strengths of cold drip, and milk, totaling five grams of caffeine. Although Benington has modified the original recipe, he cautions customers about the potential risks associated with excessive caffeine consumption, particularly for those with high blood pressure or heart conditions. He advises patrons to enjoy the drink slowly and mindfully, as rapid consumption can lead to unpleasant effects such as tremors, sweating, dilated pupils, stuttering, dizziness, and nausea.

Benington adopts a cautious approach when offering the Ass Kicker, engaging in thorough discussions with prospective customers to ensure they comprehend the associated risks. The drink costs approximately $12 for a 16-ounce serving. Compared to the average daily caffeine intake for adults, which stands at around 300 milligrams, according to a 2012 FDA report, the Ass Kicker's caffeine content is substantial.

Experts offer varying perspectives on the potential health implications of the Ass Kicker. Maggie Sweeney, a researcher at Johns Hopkins University, notes that the 5-gram caffeine content may trigger adverse reactions in individuals with low tolerance, potentially causing symptoms like restlessness, insomnia, and irregular heartbeat. She estimates that a lethal dose of caffeine could be around 10 grams, double the amount present in the Ass Kicker.

Dr. Sanjiv Chopra from Harvard Medical School advocates for moderate coffee consumption, highlighting studies linking regular coffee drinking to reduced risks of various diseases, including liver, prostate, colon, endometrial, and skin cancer. However, he expresses concern regarding the Ass Kicker, describing it as a "huge caffeine load" and recommending a maximum of two to four cups of coffee per day for optimal health benefits.

Interestingly, the creator of the Ass Kicker emphasizes that the drink is intended for those seeking a prolonged energy boost rather than an immediate caffeine rush. By savoring the drink slowly, customers can experience a sustained energy level, as opposed to a rapid spike followed by a crash. Nonetheless, the debate surrounding the safety and advisability of the Ass Kicker underscores the intricacies of caffeine consumption and the significance of responsible moderation.

reference: – One cafe owner in Adelaide, Australia, is playing around with the world's most popular drug, and experts aren't feeling terribly perky about it. Steve Benington, owner of Viscous Coffee, has created the Asskicker, a drink consisting of four shots of espresso, four 48-hour brewer cold drip ice cubes (each containing a bit more caffeine than is found in two shots of espresso), 120ml of 10-day brewed cold drip, and another four of those cold drip ice cubes to finish it off, reports Adelaide Now. All told, it has 5 grams of caffeine, about 80 times as a typical espresso, and it provides 18 hours of "sustained up time," as Benington puts it. "Some people love it and some are broken by it, but it’s all in the name of fun," he says. Benington developed the drink after an emergency room nurse asked for something that would get her through a long shift. It comes with a warning for people with heart or blood pressure issues, and he has "quite a detailed talk" with anyone who orders it because "if I can talk them out of it, they're not ready," he tells CNN. One area dietitian is not amused. "Caffeine is a drug, not a game or a toy," she says. The author of a book espousing the benefits of caffeine also chimes in that this drink goes too far. "I would be very wary of consuming it." Mashable notes that Benington does suggest people sip the drink over three to four hours to "lift you up and keep you there." It also notes that a fatal dose of caffeine is generally considered to be 18 grams over one or two hours. (There is such a thing as fatal caffeine intoxication.)

final_summary:
A controversy erupted surrounding Indiana Senate candidate Richard Mourdock's comments on rape, which sparked widespread criticism and calls for him to apologize. During a debate on October 24, Mourdock stated that pregnancies resulting from rape are "something God intended." 

President Barack Obama condemned Mourdock's remarks, emphasizing that rape is a crime and that such comments are misguided. He argued that women should be able to make their own healthcare decisions without interference from politicians. Obama also highlighted the significance of the upcoming presidential election in determining the future of reproductive rights, particularly the potential impact on Roe v. Wade.

The Romney campaign attempted to distance itself from Mourdock's comments, with spokesperson Andrea Saul releasing a statement expressing disagreement with Mourdock's views on exceptions for rape and incest. However, the campaign chose not to withdraw its support for Mourdock or request that he remove a television advertisement featuring Romney.

Initially, Senators John McCain and Barack Obama criticized Mourdock's comments, with McCain suggesting that he might reconsider his support for the candidate if Mourdock issued an apology. However, McCain later clarified that he still supported Mourdock after the candidate claimed his words had been misinterpreted. Obama's campaign responded to the controversy by releasing a web video highlighting the discrepancy between Romney's support for Mourdock and the candidate's own stance on reproductive rights. The video aimed to emphasize Romney's conservative views on the issue.

President Barack Obama weighed in on the issue, stating that rape is a crime and that Mourdock's comments were misguided. He emphasized that women should be able to make their own healthcare decisions, free from interference by politicians. Obama also highlighted the importance of the upcoming presidential election in determining the future of reproductive rights, citing the potential impact on Roe v. Wade.

Meanwhile, the Romney campaign attempted to distance itself from Mourdock's comments, with spokesperson Andrea Saul releasing a statement expressing disagreement with Mourdock's views on exceptions for rape and incest. However, the campaign chose not to withdraw its support for Mourdock or ask him to remove a television advertisement featuring Romney.

Senators John McCain and Barack Obama both criticized Mourdock's comments, with McCain suggesting that he would reconsider his support for the candidate if Mourdock issued an apology. In a subsequent clarification, McCain's office stated that he still supported Mourdock after all, following the candidate's press conference where he claimed his words had been misinterpreted.

Obama's campaign responded to the controversy by releasing a web video highlighting the discrepancy between Romney's support for Mourdock and the candidate's own stance on reproductive rights. The video concluded with the message that Romney remains an "extremely conservative" candidate. Despite the backlash, the Romney campaign maintained its backing for Mourdock, indicating that the candidate's comments would not significantly impact the Republican Party's stance on the issue.

reference: – President Obama slammed Senate candidate Richard Mourdock's rape comments on the Tonight Show last night. "I don't know how these guys come up with these ideas," Obama said. "Let me make a very simple proposition. Rape is rape. It is a crime." He noted "Roe vs. Wade is probably hanging in the balance" of the election, Politico reports. Mourdock's comments that pregnancies from rape are "something God intended" are "exactly why you don't want a bunch of politicians, mostly male, making decisions about women's health care decisions," Obama said. "Women are capable of making these decisions in consultation with their partners, with their doctors." Meanwhile, asked by Anderson Cooper whether he still supports Mourdock, John McCain said it "depends on what he does," the Huffington Post reports. "If he apologizes and says he misspoke and he was wrong and he asks the people to forgive him, then obviously I’d be the first" to support him. Mourdock has in fact issued an apology. Mitt Romney, for his part, still supports Mourdock, though his campaign says he "disagrees" with the Indiana Republican's comments: "We disagree on the policy regarding exceptions for rape and incest but still support him." The campaign says it hasn't called on Mourdock to withdraw ads featuring Romney.

final_summary:
Rapper and entrepreneur Jay-Z purchased Tidal, a small streaming service, in 2015 for $56 million with ambitions to create an online music empire offering exclusive content from high-profile celebrities. However, the service faced intense scrutiny and skepticism from the music industry due to its struggles to gain traction and significant financial losses. Despite being considered a likely candidate for failure or takeover by a major player such as Apple, Jay-Z secured a crucial lifeline through a deal with Sprint, where the telecommunications giant acquired a 33% stake in Tidal for $200 million.

As part of this agreement, Tidal became available to Sprint's 45 million retail customers, and the companies partnered to produce exclusive content from Tidal's roster of artist-owners. Sprint CEO Marcelo Claure joined Tidal's board of directors, acknowledging Jay-Z's dedication to creating a premium music streaming platform. This collaboration also established a dedicated marketing fund with an annual budget of $75 million to support artist initiatives and exclusive content.

The partnership between Tidal and Sprint dates back to the company's US launch in March 2015, when Sprint initially explored acquiring a stake in the company before ultimately deciding against it. The current deal seems to be a result of those earlier discussions. Following recent controversy surrounding Tidal's alleged inflated subscriber numbers and ongoing rumors of a possible acquisition by Apple, the partnership provides Tidal with access to a substantial customer base, free marketing opportunities, and additional resources to compete with dominant players in the streaming market, including Spotify, Apple, and Amazon.

final_summary:
A recent chemical attack in Syria has heightened concerns in the Mediterranean region, prompting the US Navy to adjust its operations. The regional commander of the Navy's fleet has delayed a scheduled port call in Naples, Italy, for one of its destroyers, keeping it alongside another destroyer equipped with Tomahawk cruise missiles in proximity to Syria. 

This decision reflects the Navy's readiness to respond to potential military action, although Pentagon officials emphasize that it was made independently of explicit instructions from Washington. The Russian government has called on Syrian President Bashar al-Assad to permit UN investigators to access the areas near Damascus affected by the attack. Diplomats suggest that this request does not signify a change in Russia's stance regarding support for Assad or opposition to UN Security Council sanctions.

Russia's Foreign Ministry places the responsibility for securing access to the alleged incident site on the Syrian opposition forces. Additionally, the ministry implies that the attack might have been a premeditated provocation by the rebels, referencing online criticisms of government troops published before the attack. However, this assertion seems to stem from a misinterpretation of YouTube's time-stamping practices, which can be confusing due to the discrepancy between Eastern Time and Pacific Time zones.

Aleksandr K. Lukashevich, a spokesperson for Russia's Foreign Ministry, commended the Assad government for welcoming UN investigator Carla del Ponte, who had earlier suggested that the rebels might have employed chemical weapons. Lukashevich also criticized the Syrian opposition for hindering the UN investigation. The Syrian government has chosen not to comment on the matter.

Reports from CBS News, citing administration officials, indicate that US intelligence agencies identified unusual activity at known chemical weapons sites in Syria preceding the attack, which they suspect might have been preparations for the assault.

reference: – A government backed by Russia is accused of killing its own civilians. That applies not only to the current situation in Syria but to the 1999 situation in Kosovo. And as the New York Times reports, top US officials are keeping Kosovo very much in mind as they weigh their options. President Obama would likely never get the approval of the UN Security Council for a military response because of Russia's veto power. When Bill Clinton was in the same boat in 1999, he turned to NATO and got its blessing for 78 days of airstrikes. “It’s a step too far to say we’re drawing up legal justifications for an action, given that the president hasn’t made a decision,” a government official tells the Times. “But Kosovo, of course, is a precedent of something that is perhaps similar.” Obama was meeting today with national security aides to determine next steps if the US concludes that Bashar al-Assad used chemical weapons. (Assad in turn accused the rebels of doing the same today.) Meanwhile, the US Navy is keeping two destroyers positioned in the Mediterranean in case the green light is given for a cruise-missile attack, reports the Wall Street Journal.

final_summary:
A Danish radio station, Radio24syv, staged a provocative stunt to spark debate about the treatment of animals in Denmark's agriculture sector. Hosts killed a baby rabbit named Allan on-air using a bicycle pump, claiming it was necessary to expose the disconnect between people's attitudes toward animal welfare and their consumption of meat. According to the hosts, they consulted with a zookeeper from Aalborg Zoo, who revealed that he regularly kills baby rabbits to feed the zoo's predators, and used a similar method to humanely euthanize the rabbit.

Reality TV star Linse Kessler, who appeared on the program as an animal rights advocate, attempted to intervene and rescue the rabbit, but was eventually asked to leave the studio. Despite the backlash, Radio24syv defended the stunt, arguing that it highlighted the harsh realities of the meat industry, including the treatment of chickens and pigs in Danish farms.

Asger Juhl, one of the hosts involved in the stunt, discussed the experience, noting that he had previously killed animals for food, including chickens, ducks, and fish. However, he found the experience of killing a mammal, particularly a cute and endearing creature like the rabbit, to be more challenging. Juhl claimed that the stunt was meant to provoke discussion and raise awareness about the connection between consuming meat and animal suffering.

Critics argue that the use of a bicycle pump to kill the rabbit was inhumane, although Juhl maintained that the method was similar to that used by the zookeeper and was intended to be quick. The hosts ultimately ate the rabbit, with Juhl stating that it was a meal. His children were reportedly fascinated by the process of turning the rabbit into dinner.

Radio24syv received severe criticism and even death threats following the stunt, but Juhl expressed surprise at the intensity of the backlash. He stated that he still had the full support of his employers at Radio24syv and acknowledged that he might face criticism for participating in the stunt.

reference: – A Danish radio DJ chose a controversial way of drawing attention to what he sees as the flawed debate over animal welfare: He hit a 9-week-old rabbit named Allan three times with an iron bicycle pump while on the air Monday, then wrung its neck. Later, Asger Juhl of Radio24syv ate it for dinner. Angry listeners have since called for a boycott of the station, and much worse for Juhl himself, reports the BBC. "I did it to show the hypocrisy in the debate," he says. "Many people are outraged by what I did, at the same time many of these people will eat meat. And when they eat meat, an animal will die." Juhl says he consulted with Aalborg Zoo on a humane way to kill rabbits and was told it used an "iron stick." The zookeeper "hits several baby rabbits every week [to feed] the snakes," Juhl explains, per the Local. His employer is unapologetic. The New York Times quotes station editor-in-chief Jorgen Ramskov as saying the rabbit "was taken good care of, it had a nice life, and it was killed in a decent way." Animals endure "horrific suffering on their way to our dinner tables … without it invoking any strong reactions or calls for boycott," a station rep tells Sky News. Perhaps fueling the outcry on social media, per the Times: The station posted two videos on its Facebook page: a 20-second clip showing Juhl cradling and petting a very-alive Allan, and a 10-second clip showing what the station described as pieces of Allan, cooking in a pan. Those two parts are important, argued the station in a Facebook post yesterday. "We knew that we would be accused of provocation. But it is not an empty provocation; the presenters of the program ate the animal after killing it." (It's not the first controversial animal killing in Denmark.)

final_summary:
A confrontation between a resident and local police in Parma, Ohio, resulted in a citation and an impending trial for Doug 'Deo' Odolecki. Odolecki stood on a street corner with a homemade sign warning drivers about an upcoming drunken-driving checkpoint. The sign, written on white poster-board with black letters, read: "Check point ahead!" However, two words - "Turn now" - proved to be the contentious issue.

Parma Police Lieutenant Brian McCann requested Odolecki remove those specific words from the sign, citing obstruction of police business. When Odolecki refused, Officer James Manzo, who was involved in a $40,000 police brutality lawsuit earlier in the year, issued him a ticket. This incident occurred despite the fact that police are required by Ohio law to inform residents a week prior to setting up a drunken-driving checkpoint and provide the exact location, start time, and end times a few hours beforehand.

Odolecki claimed that he had used the same sign for two years without incident, and that McCann and Manzo attempted to be civil while still enforcing what he believed to be a violation of his rights. His attorney, John Gold, took on the case pro bono, supporting Odolecki's actions as part of the police accountability activist movement. Gold argued that the citation would have been justified only if Odolecki had been obstructing an investigation or posing a safety hazard, which was not the case since he was standing on the sidewalk near a drunken-driving checkpoint.

Gold stated that the issue lay not with the sign itself, but rather with the instruction to turn, which he believes is protected speech under the First Amendment. In response to this incident, a bill is pending in the Ohio House of Representatives that aims to permit drivers to legally warn others of upcoming checkpoints by flashing their headlights. Despite the citation, Odolecki intends to continue warning drivers about future checkpoints.

Doug "Deo" Odolecki got the citation Friday night while standing on a street corner with his sign that began, "Check point ahead!" Police, who are obligated to make such checkpoints public in Ohio, were OK with the sign in general but told Odolecki to remove the advice to "turn now," reports the Cleveland Plain Dealer. (Ohio is poised to let drivers flash their lights at one another to warn of police, and judges elsewhere in the country have blessed that move in the name of free speech.)

final_summary:
Ralph and Marcella Bracamonte hired 64-year-old Diane Stretton as a live-in nanny to care for their three children in exchange for room and board after finding her on Craigslist. Initially, the family reported being satisfied with Stretton's work, but issues arose around the third week, with Stretton reportedly slowing down and eventually stopping altogether. Despite being fired on June 6, Stretton remained in the house, claiming she was unable to work due to health problems and citing the need for rest. 

Stretton's behavior led to a standoff with the Bracamontes, who alleged she had become increasingly demanding and had even called the police when they played music too loudly. As the situation gained national attention, Stretton's past litigious history surfaced, revealing multiple lawsuits filed against her family members, including her sisters and son, as well as a car rental agency. Her name appears on California's Vexatious Litigant List, indicating a pattern of filing frivolous or repetitive lawsuits.

In response to the mounting pressure, Stretton sent an email to the Bracamontes' lawyer, stating she would leave the house by July 4, contingent upon certain conditions: the absence of media presence and permission to stay in the house for three additional nights to avoid the impending heatwave. However, Marcella Bracamonte suspects this may be a ruse, fearing Stretton might attempt to occupy the house while they are away for a family wedding on July 2, with alternative arrangements made for someone to stay behind.

To resolve the situation, the Bracamontes must follow the proper eviction procedures, which can take months to complete. Authorities have warned the family about potential consequences if they interfere with Stretton's room, emphasizing the importance of respecting her rights as a tenant. If Stretton fails to comply with the agreed-upon terms, she may face arrest for trespassing and/or violating a court order.

reference: – The fired nanny who has been refusing to leave her former employers' home says she is now ready to go—when conditions are just right. Diane Stretton over the weekend emailed Marcella and Ralph Bracamonte's lawyer blaming the weather and the media for her failure to depart their California home, ABC News reports. There are "always a bunch of news vehicles right in front of the house," the 64-year-old complains. "The media needs to be completely gone before I continue moving." She noted a predicted near-100-degree heat wave will make it impossible to move in the next few days because she "can't work in that kind of heat"—though "if the media stays away, I will be out by the 4th of July. But that depends on the circus not continuing." But Marcella Bracamonte, who says she fired Stretton last month after she "just stopped working," isn't convinced. "I don’t believe her. She is going to show up when I am not here with a bunch of food and water and she will barricade herself in her room," she says. Stretton, for her part, tells KNX 1070 news radio she only refused to work twice, when she had the flu and had worked 90 days straight. "I didn’t get lunch breaks, I didn’t get coffee breaks, I didn't get any holidays. Basically, I was working 24/7." Bracamonte, who tells People that she hired Stretton through a Craigslist ad and that her references checked out, says she and her family will be out of town on the day when Stretton claims she is going to move—but there will be plenty of other family members house-sitting. "I feel like she knows that I'm going to be gone and that she wants to lock me out of my home," she says.

final_summary:
A recent study conducted by the Chronicle of Higher Education reveals a concerning trend in the US higher education system, where nearly one-third of four-year colleges experienced a decline in graduation rates between 2002 and 2008. However, the study also highlights the resilience of top-performing institutions, which maintained high graduation rates throughout the period. 

The study analyzed data from over 1,000 four-year public and private non-profit colleges and universities, focusing on institutions classified as research, master's, or Baccalaureate Colleges—Arts & Sciences by the Carnegie Foundation for the Advancement of Teaching in 2005. The data show that Harvard University boasts the highest undergraduate completion rate, with a staggering 98% of students graduating by 2008. Yale University and the University of Notre Dame follow closely, with rates of 97% and 96%, respectively. Among public research universities, the University of Virginia leads the pack with a 93% graduation rate, followed by the College of William and Mary and the University of California-Berkeley.

However, the study also sheds light on the struggles faced by many institutions, which experienced fluctuations in graduation rates due to various factors, including changes in student demographics and institutional selectivity. For instance, the University of Tulsa saw a significant drop in graduation rates, from 63% in 2003 to 63% in 2008, despite experiencing an increase in admissions selectivity. Similarly, the University of San Francisco witnessed a decline in graduation rates, from 67% in 2003 to 67% in 2008, while becoming more selective in its admissions process.

The study suggests that the recession may have played a role in the decline of graduation rates, as well as other factors such as changes in student demographics and institutional selectivity. Furthermore, the analysis notes that colleges often experience fluctuations in graduation rates due to small year-to-year variations in the number of students enrolling and completing degrees. Additionally, the study acknowledges that a college's graduation rate can rise or fall depending on its level of selectivity, with more selective institutions tending to have higher graduation rates.

The data also reveal that some institutions, such as Golden Gate University and Alliant International University, enrolled relatively few students who met the federal government's criteria for being counted in the graduation rate, making it challenging to accurately assess their performance. The study emphasizes the importance of considering these limitations when interpreting the data.

In terms of specific trends, the study identifies 12 public and private research universities that experienced significant declines in graduation rates, including the University of Tulsa, the University of San Francisco, and the University of the Pacific. On the other hand, several institutions demonstrated notable improvements, such as the University of Southern California, which saw a rise in graduation rates from 76% in 2003 to 88% in 2008.

Overall, the study provides valuable insights into the complex issues facing the US higher education system, highlighting the need for continued attention to graduation rates and the factors influencing them. By examining the successes and challenges of various institutions, policymakers and educators can work towards creating a more effective and equitable system that supports student success and achievement.

final_summary:
The Internet Archive conducts periodic crawls to preserve web pages and maintain accurate links, aiming to minimize the occurrence of broken links across the internet. In support of this initiative, the archive has focused on crawling websites associated with the "No More 404" project, which aims to preserve content before it becomes inaccessible.

God's Little People Cat Rescue on the Greek Island of Syros is currently advertising a paid live-in caretaker position, offering free housing and a salary. The job listing appears on the organization's Facebook page, where it assures readers of its legitimacy and invites verification from friends.

Located in Santa Barbara, California, ResqCats is a non-profit cat sanctuary dedicated to rescuing, caring for, and adopting abandoned cats and kittens. Upon arrival, many of these animals require love and socialization due to their often-frightened state. To match each cat with the ideal home environment, ResqCats' staff takes the time to get to know each animal individually.

The organization frequently receives calls from individuals mourning the loss of their pets, underscoring the strong bond between humans and felines. Those considering adopting a cat or kitten are encouraged to reach out to ResqCats, whether motivated by the loss of a beloved pet or a desire to provide a loving home. Visitors can explore the online sanctuary to learn more about ResqCats and its mission, including viewing photos of available cats and kittens.

reference: – When Joan and Richard Bowell decided it was time to leave the Greek island of Syros, they first needed to find someone to manage the cat sanctuary they'd established there. God's Little People Cat Rescue, which started with one stray mama cat and her two kittens, expanded to more than 60 cats roaming the Bowells' property as they continued to take in strays. They posted a Facebook ad for a modestly paid job managing the sanctuary—and 40,000 people responded. The ad went viral, spawning headlines about the "dream job" or "purrrfect job," leading the Bowells to explain to would-be caretakers that it would, in fact, often be a difficult job—not only would poop have to be scooped and the like, but "heartbreaking decisions" would need to be made about sick cats. Months later, as the Washington Post reports, the sanctuary's caretaker has been chosen. Jeffyne Telson's husband sent her the ad soon after it was posted in August, the 62-year-old recalls. Telson runs her own cat rescue in Santa Barbara, Calif., only taking in strays; she's adopted out 3,000 cats and kittens over the past 21 years and has kept those who can't be placed at her own small sanctuary. Her application stood out, and the Bowells traveled to California to visit the couple in September. Joan and Jeffyne clicked immediately, and the Bowells offered Telson the job. She'll run the Syros sanctuary for some months, while volunteers run her Santa Barbara rescue, and other finalists for the Syros job will likely then take their own turns managing God's Little People. Meanwhile, the Bowells are looking into expanding the sanctuary, possibly even using it as an international training center for similarly-minded volunteers.

final_summary:
A white nationalist rally, known as the Unite the Right rally, was held in Charlottesville, Virginia, on August 12, 2017, to protest the city's decision to remove a statue of Confederate General Robert E. Lee from a downtown park. The event drew hundreds of white nationalists and counter-protesters, resulting in violent clashes throughout the day. 

Tensions escalated as supporters and counter-protesters engaged in physical altercations, threw objects, and used chemical sprays. Virginia Governor Terry McAuliffe declared a state of emergency in response to the situation, and police ordered people to disperse from the area around the statue. However, the situation continued to deteriorate, with several violent clashes breaking out.

At around 1:30 pm, a silver Dodge Charger driven by 20-year-old James Alex Fields of Maumee, Ohio, plowed into a crowd of people on the Downtown Mall, killing 32-year-old Heather Heyer and injuring at least 19 others. Fields, who was later arrested and charged with second-degree murder and three counts of malicious wounding, allegedly backed up the one-way street and fled the scene.

Following the car attack, a helicopter carrying two Virginia State Police troopers, Lieutenant H. Jay Cullen and Trooper-pilot Berke M.M. Bates, crashed in a nearby wooded area, killing both occupants. The helicopter was assisting law enforcement officers monitoring the white nationalist rally at the time of the crash.

In the aftermath of the violence, the Richmond FBI Office launched a civil rights investigation into the deadly car attack, collecting evidence and gathering facts. The Charlottesville Police Department also reported that three additional individuals, Troy Dunigan, Jacob L. Smith, and James M. O'Brien, were arrested in connection with the violent clashes.

White nationalist leader Richard Spencer, who was scheduled to speak at the rally, denied responsibility for the violence and blamed the police for the chaos, claiming that they endangered lives by mishandling the situation. Meanwhile, First Lady Melania Trump called for peace, urging Americans to communicate without hate in their hearts.

The incident sparked widespread condemnation, including a statement from the NHL's Detroit Red Wings, which denounced the use of their logo by a white nationalist group and expressed interest in taking legal action. Vigils were held in memory of the victims, and the community came together to mourn the loss of life and reflect on the tragic events that unfolded in Charlottesville.

reference: – US officials have opened a civil rights investigation into the deadly car attack that took place amid clashes of white nationalists and counter-demonstrators, reports the AP. The investigation was announced late Saturday by the US Attorney's Office for the Western District of Virginia and the FBI's Richmond field office. Attorney General Jeff Sessions says US Attorney Rick Mountcastle has begun the investigation and will have the full support of the Justice Department. Sessions says, "The violence and deaths in Charlottesville strike at the heart of American law and justice. When such actions arise from racial bigotry and hatred, they betray our core values and cannot be tolerated." In other developments: The man who allegedly plowed his car into a crowd of protesters, James Alex Fields Jr., a 20-year-old from Ohio, was charged with second-degree murder and three counts of malicious wounding, reports NBC 29. Troy Dunigan, a 21-year-old from Chattanooga, Tenn., was charged with disorderly conduct; Jacob L. Smith, a 21-year-old from Louisa, Va., was charged with assault and battery; and James M. O'Brien, 44, of Gainesville, Fla., was charged with carrying a concealed handgun. White nationalist Richard Spencer accused police of endangering lives in how they handled the rally, saying he "did not attempt to engage in any kind of violence. So the idea that I could be held responsible is absurd. It's like blaming the fire department for a fire." Spencer also said he found President Trump's comments on the violence to be "rather vague and kind of lame." Fields' mother says he told her he was going to the rally and that she told him "to be careful" and peaceful. Rally organizer Jason Kessler said whoever drove a car into counter-protesters "did the wrong thing." He said he was saddened people were hurt and criticized law enforcement's response, saying they did a poor job controlling the chaos to allow free speech.

final_summary:
Regional organizations and international leaders emphasize the need for a smooth transition from crisis to democracy in Libya. United Nations Secretary-General Ban Ki-moon convened a meeting with representatives from the League of Arab States, the Organization of Islamic Cooperation, the African Union, and the European Union, resulting in a unified call for a transition based on inclusiveness, reconciliation, and national unity. The international community agrees to provide assistance in developing police capacity, considering the abundance of small arms in the country, and to help with social-economic recovery, rule of law, and institution-building.

Despite ongoing fighting, the momentum appears to be shifting in favor of the rebels, with opposition forces making significant gains and establishing a new government in Libya. However, the whereabouts of Libyan leader Muammar Gadhafi remain uncertain, with Principal Deputy Press Secretary Josh Earnest stating that there is currently no evidence indicating he has left the country. Earnest calls on Gadhafi to relinquish power and turn himself in to face accountability.

Rebel forces have taken control of various locations, including the airport in Tripoli, but face challenges securing areas still held by Gadhafi loyalists. Concerns over revenge killings by both sides persist, with Amnesty International documenting cases of abuse and summary executions by pro-Gadhafi forces and rebel groups. The International Criminal Court has issued an arrest warrant for Gadhafi, charging him with crimes against humanity.

To stabilize the situation, the National Transitional Council (NTC) has relocated its political base from Benghazi to Tripoli, with half of the leadership already in place. However, the restoration of essential services and supplies remains pending until Gadhafi's status is clarified. The UN has appealed to all parties to prevent further violence and revenge attacks, urging those in positions of authority to take steps to ensure no crimes are committed.

Atrocities continue to be reported, including the discovery of bodies in hospitals and clinics, some showing signs of torture and execution-style killings. The UN has agreed to release $1.5 billion in Libyan assets to address immediate humanitarian needs, following a modification to the original proposal. The rebels are preparing to launch a major operation to capture Sirte, Gadhafi's hometown and a key stronghold of loyalist forces, which is expected to be heavily defended. The outcome of this operation will significantly impact the conflict's trajectory and Libya's future.

history.draft:
As the conflict in Libya continues, regional organizations and international leaders emphasize the need for a smooth transition from crisis to democracy. United Nations Secretary-General Ban Ki-moon convened a meeting with representatives from the League of Arab States, the Organization of Islamic Cooperation, the African Union, and the European Union, resulting in a unified call for a transition based on inclusiveness, reconciliation, and national unity. The international community agrees to provide assistance in developing police capacity, considering the abundance of small arms in the country, and to help with social-economic recovery, rule of law, and institution-building.

Despite ongoing fighting, the momentum appears to be shifting in favor of the rebels, with opposition forces making significant gains and establishing a new government in Libya. However, the whereabouts of Libyan leader Muammar Gadhafi remain unknown, with Principal Deputy Press Secretary Josh Earnest stating that there is currently no evidence indicating he has left the country. Earnest urges Gadhafi to relinquish power and turn himself in to face accountability.

Rebel forces have taken control of various locations, including the airport in Tripoli, but struggle to secure areas still held by Gadhafi loyalists. The opposition has also expressed concerns over revenge killings by both sides, with Amnesty International documenting cases of abuse and summary executions by pro-Gadhafi forces and rebel groups. The International Criminal Court has issued an arrest warrant for Gadhafi, charging him with crimes against humanity.

In a bid to stabilize the situation, the National Transitional Council (NTC) has moved its political base from Benghazi to Tripoli, with half of the leadership already in place. However, the process of restoring essential services and supplies remains on hold until Gadhafi is either killed or captured. The UN has called on all parties to prevent further violence and revenge attacks, urging those in positions of authority to take steps to ensure no crimes are committed.

Reports of atrocities continue to emerge, including the discovery of bodies in hospitals and clinics, with some showing signs of torture and execution-style killings. The UN has agreed to release $1.5 billion in Libyan assets to address immediate humanitarian needs, but the decision was delayed due to concerns raised by South Africa regarding official recognition of the NTC as Libya's leaders.

Meanwhile, the rebels are preparing to launch a major operation to capture Sirte, Gadhafi's hometown and a key stronghold of loyalist forces. The city is expected to be heavily defended, with reports suggesting that Gadhafi has deployed tanks and troops to prepare for the impending battle. The outcome of this operation will likely determine the fate of the conflict and the future of Libya.

reference: – The UN has unfrozen $1.5 billion in frozen Libyan assets to be used as humanitarian aid as it called on both sides of the conflict to avoid revenge killings, the BBC reports. The money had been seized by the US in the spring. It won’t go straight to the rebels, notes the New York Times: Instead, the US and international agencies will direct it toward humanitarian efforts. The head of the Transitional National Council pledged to “favor the countries which helped us” in accordance with “the support which they gave us.” With about half the Council in Tripoli, the city is largely in rebel hands—but fighting has continued, including reports of summary killings by both rebels and loyalists. Meanwhile, rebels have met opposition in their struggle to reach Moammar Gadhafi’s hometown, Sirte, which British planes bombed last night, hammering what Britain called a “large headquarters bunker,” CNN reports. Meanwhile, an empty hospital in the Abu Salim area—which has seen vicious fighting—held dozens of decomposing bodies, notes CBS News.

final_summary:
The Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) has announced plans to conduct emergency inspections on 80 older Boeing 737s, including 737-300s, 737-400s, and 737-500s, built between the 1980s and 1990s, with over 30,000 flight cycles. These models account for about 175 aircraft worldwide, with 80 registered in the US, primarily operated by Southwest Airlines. 

The incident has raised concerns about aging aircraft safety, with some experts suggesting that the tear occurred in an area previously deemed low-risk. The FAA has tightened standards for aging aircraft inspections in recent years, but the latest event may prompt even stricter regulations. 

National Transportation Safety Board (NTSB) investigators found additional cracks on the same seam where the tear occurred, and Southwest Airlines discovered similar issues on three other 737-300s, prompting the grounding of these aircraft. The damaged jet had undergone a routine inspection in 2010, but the cracks were not detected until the incident. 

Inspectors will use an electro-magnetic process to detect potentially hidden cracks, followed by more advanced techniques if necessary. Southwest Airlines has already inspected 64 of its 737-300s, finding fatigue cracks in three planes, and is awaiting further guidance from Boeing and the FAA. The airline has canceled hundreds of flights due to the voluntary grounding. 

The incident highlights the importance of regular inspections and maintenance for aging aircraft. The Southwest jet involved in the incident had completed over 39,000 takeoffs and landings, and the FAA does not currently require intensive inspections for this type of fatigue crack. The NTSB is investigating the incident, and the damaged aircraft is being transported to Washington, DC for further analysis. 

On the ill-fated flight, passengers reported hearing loud popping sounds and oxygen masks dropping from the ceiling. The crew successfully diverted the plane to a nearby airport, and fortunately, no passengers were seriously injured. The incident underscores the critical role of pilots and crew in responding to emergencies and the need for continued vigilance in ensuring the safety of commercial air travel.

The order covers aircraft built with a specific process in the '80s and '90s and those that have more than 30,000 flight cycles, ABC News reports.
