final_summary:
On September 20, 2016, Keith Lamont Scott, a 43-year-old black man, was fatally shot by a Charlotte-Mecklenburg Police Department (CMPD) officer, Brentley Vinson, in a parking lot outside an apartment complex in University City, North Carolina. The incident sparked widespread protests and calls for the release of police video footage, which was eventually released on September 24, 2016. According to the police, officers were attempting to serve an arrest warrant on an unrelated suspect when they noticed Scott rolling a marijuana "blunt" in his vehicle. Upon further observation, they saw Scott exit his vehicle, turn, and walk backward with his hands at his sides before four shots were heard, and he fell to the ground. The released footage includes a dashcam video from a police SUV and a bodycam video from one of the officers at the scene. However, neither video provides clear evidence of whether Scott was holding a gun at the time of the shooting. 

The police claimed that Scott was shot after he failed to comply with repeated verbal commands to drop the gun, and that his actions and movements were perceived as an imminent physical threat to themselves and others. However, Scott's family disputes this account, claiming that he was unarmed and did not pose a threat. His wife, Rakeyia Scott, recorded a cellphone video of the incident, which shows her pleading with officers not to shoot her husband, stating that he had a traumatic brain injury and was not a threat. The family's lawyers argue that the videos do not clearly show what, if anything, Scott had in his hands. 

The police department released photos of a handgun, ankle holster, and marijuana blunt they claim were in Scott's possession at the time of the incident. The gun was loaded, and Scott's DNA and fingerprints were found on it. However, the family believes that Scott may have been unarmed, and that the police are trying to justify the shooting. 

The release of the videos and photos sparked both praise and criticism. While some praised the police for providing transparency, others argued that the footage was incomplete and that the police were trying to manipulate public opinion. The incident has reignited debates about police brutality, racial profiling, and the use of force by law enforcement agencies. The North Carolina State Bureau of Investigation is leading the inquiry into the case, and the CMPD has stated that they will not charge any officers involved in the shooting. However, the family's lawyers have expressed skepticism about the investigation and have called for a federal probe. The incident has also led to increased scrutiny of the CMPD's policies and procedures regarding the use of force and the release of police video footage.

reference: – In a reversal of course, police in Charlotte on Saturday released bodycam and dashcam footage from the fatal shooting of Keith Scott. (See it here via the Washington Post.) In addition to the video, police also released a photo of the gun they say was spotted in Scott's vehicle. Some early descriptions: Charlotte Observer: The videos "do not show Keith Lamont Scott raising a weapon toward officers nor a gun in his hand." NBC News: "The dashcam video released shows Scott come out of a white SUV while police stand behind another vehicle with their weapons raised and command him to drop the gun. Scott eventually emerges from the SUV slowly and backs away. As he is backing up, four shots can be heard, and Scott can be seen falling to the ground." AP: "Scott can be seen in police dashboard camera video backing away from his SUV with his hands down, and it's unclear if there's anything in the man's hands. Four shots are heard, and he falls to the ground." ABC News: "The actual shooting is neither seen nor heard in the body cam footage.Officer Brentley Vinson, identified by police as the officer who shot Scott, cannot be seen firing his weapon in either video." Rakeyia Scott released her own video of her husband's shooting Friday.

final_summary:
Researchers at the University of California, Davis, and other institutions have challenged a long-standing stereotype in the gaming community that men are naturally better gamers than women. The study, led by Assistant Professor Cuihua "Cindy" Shen, analyzed data from over 9,000 players across two massively multiplayer online games, EverQuest II and Chevaliers' Romance III, to determine whether men and women progress at the same rate. 

To ensure accurate results, the researchers controlled for various factors that might influence gameplay, such as playing time, character choice, and guild membership. Once these variables were taken into account, the difference in progression rates between men and women disappeared, indicating that women advanced at least as fast as men in both games. 

Professor Shen's interest in this topic stems from her broader research on the connections between gaming and real-world behaviors, as well as her desire to address the persistent hostility towards women in gaming communities. Despite the growing number of female gamers, many communities remain unwelcoming to women, perpetuating stereotypes that they are not as skilled or committed to gaming as men. 

The study's findings have significant implications for the gaming world and beyond. Video games can serve as an important gateway to science, technology, engineering, and mathematics (STEM) fields, and women who participate in gaming are more likely to pursue STEM careers. However, the stereotype that women are inferior gamers may discourage them from participating in gaming, contributing to existing inequalities in STEM fields. 

To combat this stereotype, Professor Shen suggests promoting inclusive gaming environments and supporting female-friendly gaming communities, such as the PMS Clan, which has been shown to increase confidence and perceived gaming ability among its members. Game developers can also play a crucial role by creating games that are less hostile and more welcoming to female players, such as through systems like the Tribunal, which allows players to report and vote on instances of harassment. 

Ultimately, the study's findings aim to dispel the myth that women are inherently worse gamers than men, allowing female gamers to feel more confident and empowered in their gaming pursuits. By challenging this stereotype, researchers hope to break the self-fulfilling prophecy cycle that may be discouraging women from pursuing STEM careers.

history.draft:
Researchers at the University of California, Davis, and other institutions have challenged a long-standing stereotype in the gaming community that men are naturally better gamers than women. The study, led by Assistant Professor Cuihua "Cindy" Shen, analyzed data from over 9,000 players across two massively multiplayer online games, EverQuest II and Chevaliers' Romance III, to determine whether men and women progress at the same rate. 

To ensure accurate results, the researchers controlled for various factors that might influence gameplay, such as playing time, character choice, and guild membership. Once these variables were taken into account, the difference in progression rates between men and women disappeared, indicating that women advanced at least as fast as men in both games. 

Professor Shen's interest in this topic stems from her broader research on the connections between gaming and real-world behaviors, as well as her desire to address the persistent hostility towards women in gaming communities. Despite the growing number of female gamers, many communities remain unwelcoming to women, perpetuating stereotypes that they are not as skilled or committed to gaming as men. 

The study's findings have significant implications for the gaming world and beyond. Video games can serve as an important gateway to science, technology, engineering, and mathematics (STEM) fields, and women who participate in gaming are more likely to pursue STEM careers. However, the stereotype that women are inferior gamers may discourage them from participating in gaming, contributing to existing inequalities in STEM fields. 

To combat this stereotype, Professor Shen suggests promoting inclusive gaming environments and supporting female-friendly gaming communities, such as the PMS Clan, which has been shown to increase confidence and perceived gaming ability among its members. Game developers can also play a crucial role by creating games that are less hostile and more welcoming to female players, such as through systems like the Tribunal, which allows players to report and vote on instances of harassment. 

Ultimately, the study's findings aim to dispel the myth that women are inherently worse gamers than men, allowing female gamers to feel more confident and empowered in their gaming pursuits. By challenging this stereotype, researchers hope to break the self-fulfilling prophecy cycle that may be discouraging women from pursuing STEM careers.

reference: – The hostility toward women gamers that was laid bare in 2014 during the GamerGate controversy extends to the stereotype that female gamers are inferior to their male counterparts. So researchers at the University of California, Davis, decided to track thousands of players in two multiplayer online role-playing games and compare just how quickly the players advanced to higher levels. And as Cuihua “Cindy” Shen, an assistant professor of communication, reports in the Journal of Computer-Mediated Communication, when controlling for factors such as time devoted to playing, character choice, and membership in a player's guild, "The gender differences disappear." In fact, she goes on to write for the Conversation, "Women advanced at least as fast as men did." Shen says the findings confirm certain differences in style of play, such as that men tend to focus on competing and gaining in-game status while women tend to play more socially and help others. But in terms of the bottom line: advancing to a higher level, the difference was nil among the 11,000 gamers they analyzed playing Everquest II and Chevalier's Romance III. Aside from one study in 2009, Vocativ reports that this is the first to figure out how to go about testing gender differences in gaming performance outside of a lab. Why does it matter? Nearly half the gaming population is now female, yet they are represented at even lower numbers in STEM fields of science, technology, engineering, and math. "The stereotype that women are worse players at games could contribute to a self-fulfilling prophecy," Shen says. (Guys who are hostile to girl gamers tend to be poor performers.)

final_summary:
Leslie Gibson, a Republican candidate for Maine's 57th House of Representatives seat, faced intense backlash for his derogatory comments towards two Parkland, Florida school shooting survivors, Emma González and David Hogg. Gibson's remarks, which included referring to González as a "skinhead lesbian" and Hogg as a "bald-faced liar" in a series of tweets, sparked widespread condemnation from both Democrats and Republicans, including Maine State Representative Bruce Bickford and Senator Amy Volk.

In response to Gibson's attacks, Hogg publicly urged someone to run against him, asking if anyone in Maine wanted to challenge the unopposed candidate. Two individuals answered Hogg's call: Republican Thomas Martin Jr. and Democrat Eryn Gilchrist, both of whom filed paperwork to run for the seat. Gilchrist, a 28-year-old medical device company employee, stated that she was motivated to run due to her horror and embarrassment at the prospect of Gibson representing her in the legislature. Martin, a former state senator, cited Gibson's comments as the reason for his decision to enter the race, saying he couldn't remain silent after hearing Gibson's remarks.

Gibson attempted to apologize to González in a tweet, expressing regret for his previous comments. However, González chose not to respond. Following the controversy, Gibson deleted his personal Twitter account and set his campaign's Twitter account to private. On Friday, Gibson announced his withdrawal from the race, stating that it was the best decision for everyone involved. Despite the intense backlash he faced, Gibson maintained that he was leaving with his head held high. González responded to Gibson's decision by tweeting that it was what he deserved.

Gibson initially sparked outrage when he referred to González as a "skinhead lesbian" and Hogg as a "bald-faced liar" in a series of tweets. His comments were widely condemned by both Democrats and Republicans, including Maine State Representative Bruce Bickford and Senator Amy Volk.

In response to Gibson's attacks, Hogg publicly urged someone to run against him, tweeting "Hey freinds in Maine! Who wants to run against this hate loving politician he's is running UNOPPOSED RUN AGAINST HIM I don't care what party JUST DO IT." Two individuals answered Hogg's call: Republican Thomas Martin Jr. and Democrat Eryn Gilchrist, both of whom filed paperwork to run for the seat. 

Gilchrist, a 28-year-old medical device company employee, stated that she was motivated to run due to her horror and embarrassment at the prospect of Gibson representing her in the legislature. Martin, a former state senator, cited Gibson's comments as the reason for his decision to enter the race, saying "after those recent unfortunate comments, I couldn’t sit back."

Gibson attempted to apologize to González in a tweet, stating "Emma, my name is Les Gibson from Maine... I would like to extend to you my most sincere apology for how I addressed you. It was wrong and unacceptable." However, González did not respond. Following the controversy, Gibson deleted his personal Twitter account and set his campaign's Twitter account to private.

On Friday, Gibson announced his withdrawal from the race, stating "It’s the best thing for everybody." He claimed to be leaving with his head held high, despite the intense backlash he faced. González responded to Gibson's decision by tweeting "It’s what he deserves."

reference: – A Maine candidate's attack on an outspoken survivor of the Parkland school shooting angered so many people that he ended up having to drop out of a race in which he had been running unopposed. Leslie Gibson, a Republican who had been running for a state House seat in the 57th district, tweeted that Emma Gonzalez was a "skinhead lesbian" with nothing to say "unless you’re a frothing at the mouth moonbat." In another tweet from his now-deleted account, Gibson rejected Gonzalez's claim to be a survivor of the mass shooting, saying she had been in a "completely different part of the school," BuzzFeed reports. He also called David Hogg, another survivor, a "bald-faced liar," prompting Hogg to ask whether anybody would run against him, the Washington Post reports. After the tweets—which were denounced by several Republican state lawmakers—28-year-old Democrat Eryn Gilchrist announced her candidacy, saying she would be "horrified and embarrassed" to have Gibson represent her. Thomas Martin Jr., a Republican, also announced his candidacy, saying he represented "real Republican values," the Portland Press Herald reports. Gibson, who apologized to Gonzalez earlier in the week for the "wrong and unacceptable" way in which he addressed her, dropped out of the race Friday, saying he had made the decision after speaking to family members, friends, and Martin. "I am not walking away with my head hung low. I am walking away with my head held high," he said. (After the Parkland shooting, Gonzalez called out President Trump and the NRA.)

final_summary:
Henri Bendel, a historic luxury department store, closed its doors after 123 years in business. The decision to shutter the brand came as a result of declining sales, prompting its parent company, L Brands, to discontinue the brand's e-commerce platform and all 23 physical locations, including its iconic flagship store on New York City's Fifth Avenue, in January 2019. This strategic move aims to enhance company profitability and concentrate resources on more successful brands within the L Brands portfolio, such as Victoria's Secret and Bath & Body Works.

In a statement, L Brands Chairman and CEO Leslie Wexner acknowledged the impact of the closure on employees and loyal customers, stating that the decision was essential for the company's future growth. The Fifth Avenue store will remain operational until mid-January 2019 to facilitate holiday sales, though no plans have been revealed for the site's future use.

Established in 1895 by Henri Willis Bendel as a millinery shop in Greenwich Village, the brand evolved into a pioneering force in the American fashion industry. It was the first luxury retailer to occupy an upper Fifth Avenue address and hosted the nation's inaugural fashion show in 1913. Additionally, Bendel played a pivotal role in introducing Coco Chanel's designs to the US market and employed artist Andy Warhol as its in-house illustrator during the 1960s.

The closure of Bendel presents another challenge for L Brands, which has faced difficulties due to declining sales amidst intense retail competition. Despite generating $85 million in sales for L Brands in 2018, representing a small fraction of the company's total revenue of $12.6 billion, the brand's contribution to the overall business will be minimal.

Its parent company, L Brands, made the decision to shut down the brand's e-commerce platform and all 23 physical locations, including its iconic flagship store on New York City's Fifth Avenue, effective January 2019. This move comes as part of L Brands' efforts to boost profitability and focus on its more successful brands, such as Victoria's Secret and Bath & Body Works.

The closure of Bendel marks another challenge for L Brands, which has struggled with declining sales amidst intense retail competition. The company's stock price has plummeted 55% in 2018, ranking among the worst performers in the S&P 500 index. With annual revenues totaling $12.6 billion, Bendel contributed only around $85 million to L Brands' sales in 2018, indicating that the brand's closure will have a relatively minor financial impact on the parent company.

final_summary:
A 36-year-old woman developed an itchy skin lesion on her groin area two months after returning from her honeymoon in Belize. Initially, she visited her primary care doctor, who suspected the lesion might be an insect bite and prescribed a course of antibiotics, sulfamethoxazole. Despite completing the medication, the lesion persisted, though the redness had decreased.

Seeking a second opinion, the woman consulted Dr. Enrico Camporesi, a wound healing specialist at Tampa's Memorial Hospital. Upon examining the lesion, Dr. Camporesi noted that the surrounding skin felt hard, similar to having an egg or bean underneath, prompting him to consult a surgeon for further evaluation. The surgeon suspected that the lesion contained a small creature, and upon surgical removal, a pathologist confirmed the presence of a human botfly larva.

The human botfly larva, measuring approximately 5 millimeters in length, had created a hole in the woman's skin for breathing purposes and left behind a row of black spines. This condition is medically known as myiasis. Human botflies are uncommon in the United States but prevalent in tropical regions, particularly in Central and South America. Female botflies deposit their eggs onto other insects, such as mosquitoes or flies, which then transfer the eggs to humans during feeding. Inside the human skin, the eggs hatch into larvae, which feed on tissue and cause discomfort.

During this specific instance, the woman experienced intense itching without pain, and the lesion appeared as a small pimple. If left untreated, the larva could have matured into an adult fly, potentially enlarging the hole in the skin as it grew. Fortunately, the surgical removal of the larva resulted in complete resolution of the lesion within a week.

Interestingly, locals in Belize employ various home remedies to treat botfly infestations, including using petroleum jelly, bacon strips, nail polish, or plant extracts to suffocate the larvae, allowing them to emerge and be manually extracted. While this method can be effective, surgical intervention may be required in cases where the infestation is severe or the larva has caused considerable damage.

reference: – A tiny creature grew inside a Florida woman for two months after her honeymoon in Belize—and no, she wasn't pregnant. Weeks after returning home, the 36-year-old noticed an itchy spot on the left side of her groin and assumed she'd been bitten by an insect, reports Live Science. Thinking it was an infected spider bite, her doctor fruitlessly prescribed antibiotics. Dr. Enrico Camporesi of Tampa's Memorial Hospital next took a look, noticing a hole in the center of the wound, which felt hard, as though something were inside. A surgeon suspected it was a living thing, and turned out to be right. Cutting open the lesion, doctors found a tapered human botfly larva with rows of spines and hooks meant to keep it buried in the woman's skin for up to 128 days, reports the Miami Herald. Commonly found in Central America, a human botfly first deposits her eggs on an insect, like a mosquito, which carries them to human or animal hosts. Injected in skin via bites, the egg hatches into larva that eventually drops from the skin and continues developing, eventually reaching the size of a large bumblebee, per the Journal of Investigative Medicine High Impact Case Reports. Though the botfly didn't reach the pupa stage in this case, it wasn't necessary for its human host to go under the knife. Belizeans cover lesions—with petroleum jelly, nail polish, and even bacon strips, per the Herald—to suffocate larvae, which emerge for air and can be removed with tweezers. Still, the Florida patient was healed within a week. (Caterpillar larvae might prove useful.)

final_summary:
In the Peruvian Amazon, a tragic series of events unfolded, resulting in the lynching of a 41-year-old Canadian national, Sebastian Woodroffe, and the murder of an 81-year-old indigenous healer, Olivia Arévalo. Woodroffe, a resident of Vancouver Island, had traveled to Peru multiple times to experiment with ayahuasca, a hallucinogenic plant-based brew used in traditional healing ceremonies by the Shipibo-Konibo people. However, the exact circumstances surrounding his death remain unclear. 

Olivia Arévalo, a respected spiritual healer and rights activist within the Shipibo-Conibo community, was shot dead outside her home in the village of Victoria Gracia. Villagers suspected Woodroffe of her murder due to his association with her as a client, but there was no concrete evidence linking him to the crime. A video surfaced on social media showing a man, later identified as Woodroffe, being brutally lynched by a group of locals, who dragged him behind a thatched-roof house until he became motionless.

Peruvian authorities initially investigated Woodroffe as a potential witness in Arévalo's murder, but later clarified that he was not a suspect. Forensic analysis revealed that Woodroffe died from strangulation and suffered multiple blunt-force injuries. The investigation into his death remains ongoing, with various theories emerging, including the possibility that he was mistakenly targeted due to his association with Arévalo.

Arévalo's killing has sparked widespread outrage, particularly given the history of unsolved murders of indigenous leaders in the region. These killings often occur in areas with limited police presence, where communities may resort to self-punishment. The incident highlights the complexities surrounding the intersection of traditional practices, cultural sensitivities, and modern-day tourism in the Amazon region.

Ayahuasca, the substance Woodroffe sought to learn about, has gained popularity among tourists, but its use has also been linked to several fatalities, including a British gap-year student in Colombia and a New Zealander in Peru. The Shipibo people have used ayahuasca for centuries in their healing ceremonies, but its increasing commercialization has raised concerns about exploitation and safety.

As the investigation continues, questions surround the motivations behind Woodroffe's killing and whether he was unfairly targeted due to his association with Arévalo or if there were other factors at play. The incident serves as a poignant reminder of the need for greater understanding and respect for indigenous cultures in the face of growing external influences.

history.draft:
In the Peruvian Amazon, a tragic series of events unfolded, resulting in the lynching of a 41-year-old Canadian national, Sebastian Woodroffe, and the murder of an 81-year-old indigenous healer, Olivia Arévalo. Woodroffe, a resident of Vancouver Island, had traveled to Peru multiple times to experiment with ayahuasca, a hallucinogenic plant-based brew used in traditional healing ceremonies by the Shipibo-Konibo people. He had raised over $2,000 through crowdfunding to pursue an apprenticeship with a Shipibo plant healer, aiming to use his newfound knowledge to become an addiction counselor.

Olivia Arévalo, a respected spiritual healer and rights activist within the Shipibo-Conibo community, was shot dead outside her home in the village of Victoria Gracia. Villagers suspected Woodroffe of her murder due to his association with her as a client, despite having no concrete evidence linking him to the crime. A video surfaced on social media showing a man, later identified as Woodroffe, being brutally lynched by a group of locals, who dragged him behind a thatched-roof house until he became motionless.

Peruvian authorities initially investigated Woodroffe as the primary suspect in Arévalo's murder, but later clarified that he was not a suspect. Forensic analysis revealed that Woodroffe died from strangulation and suffered multiple blunt-force injuries. reference: – The death of a Canadian man is making headlines for its location, manner, and the murkiness around it. The body of Sebastian Woodroffe was found in a shallow grave Saturday in the Ucayali region of Peru's Amazon by police who began searching for the 41-year-old after a video was posted to social media that showed him being dragged on the ground with a rope around his neck until his body goes limp. One early theory, according to local media and a police officer who spoke with the Guardian, is that he was killed over the murder of Olivia Arévalo, an octogenarian shaman from the Shipibo-Konibo tribe who was shot dead Thursday. Woodroffe's body was buried a little more than half-a-mile from the site where she was killed, and he was thought to be one of her clients. But while one potential motive being investigated is that Arévalo's son owed Woodroffe money, another theory is that the same son owed money to a gang member who pulled the trigger. And the AP says authorities have "backed away" from reports that Woodroffe is even a suspect. The BBC reports Woodroffe had been in Peru previously to try the hallucinogen ayahuasca, and that he was looking to start a new career involving the use of "plant medicine" in drug addiction treatment. The Guardian cites a years-old Indiegogo campaign that helped fund a trip from his Vancouver Island home to Peru; on the campaign page he referenced a relative's alcohol addiction and wrote, "Traditional Detox Centres have a 5-8% success rate. Unacceptable." The CBC reports Woodroffe, who was apparently also killed Thursday, leaves behind a 9-year-old son.

final_summary:
The Obama administration announced that two additional states, Washington and Wisconsin, will be exempted from several key provisions of the No Child Left Behind (NCLB) education law, bringing the total number of states granted waivers to 26. This development raises questions about the future of the decade-old law, which has been widely criticized for its ambitious goal of having all students achieve proficient math and reading scores by 2014 – a target deemed unattainable by many educators.

The waivers, which were first introduced in February, allow states to opt out of certain aspects of the law in exchange for promising to implement alternative methods for evaluating student performance and teacher effectiveness. These alternatives may include incorporating non-test-based metrics, such as college enrollment and Advanced Placement exam participation, into teacher evaluations. Education Secretary Arne Duncan stated that the waivers are a temporary solution until Congress passes a revised version of the Elementary and Secondary Education Act, the formal name of the NCLB law.

Critics argue that the waivers do little to address the fundamental issues with the NCLB law, which has been criticized for its narrow focus on standardized testing and its tendency to label schools as "failing." Some experts believe that the waivers merely perpetuate the same problems, as states continue to rely heavily on test scores to evaluate student and teacher performance. Others see the waivers as a necessary step towards reform, allowing states to experiment with innovative approaches to education.

Education officials in states that have received waivers express relief from the pressure of meeting the 2014 deadline, citing concerns that the original goal was unrealistic. Instead of labeling all struggling schools as failing, the waivers direct states to focus on the bottom 5% of low-performing schools, providing targeted support to those that need it most. However, House Republicans have criticized the Obama administration's use of waivers as an end-run around Congress, accusing the administration of imposing its own policy preferences on states.

As the debate over the future of NCLB continues, the Obama administration remains committed to finding a bipartisan solution to revise the law. Meanwhile, the waivers have created a patchwork of different approaches to education reform across the country, with some states embracing innovative strategies and others continuing to rely on traditional methods.

reference: – It's a milestone for No Child Left Behind, but not in a good way: More than half of the nation is now exempt. The White House issued waivers today to the 25th and 26th states, and another 10 are in the works, reports the New York Times. The exemptions come ahead of a 2014 deadline requiring all students to be proficient in math and reading. They also come close to making the hallmark of George W. Bush's education reform "essentially nullified," says the Times. Education chief Arne Duncan says the waivers are necessary while the administration works with Congress to rewrite the law, which critics say is too focused on test results, notes AP. "A strong, bipartisan reauthorization of the Elementary and Secondary Education Act remains the best path forward in education reform, but as 26 states have now demonstrated, our kids can't wait any longer for Congress to act," said Duncan in a statement. A school superintendent in Maryland says the waivers don't do much to get away from standardized tests, however. “I have a lot of respect for Arne Duncan, but it’s just sort of moving around the chairs on the Titanic.”

final_summary:
Typhoon Haiyan, one of the most powerful storms ever recorded on land, ravaged the Philippines on November 8, causing widespread destruction and claiming thousands of lives. The official death toll stood at 2,357, but aid workers feared it would continue to rise, with the United Nations estimating that over 11 million people were affected by the storm. The disaster-stricken areas, particularly Tacloban and Guiuan, suffered severe impacts, with extensive destruction of homes, infrastructure, and crops.

As the international community responded to the crisis, the UK government dispatched the aircraft carrier HMS Illustrious, along with a team of medical experts and a Royal Air Force transport aircraft, to assist in the relief efforts. The UK had already committed over £20 million in aid, with the Disasters Emergency Committee (DEC) raising £23 million in its first 48 hours. The US also pledged significant support, with the USS George Washington, an aircraft carrier carrying 5,000 crew, expanding search-and-rescue operations and providing a platform for helicopters to move supplies.

However, despite the influx of aid, the distribution process was hindered by logistical challenges, including clogged roads, broken bridges, and a non-functional airport in Tacloban. The city's residents were forced to resort to desperate measures, such as breaking into homes of the deceased and digging up underground pipes to extract water. Looting and lawlessness became widespread, prompting the Philippine security forces to impose a nighttime curfew and deploy reinforcements.

As the situation in Tacloban remained dire, with bodies lining the streets and a shortage of medical supplies, aid workers expressed concerns about the risk of waterborne diseases, including cholera, due to the lack of access to clean water. The European Commission's humanitarian aid directorate acknowledged the logistical challenges, stating that the major hurdle was distributing aid to numerous communities amidst the devastation.

In the midst of the chaos, there were glimmers of hope and resilience, as residents came together to share survival stories and find ways to cope with the trauma. The international community continued to mobilize resources, with multiple countries pledging aid, including the US, UK, China, Japan, and others. As the relief efforts accelerated, the focus shifted towards addressing the pressing needs of the affected communities, including providing shelter, food, and medical attention to those in dire need.

reference: – Help is finally beginning to reach hundreds of thousands of people struggling to survive in the aftermath of Typhoon Haiyan. The USS George Washington and two US cruisers have arrived in the Philippines and will distribute food, water, and medicine, as well as assist search and rescue operations in what the top US commander in the Philippines tells the BBC will be an American aid effort on an unprecedented scale. US planes are bringing in supplies and a medical ship is on the way; Britain is also sending an aircraft carrier, notes the BBC. In other developments: Supplies from the US and other countries are arriving, but some areas are still in desperate need of relief and "the major challenge is logistics," a European Commission spokesman tells the LA Times. "With all this aid arriving and at the same time, the various Philippine authorities—military, civilian structures, the Philippine Red Cross—trying to distribute aid to so many communities ... obviously there are bottlenecks." Lawlessness in the devastated city of Tacloban is also slowing the distribution of aid, the Guardian finds. A Red Cross convoy was allegedly hijacked by armed men and there are rumors that inmates who escaped during the storm have been ambushing people carrying supplies. Communist rebels have declared a ceasefire in disaster areas, but troops say there has been at least one attack. Some 2,357 people have been confirmed dead, but the toll is expected to rise significantly as remote areas hit hard by the storm are reached. In Tacloban, where bodies are being trucked to mass graves, the city administrator says he is sticking by his original estimate of 10,000 dead in the city alone. In a smaller city south of Tacloban, the devastation is even worse and a 16-member medical team from California is laboring in extreme conditions to help survivors, NBC finds. The volunteer surgeons—working by flashlight in Tanawan's ruined town hall—believe the region will need help for months. "I don’t know when or how we are going to be able to leave," one doctor says. "This is just the beginning of a wave of misery." USA Today has more on that front: The few doctors in Manila are already overwhelmed with hundreds of patients—and those are mostly minor injuries. They're soon expecting to see big problems like pneumonia, diarrhea, infections, and dehydration. And the New York Times looks at a hospital in Tacloban that has no power, where victims lay waiting for care. Food, water, and medical supplies are short; one patient interviewed by the Times says he has not received painkillers, antibiotics, or even an antiseptic for his wounds.

final_summary:
A series of tragic events shook the normally tranquil city of Scottsdale, Arizona, leaving behind a trail of loss and heartbreak. The killings began on Thursday, June 7, 2018, with the fatal shooting of Dr. Steven Pitt, a renowned forensic psychiatrist who had previously evaluated and testified against the suspect, Dwight Lamon Jones, in a 2010 divorce case. This event was followed by the deaths of two paralegals, Veleria Sharp, 48, and Laura Anderson, 49, who worked at the law firm representing Jones' ex-wife, Connie Jones. Authorities initially struggled to determine the motive behind these killings, but as the investigation progressed, they uncovered a complex web of connections to Jones' bitter divorce proceedings.

On Friday, June 8, 2018, a 72-year-old marriage counselor, Marshall Levine, was found dead in his office, bringing the total number of fatalities to five. Further investigation revealed that the same gun was used in all five killings, leading investigators to suspect a single perpetrator. As the investigation unfolded, authorities received numerous tips, but it was a call from Jones' ex-husband, a retired police detective, that proved instrumental in cracking the case. The detective had noticed a connection between the crime scenes and informed the police on Saturday evening, June 9, 2018.

The following morning, police tracked Jones to an Extended Stay America hotel in Scottsdale, where he engaged in a standoff with officers before ultimately taking his own life. The discovery of Jones' body marked the end of a 96-hour period of intense activity that resulted in six deaths, including two unidentified individuals found in a home in Fountain Hills. An analysis of shell casings and DNA evidence linked Jones to the crimes, while surveillance footage placed him near the law firm where the paralegals were killed. reference: – A suspect who killed himself as police closed in Monday is now believed to have killed six people in the Phoenix area, police say—and at least four of them were linked to his bitter 2010 divorce. Police believe 56-year-old Dwight Lamon Jones, who shot himself at a Scottsdale motel early Monday, killed all six people in around 96 hours starting early Thursday, the New York Times reports. Police say Jones started the spree when he killed prominent psychiatrist Steven Pitt, whom he'd been required to see as part of divorce proceedings. On Friday, he allegedly killed Veleria Sharp and Laura Anderson, two paralegals at the firm his wife used during the divorce. Hours later Jones allegedly killed Marshall Levine, a marriage counselor who used the same office as a counselor Jones' son saw during the divorce. Police believe Jones also killed Mary Simmons, 70, and Bryon Thomas, 72, in Fountain Hills, around 15 miles away from the other shootings, but their connection to him is still unclear. Jones left around 10 hours of YouTube video in which he outlines an alleged conspiracy to steal custody of his son, who's now 21 years old, the Arizona Republic reports. Ex-wife Connie Jones says Jones was a "very emotionally disturbed person" and she divorced him after he hit her and threatened to kill her. She was granted sole custody of their son. The AP reports that Jones says her new husband, a retired police detective, was the one who made the connection between the divorce and the shootings and told police of his suspicions.

final_summary:
On July 20, 2018, Dr. Mark Hausknecht, a renowned cardiologist who treated former President George H.W. Bush, was fatally shot while riding his bicycle to work at Houston Methodist Hospital in the Texas Medical Center. The shooting occurred during rush hour, with the gunman passing Hausknecht from behind before turning around and firing at least twice. The doctor was struck and fell to the ground, dying shortly thereafter.

Following the shooting, a massive investigation was launched, with police reviewing surveillance footage and canvassing the area for witnesses. A $5,000 reward was offered by Crime Stoppers, but the case remained unsolved for nearly two weeks. 

However, on August 1, 2018, a breakthrough came when a tip led police to identify Joseph James Pappas, a 62-year-old man with a history of working in law enforcement, as the prime suspect. Pappas' motive for the crime was revealed to be a 20-year-old grudge stemming from his mother's death during surgery performed by Dr. Hausknecht in 1998. Investigators believed that Pappas had planned and executed the attack with skill and precision.

Police Chief Art Acevedo warned that Pappas should be considered armed, dangerous, and potentially suicidal, and that he was likely using his 10-speed bicycle to get around. A search of Pappas' home yielded evidence linking him to the crime, although the exact nature of the evidence was not disclosed.

Pappas had a long history of working in law enforcement, serving as a reserve officer with Harris County Constable Precincts 2 and 7 from 1983 to 2013. He also maintained an active real estate license and had recently sold various firearms and tactical gear online using a phone number linked to his real estate company.

Before his disappearance, Pappas had sent a text message to someone indicating that he intended to take his own life. However, the exact timing of his disappearance is unclear. 

Dr. Hausknecht was remembered as a humble and generous man who was beloved by his patients and colleagues. He was an avid cyclist who rode his bike to work daily, and his death sent shockwaves throughout the medical community. The case highlights the complexities of grudge-driven violence and its devastating consequences.

reference: – A man suspected of killing one of former President George HW Bush's doctors may have been seeking revenge for his mother, who died on the doctor's operating table more than 20 years ago, authorities said Wednesday. Joseph James Pappas, 62, should be considered armed, dangerous, and possibly suicidal, Houston police Chief Art Acevedo said at a news conference Wednesday afternoon. "There was a lot of planning that went into this," Acevedo said of the July 20 attack on Dr. Mark Hausknecht, adding that it appears a "20-year-old grudge" led to the attack, per CNN. Hausknecht was gunned down while riding his bike to work at Houston Methodist Hospital, which is part of the busy Texas Medical Center. Authorities say the shooter rode past Hausknecht before turning around and firing. A tip on Tuesday led police to suspect Pappas, the chief said, though he didn't elaborate as to the nature of the tip. Acevedo said Pappas hadn't been seen in 36 to 48 hours, the AP reports. He said the last anyone had heard from Pappas was in a Tuesday morning text message in which Pappas wrote that he was going to kill himself. Acevedo said police searched his home at 4am Wednesday and found evidence that ties Pappas to the killing, but he declined to elaborate as to the nature of the evidence. The chief said Pappas, who was a peace officer in Harris County for three decades per the Houston Chronicle, is white and very fit, and that he's likely getting around on his 10-speed bicycle, which he rides "extensively and almost exclusively." Friends and family have described Hausknecht as a humble and generous man who was adored by his patients, volunteered in his community and cared about the environment. He was also an avid cyclist and rode his bike to work each morning, as he lived less than 2 miles from his office.

final_summary:
Natalie Wood's sister, Lana Wood, has made a startling claim against her former brother-in-law, actor Robert Wagner, alleging that he plotted to blacklist her from Hollywood following the actress's mysterious death in 1981. However, according to Lana, the reason behind the supposed blacklist was not directly related to Natalie's death, but rather due to her own attempts to pursue a career in production and acting. Lana stated that she received a call from Hollywood executive Rowland Perkins, who informed her that she had been blacklisted by Wagner. As a result, Lana faced difficulties finding work in the industry and took on various odd jobs, including selling furniture and working at a doll shop.

The accusation comes amid renewed attention surrounding Natalie's death, which occurred while she was on a yacht with Wagner and actor Christopher Walken off the coast of Santa Catalina Island. Initially, the cause of death was determined to be accidental drowning, but in 2012, the Los Angeles Sheriff's Department revised the ruling to include "undetermined factors." 

Dennis Davern, the former captain of the yacht, has also spoken out, claiming that he believes Wagner was responsible for Natalie's death. Davern alleges that he witnessed a heated argument between the couple and that Wagner instructed him to lie to police about the circumstances surrounding Natalie's disappearance. Lana Wood has echoed these sentiments, stating that Wagner has "something to hide" and accusing him of potentially knocking her sister out and throwing her into the water.

Wagner has consistently denied any involvement in Natalie's death and has refused to cooperate with investigators. His representative, Alan Nierob, has condemned Davern and Lana Wood for their allegations, labeling them "despicable human beings" who are capitalizing on Natalie's death for personal gain. The Los Angeles Sheriff's Department has reopened the investigation multiple times over the years, designating Wagner as a person of interest in the case.

reference: – For nearly 40 years, the death of Natalie Wood has remained a mystery. First the 43-year-old actress was ruled to have died of accidental drowning after disappearing from a yacht she was on with husband Robert Wagner and actor Christopher Walken. Then, in 2012, that ruling shifted to drowning plus "other undetermined factors," with LA County Sheriff's Department investigators suspicious there was more to the case. Wood's sister, Lana Wood, has contended for years that Wagner was responsible for her sibling's death, and this week, Dr. Phil McGraw added oxygen to that theory by having Lana Wood and the yacht's captain, Dennis Davern, on his show for two days of interviews on the subject, USA Today reports. "You believe Robert Wagner murdered Natalie Wood?" McGraw asked Davern, who'd initially stuck with the accidental drowning story. "Yes, I really do," Davern answered. He says he heard Wood and Wagner fighting, and when the noise ceased, he went up on the deck to find Wagner standing there solo; Davern says Wagner insisted they say Wood likely slipped and fell into the water. Lana Wood sticks by that, too. "Are you suggesting that [Wagner] knocked her out and threw her in the water?" McGraw asked. "Something like that, absolutely," she answered. Per Us Weekly, Lana Wood also recently appeared on a podcast in which she says that, shortly after her sister's death, Hollywood exec Rowland Perkins informed her Wagner was keeping her out of Tinseltown. "He said, 'Stop trying to get a job in production or acting. You've been blacklisted by Robert Wagner," Lana Wood said. A rep for Wagner, now 88, tells USA Today that Davern and Lana Wood are "despicable human beings" and that "they should be ashamed of themselves."

final_summary:
"If I Stay", a film adaptation of Gayle Forman's novel, tells the story of Mia Hall, a 17-year-old cellist who survives a devastating car crash that leaves her in a coma. From her hospital bed, Mia observes her surroundings and reflects on her life before the accident, grappling with the possibility of staying alive. The film, directed by R.J. Cutler, employs a similar formula to "The Fault in Our Stars", focusing on the romance between Mia and her boyfriend Adam, played by Jamie Blackley, amidst a picturesque backdrop.

Mia's family, portrayed by Mireille Enos and Joshua Leonard, appears to be a loving and supportive unit, but their overly perfect portrayal raises suspicions about the accuracy of Mia's memories. The narrative struggles to balance the emotional intensity of Mia's situation with the fantastical elements of her out-of-body excursions, which sometimes feel contrived.

Despite its flaws, the film effectively conveys the importance of friendship, family, and adolescent love, particularly in the performances of Jamie Blackley and Stacy Keach. However, the production as a whole feels somewhat clumsy, and lead actress Chloë Grace Moretz could benefit from more nuanced direction. Ultimately, Mia's decision to stay or go remains ambiguous, reflecting her growth into a more empowered individual.

Mia's family, portrayed by Mireille Enos and Joshua Leonard, appears to be a loving and supportive unit, but their overly perfect portrayal raises suspicions about the accuracy of Mia's memories. The narrative struggles to balance the emotional intensity of Mia's situation with the fantastical elements of her out-of-body excursions, which sometimes feel contrived.

Despite its flaws, the film effectively conveys the importance of friendship, family, and adolescent love, particularly in the performances of Jamie Blackley and Stacy Keach. However, the production as a whole feels somewhat clumsy, and lead actress Chloë Grace Moretz could benefit from more nuanced direction. Ultimately, Mia's decision to stay or go remains ambiguous, reflecting her growth into a more empowered individual.

reference: – If I Stay tells the story of a teen girl with a big future who's nearly killed in a car crash. Informed by out-of-body experiences, she must decide whether to stick around on Earth or head to Heaven. It'll make you cry, but it doesn't offer much otherwise, critics say. (Audiences, however, seem to disagree: 80% like it at Rotten Tomatoes, twice the figure for critics). Some examples: It's up to audiences "whether the movie's many moments of genuine sweetness and affecting tenderness can survive the persistent silliness of Mia's out-of-body excursions," writes Joe Morgenstern at the Wall Street Journal. "At its best, If I Stay evokes the primacy of friendship, the warmth of family … and the urgency of adolescent love. But the production as a whole is awfully clumsy." "Full disclosure: I went through half a pack of Kleenex watching If I Stay," writes Elizabeth Weitzman in the New York Daily News. "But know this: Those tears are no more honestly earned than if director RJ Cutler had merely been chopping onions in front of me. … The movie is designed not to explore the experience of illness, or first love, or adolescence, but merely to make us swoon, sigh, and sob." The movie "hews largely to the same formula as The Fault in Our Stars," another book-based film dealing with teens facing death, notes Ann Hornaday in the Washington Post. But If I Stay offers "the perfect bookend to a tear-stained summer": "Even at its most wrenchingly painful, the film readily delivers generous dollops of pleasure." In the Los Angeles Times, Kenneth Turan calls the film "a flat-out, all-in fantasy romance, an unashamed tear-jerker that is unafraid of glossy emotions." Sure, "every moment in If I Stay is not all that it might be"—but when its leads "are looking into each other's eyes, you are not going to care."

final_summary:
Notothenioid fish, a unique group of fish inhabiting the icy waters surrounding Antarctica, have adapted to survive in extremely cold temperatures through the production of antifreeze proteins (AFPs). These proteins function by binding to ice crystals and preventing their growth, thus safeguarding the fish from freezing. However, a recent study has revealed a counterintuitive aspect of this adaptation – the AFPs also hinder the melting of ice crystals within the fish's body.

Researchers led by Paul Cziko, a doctoral student at the University of Oregon, discovered this unforeseen characteristic of AFPs following experiments conducted in the laboratory and analysis of temperature data gathered over 11 years in McMurdo Sound, Antarctica. Their findings indicate that the ice crystals in the fish's bodies failed to melt at their predicted melting point of approximately 30 degrees Fahrenheit, remaining intact even when the temperature increased by 1.8 degrees Fahrenheit.

The AFPs accomplish this by modifying the surface of the ice crystals, rendering it challenging for them to expand or contract. This phenomenon, referred to as "superheating," represents a previously undocumented occurrence in nature. Historically, scientists have concentrated on the protective effects of AFPs against freezing, overlooking the potential risks linked to their anti-melting properties.

Consequently, notothenioids might accumulate ice crystals in their bodies throughout their lives, predominantly in their spleens, where the highest concentrations were detected. Although the long-term implications of this accumulation remain unclear, researchers hypothesize that it may pose a threat to the fish's well-being, potentially resulting in a "life-threatening traffic jam" in their tiny capillaries.

The study illustrates the intricate relationship between adaptations and their unintended consequences, demonstrating that each evolutionary innovation comes with its own set of drawbacks. According to Cziko, "evolution is a step-by-step process, where progress is often offset by setbacks." This research offers valuable insights into the complex interactions between species and their environments, emphasizing the significance of considering the entire spectrum of effects arising from evolutionary adaptations.

reference: – How do the fish that thrive in the waters around Antarctica prevent their blood from turning to ice? Turns out at least some of them don't. Scientists have long known that the group of fish species known as notothenioids have an antifreeze protein in their blood that prevents them from being frozen to death, but new research has revealed that the same protein keeps ice crystals in their veins, apparently permanently, UPI reports. The crystals melted at a few degrees above freezing, but the fish may spend their whole lives without encountering temperatures that warm, according to research published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences. The antifreeze proteins that prevented the blood from completely freezing also bound itself to the ice crystals, keeping them from getting smaller, the scientists say. Lead researcher Paul Cziko tells the Christian Science Monitor that it's not clear whether the fish have evolved some way of dealing with the ice crystals in their bodies, or if the ice ends up killing them in the long term. "This shows that with every good evolutionary innovation, some potentially bad or unintended consequences come along with that," he says. "Evolution is a stepwise process. You take two steps forward, and take one step back." (Researchers recently solved the decades-old mystery of a quacking noise detected in Antarctic waters.)

final_summary:
A local oceanographic consulting company, Roffer's Ocean Fishing Forecasting Service (ROFFS), is utilizing its expertise in analyzing currents and pinpointing convergence zones to aid in the search for two missing teenage boys, Austin Stephanos and Perry Cohen, who disappeared while fishing off the coast of Florida. Founded by Dr. Mitch Roffer, a specialist in fisheries oceanography, ROFFS provides critical data to recreational and commercial fishermen.

According to Dr. Roffer, even small variations in current speed can significantly impact the trajectory of objects drifting in the ocean, especially in areas like the Gulf Stream. A quarter-mile-per-hour difference in current speed over a period of ten hours can result in a separation of 2-3 miles, escalating to 15-20 miles, and eventually 30 miles. Moreover, if an object enters the core of the Gulf Stream, its movement doubles, leading to a substantial discrepancy in estimating the location of the missing individuals.

Dr. Roffer analyzed satellite imagery to predict the potential drift path of the boys' 19-foot white single-engine boat after it was abandoned on the western side of the Gulf Stream. His findings suggest that the boys could have drifted towards a region extending approximately 30 miles seaward from Cape Canaveral, stretching northwards. To facilitate the search efforts, Dr. Roffer shared his analysis on social media platforms, including Facebook and Twitter, to assist volunteer rescuers.

The families of the missing teenagers have been actively seeking public assistance in locating any signs of their sons. In a plea for help, they urged residents along the coastline from Palm Beach to Georgia to scour the shores for any debris or clues that might lead to their safe recovery. The Coast Guard had previously located the boys' abandoned boat, roughly 67 miles off the coast of Daytona Beach, but there was no indication of the teens being on board.

As the search continues, experts like Dr. Roffer and experienced mariners highlight the unforgiving nature of the ocean, emphasizing the significance of precise knowledge of ocean currents and conditions in such situations. Their warnings serve as a stark reminder of the risks associated with venturing into the open waters, regardless of one's experience or background.

reference: – The families of Perry Cohen and Austin Stephanos are living a nightmare—the 14-year-old boys are missing at sea and their capsized boat was found on Sunday—but they believe the teens have the skills to survive. As the search off the coast of Florida continues, family members say the boys may have crafted a flotation device using items including life jackets, an engine cover, and a cooler that were missing from their single-engine fishing boat, USA Today reports. Relatives and neighbors say the boys, like others in their Palm Beach County neighborhood, are experienced sailors and have been around boats their entire lives, reports the Palm Beach Post. A Coast Guard spokesman says the search is continuing "aggressively" and the water is warm enough that the teens, who were last seen on Friday, could survive for four or five days, the Post reports. Because of currents, search efforts are focusing on the area north of where the boat was found near Daytona Beach, though they could be hard to spot, the spokesman says. "When a person is in the water, you're basically looking for the chest up, so it's a relatively small object you're looking for," he says. "It's a challenging environment." The families have urged people from Palm Beach to the coast of Georgia to look for clues that might have washed up, especially the Yamaha engine cover and YETI cooler, NBC News reports.

final_summary:
On Sunday, a harrowing incident unfolded near Grande Prairie, Alberta, where 10 horses broke through the ice covering a slough, prompting a massive rescue effort by local firefighters and a search and rescue team. The horses, which were described as wild horses used in rodeos, had wandered onto the ice before falling through. According to Regional Fire Chief Trevor Grant, the warmer temperatures had caused the ice to become increasingly unstable, rendering it hazardous for the animals.

A total of 35 regional firefighters and search and rescue personnel responded to the distress call, which came in at approximately 4:15 pm, 65 kilometers west of Grande Prairie. The rescue operation presented numerous challenges, including the thin ice, cold water, and the unpredictable nature of the wild horses. 

Using their specialized training, the responders employed chainsaws to cut pathways to the shore, while others utilized slings and boards to carefully extract the horses from the icy waters. Unfortunately, despite their best efforts, three horses did not survive the ordeal, with two dying from exhaustion and one succumbing to injuries inflicted by the panicked animals. Seven horses were successfully rescued and received treatment from a veterinarian; their prognosis is positive.

Regional Fire Chief Trevor Grant commended the bravery and expertise of his team, acknowledging the value of their training in large animal rescue, which has developed over recent years in response to similar incidents. Grant emphasized the importance of landowners exercising caution regarding their animals' access to sloughs during this time of year, as the rapidly changing weather conditions can quickly make previously stable ice hazardous.

history.draft:
On Sunday, a harrowing incident unfolded near Grande Prairie, Alberta, where 10 horses broke through the ice covering a slough, prompting a massive rescue effort by local firefighters and a search and rescue team. The horses, described as wild bronco horses used in rodeos, had wandered onto the ice before succumbing to its fragile surface. According to Regional Fire Chief Trevor Grant, the warmer temperatures had significantly weakened the ice, making it prone to collapse under the weight of the animals.

A total of 35 regional firefighters and search and rescue personnel responded to the distress call, which came in at approximately 4:15 pm, 65 kilometers west of Grande Prairie. The rescue operation was particularly complex due to the thin ice, cold water, and the unpredictable nature of the wild horses. 

Using their specialized training, the responders employed chainsaws to cut pathways to the shore, while others utilized slings and boards to carefully extract the horses from the icy waters. Despite the challenges, all 10 horses were successfully removed from the slough by 6 pm. However, tragically, three horses did not survive the ordeal, with two dying from exhaustion and one succumbing to injuries inflicted by the panicked animals.

The remaining seven horses, who were treated by a veterinarian, are expected to recover fully. Regional Fire Chief Trevor Grant praised the efforts of his team, highlighting the importance of their training in large animal rescue, which has evolved over the past few years in response to similar incidents. Grant emphasized the need for landowners to exercise caution regarding their animals' access to sloughs during this time of year, as the rapidly changing weather conditions can render previously stable ice hazardous within hours.

Grant's warning serves as a poignant reminder of the risks associated with the unpredictable nature of Alberta's winter landscapes, where the line between safety and danger can shift dramatically in a short span of time.

reference: – Dozens of firefighters and rescue crew risked their own safety to save 10 horses that fell through the ice Sunday in Canada—and it was all caught on video, CBC reports. After the horses fell through the thinning ice in northern Alberta, rescuers had to use chainsaws to cut a path back to the shore before using boards and slings to get the horses to dry land. Rescuers were at risk not only from the cold temperatures and thin ice, but from the frantic animals, which were "wild broncing" horses used in rodeos and not domesticated, according to CTV News. All 10 horses were removed from the water after two hours, though three later died. "To rescue seven was a great feat, especially when having such a large number in the water," a local fire chief tells CBC.

final_summary:
Wade Michael Page, the gunman responsible for the mass-shooting at the Sikh temple in Oak Creek, Wisconsin, had a complex and troubled history. His family described him as a "precious child" who struggled to cope with the loss of his mother to lupus when he was 13 years old. However, details about his childhood and upbringing remain unclear. 

Page enlisted in the US Army in 1992 and served for approximately six and a half years, primarily as a repairman for the Hawk surface-to-air missile system. His military record indicates that he was demoted from sergeant to specialist and received a general discharge, rather than an honorable one, due to unspecified reasons. During his time in the Army, Page was exposed to psychological operations, studying ways to deceive enemies and influence foreign populations. He also completed the Army Basic Airborne Course and earned a parachutist badge.

After leaving the Army in 1998, Page's behavior became increasingly concerning. He developed a fascination with white supremacy ideology, referring to non-white individuals as "dirt people." His former comrade, Fred Allen Lucas, recalled that Page expressed these views openly, criticizing Lucas for dating Latina women and labeling him a "race-traitor." Page's tattoos, including one referencing the "14 words" of white supremacist doctrine, further suggest his involvement in extremist groups.

In the early 2000s, Page became involved in the white-power music scene, forming two skinhead bands – End Apathy and Definite Hate. He played at a racist music festival in Baltimore in 2010 and was known to wear a stars-and-bars strap on his guitar. Page's involvement in these groups indicates a deep commitment to white supremacist ideologies.

Despite his troubled past, Page maintained a relatively low public profile until recent years. He worked as a truck driver and later as a parts coordinator at a Harley-Davidson dealership in North Carolina, but was fired from both jobs due to conflicts with colleagues. In 2010, he lost his home to foreclosure and relocated to Wisconsin, where he continued to work as a truck driver before losing his job.

Neighbors in South Milwaukee described Page as a gruff loner who occasionally played loud music and spent weekends touring with his band. His behavior became increasingly erratic, with neighbors reporting that he rarely interacted with them and seemed to spend most of his time alone. Psychiatrist John Liebert noted that Page's past behavior exhibited warning signs of potential trouble, particularly his sudden departure from the Army and his involvement in extremist groups. Liebert speculated that Page may have been struggling with internal demons, leading to the tragic events at the Sikh temple. The FBI has deemed the attack an act of domestic terrorism, citing Page's white supremacist ideology as a motivating factor.

After a brief stint in South Milwaukee, Page moved to Cudahy, where he resided in a small apartment. His behavior became increasingly erratic, with neighbors reporting that he rarely interacted with them and seemed to spend most of his time alone.

Psychiatrist John Liebert noted that Page's past behavior exhibited warning signs of potential trouble, particularly his sudden departure from the Army and his involvement in extremist groups. Liebert speculated that Page may have been mentally ill and struggling with internal demons, leading to the tragic events at the Sikh temple. The FBI has deemed the attack an act of domestic terrorism, citing Page's white supremacist ideology as a motivating factor.

history.draft:
Wade Michael Page, the gunman responsible for the mass-shooting at the Sikh temple in Oak Creek, Wisconsin, had a complex and troubled history. His family described him as a "precious child" who struggled to cope with the loss of his mother to lupus when he was 13 years old. However, details about his childhood and upbringing remain unclear. 

Page enlisted in the US Army in 1992 and served for approximately six and a half years, primarily as a repairman for the Hawk surface-to-air missile system. His military record indicates that he was demoted from sergeant to specialist and received a general discharge, rather than an honorable one, due to unspecified reasons. During his time in the Army, Page was exposed to psychological operations, studying ways to deceive enemies and influence foreign populations. He also completed the Army Basic Airborne Course and earned a parachutist badge.

After leaving the Army in 1998, Page's behavior became increasingly concerning. He developed a fascination with white supremacy ideology, referring to non-white individuals as "dirt people." His former comrade, Fred Allen Lucas, recalled that Page expressed these views openly, criticizing Lucas for dating Latina women and labeling him a "race-traitor." Page's tattoos, including one referencing the "14 words" of white supremacist doctrine, further suggest his involvement in extremist groups.

In the early 2000s, Page became involved in the white-power music scene, forming two skinhead bands – End Apathy and Definite Hate. He played at a racist music festival in Baltimore in 2010 and was known to wear a stars-and-bars strap on his guitar. Page's involvement in these groups indicates a deep commitment to white supremacist ideologies.

Despite his troubled past, Page maintained a relatively low public profile until recent years. He worked as a truck driver and later as a parts coordinator at a Harley-Davidson dealership in North Carolina, but was fired from both jobs due to conflicts with colleagues. In 2010, he lost his home to foreclosure and relocated to Wisconsin, where he continued to work as a truck driver before losing his job.

Neighbors in South Milwaukee described Page as a gruff loner who occasionally played loud music and spent weekends touring with his band. His behavior became increasingly erratic, with neighbors reporting that he rarely interacted with them and seemed to spend most of his time alone. Psychiatrist John Liebert noted that Page's past behavior exhibited warning signs of potential trouble, particularly his sudden departure from the Army and his involvement in extremist groups. Liebert speculated that Page may have been struggling with internal demons, leading to the tragic events at the Sikh temple. The FBI has deemed the attack an act of domestic terrorism, citing Page's white supremacist ideology as a motivating factor.

reference: – More is emerging on suspected Sikh temple gunman Wade Michael Page: The white supremacist and former Army soldier was demoted in rank and discharged in 1998, the LA Times reports. A military official didn't say why Page received a general discharge rather than an honorable one after 6 1/2 years of service. But a dishonorable discharge would have stopped him from buying the 9mm handgun he purchased July 28, picked up July 30, and used days later in the shooting, the Milwaukee Journal Sentinel reports. Among other emerging details: Lt. Brian Murphy, 51, was the first officer on the scene. He came upon a victim in the temple parking lot and was ambushed by Page, who shot him eight or nine times. When other officers arrived, Page fired on them and was killed by a policeman with a rifle. Murphy remains in critical condition. Authorities said this morning they were trying to identify a white male who was a "person of interest." But FBI officials said later he was not relevant to the case. Page was quiet and "not a friendly guy," said a neighbor. "You'd have more fun with a camel. He was very quiet. You'd say hi and he'd kind of 'uh.' It was like he didn't care if you were talking or not." Page's grandmother, Elaine Lenz, called Page a good grandson who gave her a dozen roses last month "just to tell me he loved me," she said. "I'm sorry he has caused so much trouble."

final_summary: Minnesota Representative Michele Bachmann announced her intention to withdraw her Swiss citizenship, which she acquired through marriage to her husband, Marcus Bachmann, a dual American and Swiss citizen, in 1978. According to Bachmann, she made the decision to relinquish her dual citizenship to demonstrate her commitment to the United States and its constitution. In a statement, Bachmann emphasized her pride in being an American citizen, citing her family's military background and her own dedication to the country. Initially, Bachmann and her spokesperson stated that the family had simply updated their documents, but it was later revealed that Bachmann had sought to register her Swiss citizenship with Swiss authorities in February. This development drew attention, particularly given Bachmann's past criticism of President Barack Obama's policies. Some observers noted that Bachmann's decision to hold dual citizenship may raise questions about her commitment to the United States, although others argued that dual citizenship is a common practice among many individuals who benefit from holding multiple passports.

Minnesota Representative Michele Bachmann announced her intention to withdraw her Swiss citizenship, which she acquired through marriage to her husband, Marcus Bachmann, a dual American and Swiss citizen, in 1978. According to Bachmann, she made the decision to relinquish her dual citizenship to demonstrate her commitment to the United States and its constitution. In a statement, Bachmann emphasized her pride in being an American citizen, citing her family's military background and her own dedication to the country. 

Contrary to initial claims by Bachmann and her spokesperson that the family had simply updated their documents, it appears that Bachmann had indeed sought to register her Swiss citizenship with Swiss authorities in February. This revelation sparked controversy, particularly given Bachmann's history of criticizing President Barack Obama for his alleged lack of patriotism and questioning his birth certificate. Critics pointed out the apparent hypocrisy in Bachmann's actions, suggesting that her decision to hold dual citizenship undermined her claims of unwavering loyalty to the United States. 

Some commentators questioned whether Bachmann's dual citizenship posed a potential issue regarding her security clearance, given her position on the House Intelligence Committee. Others defended the practice of dual citizenship, noting that many individuals benefit from holding multiple passports and that it does not necessarily imply a lack of allegiance to one's primary country of residence.

reference: – If you were concerned that Michele Bachmann's Swiss citizenship meant she'd given up on America, worry no longer: She's getting rid of it. "Today I sent a letter to the Swiss Consulate requesting withdrawal of my dual Swiss citizenship, which was conferred upon me by operation of Swiss law when I married my husband in 1978," she said in a statement, according to Minnesota Public Radio. "I took this action because I want to make it perfectly clear: I was born in America and I am a proud American citizen." Bachmann hopes to put an end to the story that surfaced earlier this week. At the time, her spokeswoman described the situation this way, notes the LA Times: "Congresswoman Bachmann's husband is of Swiss descent so she has been eligible for dual citizenship since they got married in 1978. However, recently some of their children wanted to exercise their eligibility for dual citizenship so they went through the process as a family." Her opponent in November, Jim Graves of the Democratic-Farmer-Labor party, has called the Swiss story a "distraction." He has confirmed that he and his family are also "proud to be Americans."

final_summary:
A 42-year-old woman, Kathleen Dawn "Kat" West, was found dead outside her home in Calera, Alabama, on January 13th. The case is currently being investigated as a homicide, but the cause of death remains unknown. West's mother, Nancy Martin, launched an online fundraising campaign to assist with funeral expenses, stating that her daughter was a devoted wife and mother who brought comfort and joy to those around her.

West's online presence was multifaceted, with a subscription-based website where she went by the alias "Kitty Kat West," featuring revealing photos. Her social media profiles, including Twitter and Instagram, showcased her personality and interests, including a fascination with Marilyn Monroe. However, some of her online activity was more explicit, with a paid adult website offering "sexier images" for $15.99 per month. Neighbors reported that West was a private individual who kept to herself, often seen mowing her lawn and tending to her home.

Her husband, Jeff West, is a campus police officer at Birmingham-Southern College, and the couple resided in their home, which they built in 2015, along with their 12-year-old daughter. Following West's death, two private Facebook groups were established, attracting over 3,000 members, to discuss the case and speculate about potential motives. Many participants focused on West's online activities, suggesting that her death might be linked to her adult website or her marriage; however, law enforcement officials have not released any information connecting her online presence to her murder, and the investigation remains ongoing.

Local residents expressed shock and concern over the incident, with some describing West as a friendly and outgoing person who kept to herself, while others remembered her as a kind and pleasant individual who would often wave and smile. West's husband has not spoken publicly since her passing, and the couple's financial history, including a 2007 bankruptcy filing, has raised questions among neighbors. As the investigation continues, concerns have been raised about the impact of social media speculation and amateur sleuthing on the case, with law enforcement officials stating that much of the information and speculation shared on social media has hindered their investigation, highlighting the potential risks associated with online speculation and the importance of respecting the integrity of official investigations.
