reference: – Kathleen Dawn West described herself as a full-time wife and mom on Facebook but lived another life on other social media platforms, calling herself an exhibitionist and posting risque photos with a chance for subscribers to see sexier images for $15.99 a month. The 42-year-old's partially nude body was found dead outside her Alabama home, and authorities are now faced with a question: Did West's online activities play a role in her death? Police have classified West's death as a homicide, but they haven't said how she died. No charges had been filed by Friday, six days after she died. But the mysterious nature of West's death—she was found dead early Jan. 13 in the quiet bedroom community of Calera, a town of 14,000 people about 35 miles south of Birmingham—has people buzzing. At least two Facebook groups with more than 2,200 members total have been created to discuss the case (Gizmodo digs into their theories). West posted photos of her husband and daughter on her own Facebook account, but seemed like a different person on other online accounts, reports the AP. With a personal description that included "exhibitionist," West had a private Instagram account under the name Kitty Kat West, reports Al.com, with nearly 52,000 followers that linked to a paid site that featured adult content and "naughty fun." Some of the images were included on a site for "mature hotties." Initially, West just tweeted links to a site where she sold clothing items, including dresses, coats, shoes, and kid's clothing. The feed took a sharp turn Aug. 11, when she tweeted: "Happy Frisky Friday - Let's get this party started," with a link to the pay-per-view site. The posts continued until the morning before her death. The AP has more.

final_summary:
On March 23, 2016, the music world mourned the loss of Malik Taylor, better known by his stage name Phife Dawg, a member of the influential hip-hop group A Tribe Called Quest. The news of his passing sent shockwaves through the industry, prompting numerous tributes from fellow artists. Among those paying their respects were Jill Scott and Chuck D, who praised Phife Dawg as a "GREAT MC" and a "HipHop & Rap word Warrior." In response, WSB-TV Atlanta traffic reporter Mark Arum paid homage to the late rapper during his live broadcast, incorporating Phife Dawg's lyrics into his traffic updates.

Arum, who regularly incorporates hip-hop lyrics into his reports, decided to give Phife Dawg's words extra attention, skillfully weaving references into his commentary. He referenced several of Phife Dawg's notable lines, including "More hits than the Braves or the Yankees," "Are things ludicrously speedy or infectious with the slow-mo?", "Track record's longer than a DC-20 aircraft," "Do that, do that, do do that that that," "Tell your mother, tell your father, send a telegram," and "One for the treble, two for the bass, you know the style."

The impromptu tribute added a touching moment to the usual routine of traffic updates, allowing viewers to reflect on Phife Dawg's legacy. Fans can also look back on his impact through documentaries like the critically acclaimed film "Beats, Rhymes, and Life," which explores the history and influence of A Tribe Called Quest.

reference: – A Tribe Called Quest rapper Phife Dawg lived in Atlanta for more than a decade, and there are apparently at least a couple Quest fans at Atlanta's WSB-TV. After the rapper's death Wednesday, the station's traffic reporter, Mark Arum, worked an impressive number of Quest references into his traffic report. The video went viral and was shared by sites including the Daily Dot and BuzzFeed, who note some of the best call-outs: "This rush hour comin' on with more hits than the Braves or the Yankees." "Are things ludicrously speedy or infectious with the slow-mo?" "[Interstate-]85 is stacked and packed now, heading into midtown Atlanta with a crash south of 400—tell your mother, tell your father, send a telegram." Anchor Fred Blankenship even got in on the action, noting at one point, "Sometimes the definition of traffic comes sideways and straightways." "Phife and Tribe were the soundtrack of my youth," Arum tells BuzzFeed. "I was crushed when I heard the news this morning just before we went on the air. I wanted to pay tribute to him somehow." He says he's been inserting hip-hop lyrics into his traffic reports for years, but he really "let them fly" Wednesday—he typically only includes one or two a show. Arum and Blankenship also discussed their love for A Tribe Called Quest in a Facebook video Thursday, with Blankenship expressing his amazement that Arum "dropped deep Tribe Called Quest lyrics, and then didn't short-change the traffic."

final_summary:
A bitter fight has erupted between Robin Williams' widow, Susan Schneider Williams, and his three children, Zak, Zelda, and Cody Williams, over the distribution of the late actor's estate nearly six months after his passing. The dispute centers around a range of valuable assets, including cherished belongings and a significant portion of the wealth accumulated during his illustrious 40-year career in film, television, and stage. 

Court documents reveal a deep-seated disagreement between the parties regarding the interpretation of Robin Williams' trust and will, which he established prior to his marriage to Susan. The children claim that their father had explicitly stated in the trust that his personal effects, including his Oscar award, movie memorabilia, action figures, graphic novels, theater masks, and other items, should go to them upon his death. However, Susan Schneider Williams disputes this, arguing that certain items, such as the tuxedo from their wedding and other personal knick-knacks, fall under her purview despite acknowledging that she has no rights to items directly related to Robin's acting career, such as his suspenders from the movie "Mrs. Doubtfire."

The children assert that Susan can retain the items she acquired during their marriage, but take issue with her attempts to reclassify certain possessions, including jewelry, which they believe should be divided among them according to their father's original wishes. Sources close to the family suggest that Robin Williams would have been devastated if the matter had not been resolved amicably within the family. The dispute has led to a public airing of grievances through court filings, highlighting the complexities and sensitivities surrounding the division of a celebrity's estate following their passing.

According to court documents filed in December and January, the disagreement revolves around the interpretation of Robin Williams' trust and will, which he established prior to his marriage to Susan.

The children claim that their father had explicitly stated in the trust that his personal effects, including his Oscar award, movie memorabilia, action figures, graphic novels, theater masks, and other items, should go to them upon his death. However, Susan Schneider Williams disputes this, arguing that certain items, such as the tuxedo from their wedding and other personal knick-knacks, fall under her purview despite acknowledging that she has no rights to items directly related to Robin's acting career, such as his suspenders from the movie "Mrs. Doubtfire."

The children assert that Susan can retain the items she acquired during their marriage, but take issue with her attempts to reclassify certain possessions, including jewelry, which they believe should be divided among them according to their father's original wishes. Sources close to the family suggest that Robin Williams would have been devastated if the matter had not been resolved amicably within the family. The dispute has led to a public airing of grievances through court filings, highlighting the complexities and sensitivities surrounding the division of a celebrity's estate following their passing.

history.draft:
A bitter fight has erupted between Robin Williams' widow, Susan Schneider Williams, and his three children, Zak, Zelda, and Cody Williams, over the distribution of the late actor's estate nearly six months after his passing. The dispute centers around a range of valuable assets, including cherished belongings and a significant portion of the wealth accumulated during his illustrious 40-year career in film, television, and stage. 

Court documents reveal a deep-seated disagreement between the parties regarding the interpretation of Robin Williams' trust and will, which he established prior to his marriage to Susan. The children claim that their father had explicitly stated in the trust that his personal effects, including his Oscar award, movie memorabilia, action figures, graphic novels, theater masks, and other items, should go to them upon his death. However, Susan Schneider Williams disputes this, arguing that certain items, such as the tuxedo from their wedding and other personal knick-knacks, fall under her purview despite acknowledging that she has no rights to items directly related to Robin's acting career, such as his suspenders from the movie "Mrs. Doubtfire."

The children assert that Susan can retain the items she acquired during their marriage, but take issue with her attempts to reclassify certain possessions, including jewelry, which they believe should be divided among them according to their father's original wishes. Sources close to the family suggest that Robin Williams would have been devastated if the matter had not been resolved amicably within the family. The dispute has led to a public airing of grievances through court filings, highlighting the complexities and sensitivities surrounding the division of a celebrity's estate following their passing.

reference: – In a sad but all-too-common postscript to someone's life cut short, Robin Williams' children and wife are locked in a bitter dispute over his estate. In court papers filed in December and January, the comedian's three adult children say they are "heartbroken" that widow Susan Williams is "challenging the plans he so carefully made for his estate" by trying to keep personal effects she's not entitled to, reports the New York Times. The children—Zak, Zelda, and Cody—are from two previous marriages, and they note that Susan was married to their father for "less than three years." They accuse her of adding "insult to a terrible injury" and say she refused to allow them in his Tiburon, Calif., home in the days after his death. Williams, who committed suicide in August, specified his estate go to his three children, but the dispute centers around a provision that the home, its contents, and enough cash to cover "all costs related to the residence" go to Susan Williams. The children accuse her of wrongly interpreting this to mean she can keep belongings she was never meant to have and of "greedily" trying to claim extra funds. Sources tell TMZ that the widow, who refers to Williams' collections of things like action figures and movie posters as "knickknacks," also argues that "jewelry" doesn't include things like watches. Her lawyer tells the Times that his client doesn't have "sticky fingers" and "compared to what the Williams children were set to receive from their father, this is a bucket of water in a lake."

final_summary:
Researchers at the University of Tokyo and Kyoto University have achieved a groundbreaking success in regenerative medicine by creating human ear cartilage from induced pluripotent stem cells (iPS cells). The researchers utilized a novel approach by culturing these cells into cartilage cells and shaping them into numerous tiny balls, which were then implanted onto the back of a rat. The implantation site eventually developed into a fully formed ear, approximately 5 centimeters in length, within two months. This remarkable achievement demonstrates the potential of this technology to treat individuals born without ears or with small ears.

This breakthrough builds upon the capabilities of iPS cells, which possess the ability to differentiate into various human tissues and hold great promise for regenerative medicine. Moreover, researchers are actively exploring similar applications, such as creating artificial kidneys, intestines, and hearts, to address pressing medical needs. Notably, the University of California, San Francisco, is developing a sophisticated artificial kidney device using human kidney cells, silicon nanofilters, and blood pressure power, with plans to initiate testing in 2017.

Scientists at Cornell University have engineered implants using gel and collagen seeded with rat cells, which were successfully placed into rat spines, providing an alternative to traditional surgical fusion methods. Furthermore, researchers are making significant strides in creating bioengineered organs for transplantation. For instance, a team at Wake Forest University Baptist Medical Center grew miniature livers in the lab using decellularized animal livers and human cells in 2011. More recently, a team from Yokohama City University Graduate School of Medicine reprogrammed human adult skin cells, added other cell types, and coaxed them into growing into early-stage liver "buds."

While producing a full-sized liver remains a substantial challenge, requiring tens of thousands of these buds, researchers continue to push the boundaries of bioengineering. Artificial heart devices have been implanted since the 1980s, but none have replicated the effectiveness of a natural heart. Recent developments involve incorporating more biological material into these devices, such as the French company Carmat's artificial device containing cow heart tissue, which is set to undergo testing soon. Researchers at Massachusetts General Hospital are working on a bioartificial heart scaffold, while MIT scientists have printed functional heart tissue from rodent cells.

The creation of blood vessels in the lab from a patient's own cells holds immense promise for treating cardiovascular disease, kidney disease, and diabetes. A study by Cytograft Tissue Engineering demonstrated that three end-stage kidney disease patients implanted with bioengineered blood vessels experienced improved access to dialysis after eight months. Additionally, a team at Massachusetts General Hospital discovered a method to encourage stem-like cells to develop into vascular precursor cells, resulting in the generation of long-lasting blood vessels in living mice.

Surgeon Anthony Atala, director of the Wake Forest Institute for Regenerative Medicine, has successfully bioengineered bladders using patients' cells grown on a biodegradable scaffold, which have shown positive results in clinical trials. Similarly, a team at Princeton University employed 3D printing technology to create a functional ear from calf cells and electronic materials, capable of detecting radio frequencies beyond human range. Other notable achievements include the development of artificial skin for grafting and toxicity testing, with the Fraunhofer Institute for Interfacial Engineering and Biotechnology introducing a system capable of manufacturing 5,000 skin sheets per month.

Researchers are also working on creating bioengineered body parts, such as noses and windpipes, using tissue engineering techniques. These advancements demonstrate the rapid progress being made in the field of bioengineering, which may one day provide life-saving solutions for those awaiting organ transplants.

reference: – Cool—and/or creepy—news out of Japan, where researchers at the University of Tokyo and Kyoto University have created a human ear ... on the back of a rat. The researchers used stem cells that grew into ear cartilage, shaping the cells into an ear by putting them in "biological tubing," Discovery News reports. More specifically, they used "induced pluripotent stem cells," which the National Institutes of Health explains are "adult cells that have been genetically reprogrammed to an embryonic stem cell-like state." The ear-shaped cartilage was implanted under a rat's skin, where it was allowed to grow for two months; the tubing dissolved and a 2-inch-long ear was left behind. The researchers believe this technology could be used to treat people who are born with no ears or with smaller-than-normal ears, Japan Today reports. It could also be used to repair ears disfigured in accidents, Discovery adds. In the case of a child born with a missing or misshapen ear, the researchers note that the ear they created is "living," and thus would develop and grow along with the child. A clinical trial involving humans is expected to start in five years. In 2013, researchers in Boston used sheep cells, bovine collagen, and an ear-shaped titanium wire frame to grow an ear on the back of another rat, Discovery notes. (These creatures can grow the heads and brains of other species.)

final_summary:
On Friday, April 1, 2016, the U.S. government issued its jobs report for March, revealing a solid month of hiring with 215,000 new positions added across various sectors. This uptick in employment was largely driven by significant gains in the construction, retail, and healthcare industries. However, despite the positive job numbers, the unemployment rate ticked up to 5% from 4.9%, primarily due to more people entering the workforce in search of employment.

The steady hiring trend suggests that employers remain optimistic about their business prospects, even as the overall economic growth has slowed down since the previous winter. Analysts predict that the economy expanded at a rate of around 1% annually during the first quarter. The continued job creation indicates that businesses might view the slowdown as temporary.

Wage growth, a long-standing concern in the post-recession era, showed a modest improvement, increasing by 2.3% over the past year to reach $25.43 per hour. While this is still below the typical 3.5% pace seen in a robust economy, it marks an improvement from earlier years. Construction companies contributed significantly to the job market, adding 37,000 positions, possibly influenced by favorable weather conditions. This gain helped offset losses in the manufacturing and mining sectors, which have struggled due to decreased demand abroad and low oil prices. Notably, the mining industry has lost 185,000 jobs since September 2014.

Consumer spending, a critical driver of economic growth, has experienced a recent downturn, following a strong performance in 2015. In February, spending increased by only 0.1% for the third consecutive month, leading many economists to revise downward their growth forecasts. Furthermore, Americans appear to be saving more money from reduced gasoline costs, with the savings rate reaching 5.4% in February, the highest level in a year. Economic indicators have presented mixed signals, with some suggesting that manufacturers may be stabilizing after facing challenges in 2015. Homebuyers have also become more cautious, with sales of existing homes declining 7.1% in February due to limited availability and rising prices.

reference: – US employers notched another solid month of hiring in March by adding a higher-than-expected 215,000 jobs, driven by large gains in the construction, retail, and health care industries, the AP reports. Despite the jump, the Labor Department said Friday that the unemployment rate ticked up to 5% from 4.9%. But that increase includes some good news: More Americans came off the sidelines to look for work, though not all found jobs. The figures suggest that employers remain confident enough in their business prospects to add staff, even as overall growth has slowed since last winter. Steady hiring is also contributing to higher pay, which rose a modest 2.3% from a year earlier to $25.43. Paychecks typically grow at a 3.5% pace in a strong economy. Construction firms added 37,000 jobs, likely aided by warmer weather. Low oil prices have cost that industry 185,000 jobs since September 2014. Meanwhile, the MoneyBeat team at the Wall Street Journal has deemed Friday "datageddon" for all of the additional info scheduled to be released, including manufacturing data from the US, China, and Europe and an update from the University of Michigan's Consumer Sentiment Index.

final_summary: On August 11th, sixty-one-year-old Leslie Kahn found herself in a precarious situation while swimming in her Epping, New Hampshire, backyard pool. As she attempted to exit the pool using the ladder, a critical step gave way beneath her weight, causing her to become stranded in approximately four feet of water. With no one else at home and her cell phone inaccessible due to being indoors, Kahn realized she needed assistance. Utilizing a pool pole, she managed to retrieve her iPad from its position on a nearby chair and accessed the internet through the device. Kahn then created a distress call on the local community Facebook page "Epping Squawks," labeling it "911" to garner immediate attention. In response to her plea for help, several concerned neighbors offered aid, and a nearby resident arrived quickly, soon joined by law enforcement and another neighbor. Initially, attempts to repair the broken ladder and employ makeshift solutions, such as placing a chair in the water, did not prove successful. However, a step ladder eventually provided the necessary support for Kahn to safely exit the pool. This incident highlights the importance of community connections and the role that social media can play in facilitating timely assistance during emergencies.

As she attempted to exit the pool using the ladder, a critical step gave way beneath her weight, causing her to become stranded in approximately four feet of water. With no one else at home and her cell phone inaccessible due to being indoors, Kahn realized she needed assistance. Utilizing a pool pole, she managed to retrieve her iPad from its position on a nearby chair and accessed the internet through the device. Kahn then created a distress call on the local community Facebook page "Epping Squawks," labeling it "911" to garner immediate attention. In response to her plea for help, several concerned neighbors offered aid, and a nearby resident arrived quickly, soon joined by law enforcement and another neighbor. Initially, attempts to repair the broken ladder and employ makeshift solutions, such as placing a chair in the water, did not prove successful. However, a step ladder eventually provided the necessary support for Kahn to safely exit the pool. This incident highlights the importance of community connections and the role that social media can play in facilitating timely assistance during emergencies.

reference: – A New Hampshire woman who became stuck in her swimming pool after the ladder broke turned to the loving arms of Facebook to ask for help getting out. "I had one foot on (the ladder), and when I put my other foot on it, it went down. And I cracked my knee," Leslie Kahn tells WBZ-TV of the Aug. 11 incident. The 61-year-old says she didn't have the upper-body strength to hoist herself out, and "my tenants were out, nobody was home. My phone was inside." With few other options, Kahn used a pool pole to drag the chair her iPad was on toward her and posted in a community Facebook page, asking for help. She said she labeled the post "911" to get people's attention. A woman who lived nearby showed up, followed by police and a neighbor. "Every idea we thought of didn’t work," she says, until her rescuers thought to try a step ladder. "And I just climbed out. Happily ever after at that point." Kahn, whom the AP notes is a breast cancer survivor, is apparently no worse for the wear: "People keep saying, 'I bet you can laugh about it now.' I was laughing about it then. What else can you do!"

final_summary:
Thousands of mourners gathered at Friendly Temple Missionary Baptist Church in St. Louis on August 25, 2014, to bid farewell to Michael Brown, the 18-year-old who was shot and killed by a police officer, Darren Wilson, on August 9. The funeral service was a celebration of Brown's life, a search for meaning in his death, and a call to action for change in policing practices in America. However, the event was not without controversy, as some critics argued that the language used to describe the service was overly emotive and failed to maintain a neutral tone.

The church's sanctuary, which held approximately 2,500 people, was filled to capacity, with an additional 2,000 attendees waiting outside in the sweltering heat. Brown's family and friends remembered him as a "kind, gentle soul" with a deepening faith in Christianity and ambitions to become a heating and air conditioning technician. His cousin, Eric Davis, urged the crowd to use their collective voice to demand change, stating that the community had "had enough of senseless killings." Brown's uncle, Bernard Ewing, shared a poignant anecdote about his nephew's aspirations, recalling that Brown had once said, "one day, the world will know my name."

The Rev. Al Sharpton delivered a powerful eulogy, calling for calm and restraint in the face of future protests, emphasizing that Michael Brown did not want to be remembered for riots, but rather as a catalyst for change in American policing. Sharpton also criticized police who pointed rifles at peaceful protesters, urging the crowd to respect the Brown family's wishes for a day of silence. Other speakers, including Bishop Edwin Bass and the Rev. Charles Ewing, echoed the theme of seeking justice and change through nonviolent means. Ewing drew parallels between Brown's life and death with biblical scripture, stating that Brown's blood cried out for vengeance and justice, not just for him, but for countless others who had suffered similar fates.

As the service came to a close, mourners filed past Brown's casket, adorned with a St. Louis Cardinals baseball cap, to pay their final respects. Scores of motorcycles accompanied the funeral procession to St. Peter's Cemetery, where Brown was laid to rest. The event was attended by a diverse array of guests, including celebrities, politicians, and civil rights leaders, as well as ordinary citizens who had been moved by Brown's story. In a poignant moment, Brown's mother, Lesley McSpadden, read a heartfelt letter to her son, expressing her love and gratitude for the gift of his life. His father, Michael Brown Sr., also penned a letter, assuring his son that he would never forget him and that he would always live on in his heart.

As the nation grappled with the aftermath of Brown's death, the mood in Ferguson, Missouri, shifted from tension and anger to a sense of calm and reflection. The town was slowly returning to normalcy, with children in the Ferguson-Florissant School District finally resuming classes after a week-long delay due to safety concerns. Despite the progress, however, the underlying issues of racism and police brutality remained unresolved, leaving behind a lingering sense of unease and a pressing need for reform.

reference: – More than 2,500 mourners crowded Friendly Temple Missionary Baptist Church in St. Louis this morning to lay Michael Brown to rest, reports the AP, with another 2,000 in overflow seating and national television carrying the 18-year-old's funeral. Brown's casket was closed, with a St. Louis Cardinals cap atop it, notes the Post-Dispatch. His uncle, Bernard Ewing, recalled him as a "big guy, but a kind, gentle soul" in a service that the New York Times calls "deeply religious." The overarching message was one of change, with Brown's family members urging mourners to vote, and the Rev. Al Sharpton saying, "We are required to leave here today and change things. Michael Brown must be remembered for more than disturbances." Michael Brown Sr., wearing a tie bearing his son's image, had yesterday pleaded with protesters for "a day of silence so I can, so we can, lay our son to rest." Other highlights: Among those gathered: Jesse Jackson, Martin Luther King III, Spike Lee, Rep. Maxine Waters, Rep. William Lacy Clay; family members of Trayvon Martin and Sean Bell; three White House representatives. Gov. Jay Nixon declined to attend "out of respect for the family." Sharpton on protesters who turned to violence, as per CNN: "Can you imagine, they're heartbroken, their son taken, discarded, and marginalized, and they have to stop mourning to get you to control your anger, like you're more angry than they are, like you don't understand that Michael Brown does not want to be remembered for a riot." The Rev. Charles Ewing, Brown's uncle who eulogized him: "Michael Brown's blood is crying from the ground, crying for vengeance, crying for justice. There is a cry being made from the ground, not just for Michael Brown, but for the Trayvon Martins, for those children at Sandy Hook Elementary School, for the Columbine massacre, for the black-on-black crime."

final_summary:
A 51-year-old German tourist, identified as Oliver Park, tragically lost his life while attempting to take a photo at the Inca citadel of Machu Picchu in the Peruvian Andes on December 2, 2014. Park slipped from a ledge and fell approximately 130 feet to his death. 

Eyewitness Guillermo Mestas stated that Park was posing for a mid-leap photo when he lost his balance and fell, hitting a narrow ledge before continuing to plummet to the ground. 

This incident marks the latest in a series of accidents involving tourists at Machu Picchu, a UNESCO World Heritage site and Peru's top tourist destination. The site attracts millions of visitors each year, raising concerns about visitor safety. To address these concerns, the Peruvian government implemented a daily cap of 2,500 tourists in 2014 to mitigate risks.

Park's death is reminiscent of other incidents at Machu Picchu, including a 1997 fatality where an American tourist, Ingrid Mason, fell 400 feet while descending Huayna Picchu, a mountain overlooking the citadel. Other notable incidents include a 2004 fatality where a Russian tourist died after being struck by lightning while climbing the same peak, and a 2011 incident in which an Australian man died inside a tower at Machu Picchu after suffering a suspected heart attack.

Machu Picchu's unique geography, situated 8,000 feet above sea level, poses significant challenges for visitors. The site's breathtaking views and steep drop-offs make it a hazardous location for those seeking to capture the perfect photo. However, the incident also highlights the importance of adhering to safety guidelines and respecting restricted areas to prevent such tragedies.

In related news, a South Korean tourist fell to his death while taking a selfie at the Gocta waterfall in the Amazonas region just a day prior to Park's incident. This case, along with several others worldwide, underscores the growing concern about selfie-related accidents. These incidents have resulted in fatalities in various locations, including Yellowstone National Park, the Taj Mahal, and the Ganges River.

Oliver Park had ignored safety signs and warnings from wardens, entering a restricted area close to the edge of a cliff. Rescuers retrieved his body on Thursday and transported it to a local police station, with plans to transfer it to a morgue in Cusco.

reference: – It wasn't quite death-by-selfie, but it wasn't far off. Peru This Week reports that a German tourist died Wednesday while posing for a photo at Peru's famed Machu Picchu site. Oliver Park, 51, went into a restricted area of the mountainous tourist locale in the Andes, despite warning signs and instructions from wardens, reports the BBC. The news agency says that while posing for a photo at the edge of a ravine, Park apparently decided to jump in the air to make it look like he was flying. Instead, he ended up falling off the cliff. A witness gives a slightly different take to TV station Canal N, as translated by the Washington Post: "He asked a man who was there to take a photo of him," recounts the Peruvian tourist. "The man came over to take the photo and in the moment he was handing him the camera, he lost his balance and fell.” Both accounts agree that Park had ventured into an area of Machu Picchu, home to an ancient Incan citadel, that's supposed to be off limits to tourists. His body was recovered Thursday. (In 2014, Machu Picchu was plagued by streakers.)

final_summary:
Jeff Herman, a 54-year-old lawyer from Boca Raton, Florida, has dedicated nearly three decades to advocating for victims of childhood sexual abuse, taking on powerful institutions like the Catholic Church, the Boy Scouts of America, and now Hollywood. Herman's law firm, Herman Law, specializes in litigating cases involving child sex abuse, with over 800 cases under his belt. He has become a prominent figure in the fight against child sex abuse, frequently holding press conferences to raise awareness about his cases.

Herman's motivation stems from his own traumatic experiences. He has a fragmented memory of being held by his arms and legs when he was young, possibly by his older sisters, and his pants were cut open. Although he does not recall the full details, the experience has left him with an unsettling feeling whenever he sees a child being held in a similar manner. Furthermore, his older sister revealed to him five years ago that their grandfather had sexually abused her when she was a child, which Herman had suspected but chose not to confront.

Herman's current cases involve allegations against "X-Men" director Bryan Singer and Broadway producer Gary Goddard, whom he accuses of sexually assaulting a 17-year-old boy in London in 2006. Herman has filed a lawsuit on behalf of the anonymous victim, John Doe No. 117, who claims that Singer offered him a Quaalude and plied him with alcohol before bringing him to his hotel suite for the alleged assault. The lawsuit also alleges that Goddard sent the boy explicit emails and messages, including one with a photo of the boy wearing only a towel around his waist.

Singer and Goddard have denied the allegations, with Singer branding the lawsuit a "shakedown" and Goddard's lawyer calling the claims "vehemently denied" and "vigorously defended." Herman has responded by stating that the pushback he is receiving is reminiscent of the early days of the clergy sex abuse scandals in the Catholic Church, where victims were met with blanket denials and skepticism.

Herman approaches child sex abuse cases with sensitivity and care, creating a supportive environment for his clients. He uses visual aids like timelines and drawings to help them recount their experiences, emphasizing the importance of empowerment and healing. Giving victims a voice is crucial in the recovery process, Herman believes. He has also developed an app to help identify potential predators and advises parents to trust their instincts and remove their children from situations that make them feel uncomfortable.

Despite facing criticism from his opponents, Herman remains committed to his cause, highlighting the devastating impact of child sex abuse on victims and their families. He has vowed to continue filing lawsuits against those responsible, including a new case involving an alleged sex ring operating in Hollywood. Herman's dedication to exposing the truth and bringing justice to victims has earned him recognition as a champion for those who have been silenced for far too long.

history.draft:
Jeff Herman, a 54-year-old lawyer from Boca Raton, Florida, has dedicated nearly three decades to advocating for victims of childhood sexual abuse, taking on powerful institutions like the Catholic Church, the Boy Scouts of America, and now Hollywood. Herman's law firm, Herman Law, specializes in litigating cases involving child sex abuse, with over 800 cases under his belt. He has become a prominent figure in the fight against child sex abuse, frequently holding press conferences to raise awareness about his cases.

Herman's motivation stems from his own traumatic experiences. He has a fragmented memory of being held by his arms and legs when he was young, possibly by his older sisters, and his pants were cut open. Although he does not recall the full details, the experience has left him with an unsettling feeling whenever he sees a child being held in a similar manner. Furthermore, his older sister revealed to him five years ago that their grandfather had sexually abused her when she was a child, which Herman had suspected but chose not to confront.

Herman's current cases involve allegations against "X-Men" director Bryan Singer and Broadway producer Gary Goddard, whom he accuses of sexually assaulting a 17-year-old boy in London in 2006. Herman has filed a lawsuit on behalf of the anonymous victim, John Doe No. 117, who claims that Singer offered him a Quaalude and plied him with alcohol before bringing him to his hotel suite for the alleged assault. The lawsuit also alleges that Goddard sent the boy explicit emails and messages, including one with a photo of the boy wearing only a towel around his waist.

Singer and Goddard have denied the allegations, with Singer branding the lawsuit a "shakedown" and Goddard's lawyer calling the claims "vehemently denied" and "vigorously defended." Herman has responded by stating that the pushback he is receiving is reminiscent of the early days of the clergy sex abuse scandals in the Catholic Church, where victims were met with blanket denials and skepticism.

Herman approaches child sex abuse cases with sensitivity and care, creating a supportive environment for his clients. He uses visual aids like timelines and drawings to help them recount their experiences, emphasizing the importance of empowerment and healing. Giving victims a voice is crucial in the recovery process, Herman believes. He has also developed an app to help identify potential predators and advises parents to trust their instincts and remove their children from situations that make them feel uncomfortable.

Despite facing criticism from his opponents, Herman remains committed to his cause, highlighting the devastating impact of child sex abuse on victims and their families. He has vowed to continue filing lawsuits against those responsible, including a new case involving an alleged sex ring operating in Hollywood. Herman's dedication to exposing the truth and bringing justice to victims has earned him recognition as a champion for those who have been silenced for far too long.

reference: – Bryan Singer has already been hit with two lawsuits alleging sexual abuse of minors—and the lawyer who filed both of them, plus other suits against Hollywood bigwigs, says more are coming. "I’ve heard from victims about very recent claims of being sexually exploited in Hollywood," Jeff Herman said at a news conference yesterday, according to Variety. He said one coming lawsuit involves another Hollywood "sex ring." Herman also shared photos showing the second alleged victim (with his face redacted) and Singer at the London premiere of Singer's Superman Returns in 2006; John Doe, then 17, says Singer abused him after the premiere, the New York Daily News reports. As for the strenuous denials from all the Hollywood VIPs named in the various suits, Herman said, "The pushback I’m getting reminds me of the early days of the clergy sex abuse scandal." Meanwhile, in an interview with the Daily Beast, Herman says he himself has vague memories of being sexually abused as a child. He says he's worked on some 800 sex abuse cases in his career, notably involving the Catholic Church and the Boy Scouts.

final_summary:
In 2014, Google CEO Sundar Pichai described one of the company's new Android Nexus smartphones. Around the same time, Alphabet Inc., Google's parent company, granted Pichai an equity award valued at nearly $200 million, making him one of the world's highest-paid executives. According to a securities filing, Pichai received 273,328 Class C Google stock units on February 3, valued at $199 million at the time, although the current value stands at approximately $182 million due to subsequent declines in Alphabet shares. The award will vest in quarterly increments over the next four years as long as Pichai remains employed by Alphabet, increasing the value of his equity stake in the company to over $600 million.

This significant award places Pichai among the ranks of other high-profile executives, including Apple's Tim Cook, who received a grant valued at around $376 million in 2011. Notably, Pichai's award is still lower than the stakes held by Google's co-founders, Larry Page and Sergey Brin, and Executive Chairman Eric Schmidt, who was hired several years after the company began. Pichai's new shares bring his total Alphabet holdings to approximately $650 million, which is significantly higher than the median net worth of US citizens, calculated by Credit Suisse to be around $45,000. However, his wealth pales in comparison to that of Google's founders, who are reportedly worth $34.6 billion and $33.9 billion respectively, according to Forbes. Additionally, former CEO Eric Schmidt holds shares worth $3 billion.

The award has raised questions about Google's tax dealings with the UK government. While the company agreed to pay £130 million ($187 million) in back taxes since 2005, critics argue that this amount is relatively low considering the estimated £7.2 billion ($10.4 billion) in profits generated during that period. This discrepancy highlights the issue of Google's effective tax rate, which is reportedly between 2-3%, compared to the UK's 20% headline corporation tax rate.

Other Google executives also received substantial awards, including CFO Ruth Porat, who was handed shares worth around $38 million, in addition to a $30 million signing bonus, and Diane Greene, who was awarded shares worth $42.8 million, on top of $148 million granted last year following Google's acquisition of Bebop Technologies, a company she founded.

final_summary: On November 19, 2018, American rapper, singer, songwriter, record producer, television personality, entrepreneur, and football coach Snoop Dogg received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame. This honor marked the 2,651st tribute to a celebrity on the iconic sidewalk in Los Angeles. During the ceremony, held outside the El Capitan theater on Hollywood Boulevard, Snoop Dogg expressed gratitude to various individuals who played significant roles in his life and career, including his parents, wife Shante Broadus, DJ Warren G, Dr. Dre, Jimmy Kimmel, and Quincy Jones. However, in a humorous yet self-deprecating tone, Snoop Dogg saved his most heartfelt thanks for himself, acknowledging his own hard work, dedication, and perseverance. He stated, "I want to thank me for believing in me; I want to thank me for doing all this hard work; I want to thank me for having no days off; I want to thank me for never quitting; I want to thank me for always being a giver, and trying to give more than I receive; I want to thank me for doing more right than wrong; I want to thank me for being me at all times." This tongue-in-cheek expression of gratitude was met with laughter and applause from the audience, which included his family members, friends, and fellow celebrities. Snoop Dogg's star is located near the TCL Chinese Theatre, serving as a testament to his remarkable achievements in the entertainment industry. With a career spanning over two decades, Snoop Dogg has sold over 40 million albums worldwide, including his debut album "Doggystyle," which debuted at number one on the Billboard 200 chart in 1993. Throughout his illustrious career, Snoop Dogg has released 17 studio albums, collaborated with numerous artists, and acted in various films and television shows. He has also ventured into entrepreneurship, co-founding Merry Jane, a marijuana-focused media company, and establishing the Snoop Youth Football League to provide opportunities for underprivileged children. As a result of his tireless efforts, Snoop Dogg has become a household name, earning him a spot on the Hollywood Walk of Fame and cementing his status as a cultural icon. In related news, Snoop Dogg has been keeping busy with various projects, including releasing an Amazon Original song titled "Grateful" and penning his first cookbook, "From Crook to Cook: Platinum Recipes from Tha Boss Dogg's Kitchen." The cookbook features 50 of his favorite recipes, including baked mac and cheese, chicken and waffles, and baby back ribs, all of which are cannabis-free. Additionally, Snoop Dogg has been touring with his semi-autobiographical musical, "Redemption of a Dogg," and hosting his cooking show, "Martha & Snoop's Potluck Dinner Party," alongside Martha Stewart. As Snoop Dogg continues to break barriers and push boundaries in the entertainment industry, his star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame serves as a reminder of his enduring impact and influence on popular culture.

reference: – Snoop Dogg got a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame Monday, and the final person the rapper thanked in his speech wasn't God, his wife, or his parents—it was himself. "Last but not least, I want to thank me for believing in me. I want to thank me for doing all this hard work, I want to thank me for having no days off, I want to thank me for never quitting, I want to thank me for always being a giver and trying to give more than I receive," the 47-year-old said, per People. "I want to thank me for trying to do more [right] than wrong, I want to thank me for just being me at all times. Snoop Dogg, you a bad motherf-----." Of course, he did also thank all of those aforementioned people and entities, plus colleagues including Dr. Dre and Quincy Jones, KTLA reports. And on Instagram, he thanked his fans. "Blessed," he captioned a photo of the star on Twitter. NBC Los Angeles notes the tribute came four days before the 25th anniversary of his debut album, Doggystyle. Snoop, born Calvin Broadus, is "the Susan Lucci of the Grammys," per the AV Club, having racked up 17 nominations since then with no wins. "Snoop Dogg is one of those people who can do anything. He raps, he acts, he hosts a television show, he cooks, and helps community kids with his philanthropic work," the producer of the Hollywood Walk of Fame said in a news release. "He is a man of the ages and we welcome him to our Hollywood Walk of Fame." (He's also into weed.)

final_summary:
Several hundred relatives of Jang Song-thaek, the uncle of North Korean leader Kim Jong-un, were arrested and taken into custody by the Ministry of State Security following his execution. According to a source in the North Korean capital, armed men arrived in the Pyongchon area, where many of Jang's relatives resided, and took away a few hundred individuals, including both close and distant family members. The exact date of the arrests is unclear, but it is believed to have occurred shortly after Jang's execution.

Jang, who was married to the sister of former leader Kim Jong-il, held a high-ranking position in North Korea before his downfall. He faced numerous charges, including undermining the unity and cohesion of the party, dreaming of alternative futures, creating illusions about himself, committing corruption, and squandering foreign currency at casinos. Additionally, he was accused of engaging in improper relationships with multiple women.

The events surrounding Jang's execution were shrouded in secrecy, but it is understood that a power struggle between him and Kim Jong-un began over control of lucrative business ventures, particularly the coal, crab, and clam fishing industries. Tensions arose among the North Korean elite due to Jang's dominance of these sectors, with some officials resentful of his influence and wealth. A violent confrontation between Jang's supporters and rival factions broke out near the western sea border between North and South Korea, resulting in a decisive victory for Jang's forces. However, Kim Jong-un eventually mobilized a larger military force, which defeated Jang's loyalists and led to the execution of his top lieutenants.

Jang himself was put on trial, publicly denounced, and executed by firing squad. Following his demise, a significant shift in the balance of power within the North Korean regime occurred, with Kim Jong-un consolidating his control and eliminating a potential rival. Meanwhile, Dennis Rodman, the former NBA player, visited North Korea to train local basketball players for an exhibition game scheduled for Kim Jong-un's birthday. Although Rodman did not meet with the North Korean leader, he expressed admiration for the players and stated that he would return to the country in a week. However, his trip was marred by controversy, with the online betting company Paddy Power withdrawing its sponsorship due to international criticism of the North Korean regime.

The scale of the crackdown on Jang's relatives serves as a stark reminder of the severity of punishment meted out to those deemed enemies of the state in North Korea. Family members of those convicted of crimes often face persecution, with the most severe cases resulting in imprisonment in labor camps or execution. In this instance, the sheer number of arrests and the involvement of distant relatives suggest that Kim Jong-un is taking drastic measures to eradicate Jang's influence and legacy entirely.

history.draft:
Several hundred relatives of Jang Song-thaek, the uncle of North Korean leader Kim Jong-un, were arrested and taken into custody by the Ministry of State Security following his execution. According to a source in the North Korean capital, armed men arrived in the Pyongchon area, where many of Jang's relatives resided, and took away a few hundred individuals, including both close and distant family members. The exact date of the arrests is unclear, but it is believed to have occurred shortly after Jang's execution.

Jang, who was married to the sister of former leader Kim Jong-il, held a high-ranking position in North Korea before his downfall. He faced numerous charges, including undermining the unity and cohesion of the party, dreaming of alternative futures, creating illusions about himself, committing corruption, and squandering foreign currency at casinos. Additionally, he was accused of engaging in improper relationships with multiple women.

The events surrounding Jang's execution were shrouded in secrecy, but it is understood that a power struggle between him and Kim Jong-un began over control of lucrative business ventures, particularly the coal, crab, and clam fishing industries. Tensions arose among the North Korean elite due to Jang's dominance of these sectors, with some officials resentful of his influence and wealth. A violent confrontation between Jang's supporters and rival factions broke out near the western sea border between North and South Korea, resulting in a decisive victory for Jang's forces. However, Kim Jong-un eventually mobilized a larger military force, which defeated Jang's loyalists and led to the execution of his top lieutenants.

Jang himself was put on trial, publicly denounced, and executed by firing squad. Following his demise, a significant shift in the balance of power within the North Korean regime occurred, with Kim Jong-un consolidating his control and eliminating a potential rival. Meanwhile, Dennis Rodman, the former NBA player, visited North Korea to train local basketball players for an exhibition game scheduled for Kim Jong-un's birthday. Although Rodman did not meet with the North Korean leader, he expressed admiration for the players and stated that he would return to the country in a week. However, his trip was marred by controversy, with the online betting company Paddy Power withdrawing its sponsorship due to international criticism of the North Korean regime.

The scale of the crackdown on Jang's relatives serves as a stark reminder of the severity of punishment meted out to those deemed enemies of the state in North Korea. Family members of those convicted of crimes often face persecution, with the most severe cases resulting in imprisonment in labor camps or execution. In this instance, the sheer number of arrests and the involvement of distant relatives suggest that Kim Jong-un is taking drastic measures to eradicate Jang's influence and legacy entirely.

reference: – Authorities have rounded up hundreds of relatives of Kim Jong Un's recently executed uncle and ferried them to prison camps, according to a report out of Seoul. The night after Jang Song Thaek was executed, "armed men from the Ministry of State Security arrived unannounced in the Pyongchon area of Pyongyang where a lot of his relatives lived," an insider tells the Daily NK, which is run by defectors, Fox News notes. "It was not just his close relatives who were taken away, but distant members of his family, too." Observers say family members could be executed or spend life in prison (though things appear OK for Kim's aunt). In other news from Pyongyang: The Telegraph lists some of the 24 charges against Jang, which apparently included "gnawing at the unity and cohesion of the party" and "dreaming different dreams." He was also blamed for "improper relations with several women" and "squandering foreign currency at casinos." But Pyongyang's claims that Jang was executed over his plans to seize power are likely inaccurate, says South Korea's head of intelligence: The real problem was Jang and his associates' push to take control of major business deals—for instance, coal sales to China, the official said in a closed meeting, as noted by the New York Times. After Jang's rivals complained, Kim ordered Jang's associates to drop certain deals, but they refused; Kim believed his authority was being questioned. In less consequential North Korea news, Dennis Rodman has exited the country, having wrapped up his third "fun" visit. He didn't get to see his pal Kim this time around (he was training basketball players), but tells CNN, "I'm not worried about it. I'll see him again. I will be coming back in another week."

final_summary:
A 103-year-old man and a 91-year-old woman, George Kirby and Doreen Luckie, have set a new world record by becoming the oldest couple to get married, with a combined age of 194 years. The couple, who have been together for 27 years, tied the knot at a hotel in Eastbourne, southern England, surrounded by close friends and family. George, a former boxer who won all 60 of his fights, was born in London in June 1912 and served in the Royal Air Force for 25 years as a physical education instructor. Following his service, he pursued various careers before ultimately retiring at the age of 94. Doreen, born in Brixton in September 1923, worked as a shorthand typist for the Director of Public Prosecutions.

The couple, who have seven children, 15 grandchildren, and seven great-grandchildren between them, got engaged on Valentine's Day this year. Despite being together for nearly three decades, they decided to formalize their relationship due to pressure from their elderly children. The ceremony took place at the Langham Hotel in Eastbourne, which is owned by George's son, Neil Kirby. Speaking ahead of the ceremony, Doreen mentioned that they had "no regrets" about not getting married sooner, stating that they didn't want to bother with it initially but eventually decided to go through with it. George, who was in a wheelchair due to a recent fall, expressed his delight at marrying Doreen, noting that she keeps him young and that they plan to spend the rest of their lives together. Their union broke the previous record held by a French couple, Francois Fernandez and Madeleine Francineau, who had a combined age of 191 years.

reference: – A UK couple with nearly 200 years between them tied the knot on Saturday in Britain, in a move that was a long time coming. George Kirby, 103, and Doreen Luckie, 91, are the world’s oldest married couple, according to AFP. The newlyweds have been together (and living together) for 27 years, and have a slew of children, grandchildren, and great-grandchildren between them. In regards to why they waited so long to marry, Luckie said they just "didn’t want to bother." That changed when Kirby popped the question on Valentine's Day—albeit in a nontraditional way. "I didn't get down on one knee, because I don't think I would have been able to get back up," he explained. The pair married at a hotel in Eastbourne, on the southern English coast, and a Guinness Book of World Records guest was in attendance, reports the Independent. The happy couple were a combined 194 years and 281 days old on their wedding day—three years older than the previous "world’s oldest couple," Francois Fernandez and Madeleine Francineau of France. The Brits met in 1988 through a group that organizes senior friendships, and hit it off right away. Kirby was a recently divorced 76-year-old, and Luckie had been widowed three years prior. As for whether any wild pre-wedding partying occurred, Kirby's 63-year-old son had this to say: "Dad missed his stag do lunch because he wasn't feeling up for it, but Doreen had a hen party luncheon which was a complete laugh with all her golden girls." (Want to stay married til you're 103? Avoid these 5 odd things that up your chances of divorce.)

final_summary:
On September 8th, customs officers at the regional airport of Graz in Austria discovered human entrails in the luggage of a Moroccan woman arriving from Morocco. The unusual find consisted of two receptacles containing the carefully wrapped intestines, which the woman claimed belonged to her deceased husband. According to the woman's lawyer, Anton Karner, she had brought the sample to Austria to undergo a toxicology analysis, suspecting that her husband may have been poisoned during a family meal in Morocco. 

The woman, 35, and her husband, 40, had been living in Graz for eight years before his death. Her concerns about potential poisoning arose after some members of her husband's family expressed disapproval of their marriage. A doctor in Marrakesh, where the couple had visited, extracted the piece of intestine and helped package it in formaldehyde and thick plastic containers, following what Karner described as "European Union norms." 

Customs officials initially consulted with the police regarding the unusual cargo, but ultimately decided that the woman had not committed any wrongdoing by transporting the sample. The intestine was subsequently sent to the pathology institute in Graz, led by Dr. Gerald Höfler, for further examination. Höfler noted that the packaging was "very professional" and "absolutely secure," suggesting it was likely prepared by a pathologist. The results of the examination are expected to take several days to determine.

reference: – On Sept. 8, a 35-year-old Moroccan woman's bag was randomly checked upon her arrival in Graz, Austria. What was inside was "absolutely secure, triple wrapped, according to European Union norms," per a pathologist. Yes, pathologist: The New York Times reports the woman was carrying a four-inch piece of her late husband's intestine; the Independent picks up a local paper's report that describes the entrails as being in two containers. The Local reports a government agency confirmed the find. The woman's lawyer tells the Times that the woman was bringing the body part back to Graz, where the couple had spent the last eight years, to have it tested. This after a Marrakesh doctor suggested the 40-year-old might have been surreptitiously poisoned, perhaps by members of her husband's own family who were against the marriage. The BBC reports a doctor was called and said such testing wasn't possible in the absence of the whole cadaver, but the Times reports the intestines have been sent to the aforementioned pathologist's clinic, which is examining the entrails and expects results next week. The woman's transport of the organ was not illegal, notes the paper. (The airport isn't the only unexpected place intestines have turned up.)

final_summary:
France's lower house of parliament, the National Assembly, has approved a contentious bill allowing same-sex couples to marry and adopt children, marking a significant milestone in the country's ongoing debate on LGBTQ+ rights. The legislation, championed by President François Hollande, was passed with a clear majority of 329 votes in favor and 229 against, with 10 deputies abstaining. 

The bill, which redefines marriage as a contract between two consenting adults, grants same-sex couples the same rights as their heterosexual counterparts, including the ability to adopt children. This development comes after months of intense debate and protests, with the Roman Catholic Church and various conservative groups expressing strong opposition to the legislation. 

Supporters of the bill, including LGBTQ+ advocacy groups such as Inter-LGBT, have welcomed the decision as a major victory for equality and human rights. They argue that the law will allow all couples to form unions freely and protect families without discrimination based on sexual orientation or gender identity. 

The bill now heads to the Senate, the upper house of parliament, where it is likely to face less resistance due to the left-wing majority. If passed, France will join a growing list of countries that have recognized same-sex marriage, including Argentina, Belgium, Canada, Spain, South Africa, and Norway. 

The controversy surrounding the bill has sparked massive protests, with over 340,000 people taking to the streets in Paris on January 14 to express their opposition. The anti-gay marriage camp, largely driven by the Catholic Church, has accused President Hollande of rushing the legislation through without adequate consultation. 

However, supporters argue that the law represents a crucial step towards equality and human rights for the LGBTQ+ community. The approval of the bill marks a significant shift in France's stance on same-sex marriage, which has been a topic of debate for years. 

While polls suggest that a majority of the French population supports same-sex marriage, there is still a significant divide on the issue, particularly regarding the adoption of children. The debate has sparked heated discussions about the role of religion in shaping societal values and the importance of protecting traditional family structures. 

As the bill moves forward, it remains to be seen how the Senate will respond. Nevertheless, the passage of this legislation is a testament to France's commitment to promoting equality and human rights, and it sets a precedent for future reforms aimed at protecting the rights of marginalized communities.

final_summary:
On Thursday night, Rep. Pete Sessions (R-Texas) announced he was ending his bid to become the next House majority leader, clearing the way for Majority Whip Kevin McCarthy (R-California) to assume the role. Sessions stated that he made the decision after careful consideration, citing concerns about the potential impact on party unity. Running a successful campaign, he believed, would have created unnecessary and painful divisions within the party.

Sessions, the current chair of the House Rules Committee and former head of the House GOP campaign committee, had been actively seeking support since Rep. Eric Cantor's (R-Virginia) surprise primary loss on Tuesday. Despite his efforts, however, he ultimately faced significant challenges due to McCarthy's established advantages as the party's chief vote counter and top fundraiser.

The timing of the leadership election, scheduled for June 19, also worked in McCarthy's favor. Speaker John Boehner's (Ohio) decision to hold the election just over a week after Cantor's announcement gave McCarthy a head start in securing support. Furthermore, several prominent conservatives, including Reps. Paul Ryan (Wisconsin), Tom Price (Georgia), and Jim Jordan (Ohio), had already declined to enter the race, limiting Sessions' opportunities to build momentum.

As the field narrowed, Rep. Raúl Labrador (R-Idaho) expressed interest in challenging McCarthy, receiving encouragement from fellow members of the 2010 GOP class. Although Labrador's candidacy may still pose a challenge, it remains unclear how much of a threat he poses to McCarthy's chances.

With McCarthy poised to become the next majority leader, attention turned to the competition for the position of majority whip. Rep. Peter Roskam (Illinois), McCarthy's chief deputy, is facing off against Rep. Steve Scalise (Louisiana), chairman of the conservative Republican Study Committee, and Rep. Marlin Stutzman (Indiana), who surprisingly entered the fray on Thursday morning.

Pete Sessions (R-Texas) announced he was ending his bid to become the next House majority leader, clearing the way for Majority Whip Kevin McCarthy (R-California) to assume the role. Sessions cited concerns about creating divisions within the party as the reason for his withdrawal, stating that running a successful campaign would have led to "unnecessary and painful division" among Republicans. 

Sessions, the current chair of the House Rules Committee and former head of the House GOP campaign committee, had been actively seeking support since Rep. Eric Cantor's (R-Virginia) surprise primary loss on Tuesday. However, despite his efforts, he failed to gain traction due to McCarthy's established advantages as the party's chief vote counter and top fundraiser.

McCarthy's prospects were bolstered by Speaker John Boehner's (Ohio) decision to schedule the leadership election for June 19, just over a week after Cantor's announcement. This swift timeline limited Sessions' ability to gather sufficient support to challenge McCarthy. Additionally, several prominent conservatives, including Reps. Paul Ryan (Wisconsin), Tom Price (Georgia), and Jim Jordan (Ohio), had already declined to enter the race.

As the field narrowed, Rep. Raúl Labrador (R-Idaho) expressed interest in challenging McCarthy, receiving encouragement from fellow members of the 2010 GOP class. Nevertheless, it remains uncertain whether Labrador can pose a serious threat to McCarthy's candidacy.

With McCarthy poised to become the next majority leader, attention shifted to the competition for the position of majority whip. Rep. Peter Roskam (Illinois), McCarthy's chief deputy, faces off against Rep. Steve Scalise (Louisiana), chairman of the conservative Republican Study Committee, and Rep. Marlin Stutzman (Indiana), who unexpectedly entered the fray on Thursday morning.

Cantor—the first majority leader ever to lose a primary—is stepping down at the end of July following his shock defeat at the hands of little-known Tea Party challenger David Brat. "It became obvious to me that the measures necessary to run a successful campaign would have created unnecessary and painful division within our party," Sessions said. Marlin Stutzman, a hardline conservative who was punished last year for defying House leaders, the Indianapolis Star notes.

final_summary:
Former Speaker of the House Newt Gingrich has been attempting to associate himself with the legacy of Ronald Reagan, claiming to have worked with the president to defeat communism and the Soviet empire. However, this portrayal is inaccurate, as Gingrich frequently criticized Reagan, his top aides, and their policies during the Reagan years. 

Although Gingrich voted with the president regularly, his rhetoric was consistently negative, and he was proven wrong in his predictions that Reagan's policies would fail. The struggles against Soviet expansionism in the Third World, including in Angola, Grenada, Afghanistan, and Central America, were intense, with Reagan's top team, including William Casey, Caspar Weinberger, and George Shultz, recognizing the significance of countering the Soviet empire. In contrast, Gingrich's criticisms of Reagan's efforts were vocal and persistent, contradicting the consensus among policymakers at the time.

On Capitol Hill, the battle against Soviet expansionism was particularly contentious, with leading Democrats such as Ted Kennedy, Christopher Dodd, Jim Wright, and Tip O'Neill employing various tactics to undermine Reagan's initiatives. Meanwhile, Republicans like Henry Hyde, Dick Cheney, Dan Burton, Connie Mack, and Tom DeLay stood in support of the president's efforts. However, Gingrich chose to attack Reagan instead of working with him, despite voting with the caucus.

A notable example of Gingrich's criticism came in a floor statement on March 21, 1986, during the fight over funding for the Nicaraguan contras. Gingrich declared that Reagan's administration was failing to effectively counter the Soviet empire and that the president's policies were weak and inadequate. He also accused the CIA, State Department, and Defense Department of lacking strategies to defeat the empire.

Former Speaker of the House Tom DeLay has publicly stated that Gingrich is not a true conservative, drawing comparisons to Bill Clinton. DeLay notes that Gingrich has a history of making promises he fails to keep and has a tendency to shift his positions to suit his audience. Critics argue that Gingrich lacks the charm and charisma of Clinton, but shares his narcissistic tendencies and a penchant for controversy.

Gingrich's presidential campaign has been marred by a series of gaffes and controversies, including his criticism of Paul Ryan's budget proposal as "right-wing social engineering" and his subsequent reversal on the issue. He has also been accused of hypocrisy for advocating for a guest worker program that would allow undocumented immigrants to work in the US while simultaneously opposing amnesty for those already living in the country.

In contrast to Gingrich, Mitt Romney has demonstrated a track record of consistency on conservative issues, including his opposition to abortion, support for entitlement reform, and pledge to repeal Obamacare. Romney has been praised by conservative commentators such as John Bolton and Larry Kudlow for his fiscal conservatism and commitment to reducing government spending. Ann Coulter has been a vocal critic of Gingrich, labeling him a "Clintonian fraud" and warning that re-electing Obama would be preferable to nominating Gingrich. Other conservative commentators, including the editors of the National Review and Bob Dole, have also expressed concerns about Gingrich's candidacy, citing his lack of discipline and his tendency to alienate potential allies.

Ultimately, Gingrich's record and behavior raise serious questions about his fitness for the presidency, and his critics argue that he lacks the integrity and character necessary to lead the country.

reference: – The conservative media have apparently figured out that Newt Gingrich could actually win the Republican nomination, and they are responding with a breathtaking collective attack on the candidate. Politico rounds up the highlights, noting that "this is just a sampling of what's hitting Newt." In the National Review, Elliott Abrams writes that Gingrich often undermined Ronald Reagan, in what Politico calls "a devastating takedown." In another recent National Review piece being circulated by conservatives, the editors ramp up their opposition to Gingrich and urge conservatives not to support him. R. Emmett Tyrrell Jr. of the American Spectator compares Gingrich to Bill Clinton, calling them both "1960s generation narcissists" and noting that, for "girl hopping" Gingrich, "there are private encounters with the fair sex that doubtless will come out." On her website, Ann Coulter minces no words: "Re-elect Obama, Vote Newt!" Her column blasts Gingrich's "hotheaded arrogance." The Drudge Report linked to the Abrams piece with a banner reading, "Insider: Gingrich Repeatedly Insulted Reagan." The site, which has been viewed as pro-Romney in the past, also linked prominently to Tyrrell and Coulter. Tom DeLay told KTRH, "He’s not really a conservative. I mean, he’ll tell you what you want to hear. … When he was speaker, he was erratic, undisciplined." A top figure in the conservative media tells Politico that Republican leaders and media are beginning to realize, "It could happen, and it would be a disaster. All of us who were around and saw how he operated as speaker—there’s no one who’s not appalled by the prospect of what could happen." Politico notes that dozens of GOP lawmakers, governors, and other VIPs have said the same, privately, in recent weeks. Click for more on why tonight's debate is crucial for Gingrich.

final_summary:
A controversy has erupted over a "Win an Inn" essay contest organized by Janice Sage, the retiring owner of the 210-year-old Center Lovell Inn in Maine. The contest, which drew thousands of entrants from across the country, awarded the inn to Prince and Rose Adams, a couple from the US Virgin Islands who run a Caribbean-style restaurant on St. John. According to the contest rules, the winner would be chosen by an independent panel of local judges based on a 200-word essay submission accompanied by a $125 entry fee.

Numerous unsuccessful contestants have come forward alleging that the contest was unfairly rigged in favor of the Adamses, citing concerns about deceptive advertising practices. The group, known as the Center Lovell Contest Fair Practices Commission, claims that Sage misrepresented the contest as a fair competition. 

Maine State Police are investigating the matter to determine whether the contest complied with state law on games of skill. The investigation focuses on whether the contest's outcome was influenced by factors beyond the entrants' control. The police investigation is ongoing, and its scope does not extend to potential federal laws or civil liabilities.

Janice Sage, the contest organizer, declined to comment on the allegations, as did the Adamses, the winners of the contest. Kelley Prass Collins, founder of the Center Lovell Contest Fair Practices Commission, expressed concerns about the fairness of the contest. The investigation is expected to continue, and the contestants are advised to seek private counsel to explore further action.

history.draft:
A controversy has erupted over a "Win an Inn" essay contest organized by Janice Sage, the retiring owner of the 210-year-old Center Lovell Inn in Maine. The contest, which drew thousands of entrants from across the country, awarded the inn to Prince and Rose Adams, a couple from the US Virgin Islands who run a Caribbean-style restaurant on St. John. According to the contest rules, the winner would be chosen by an independent panel of local judges based on a 200-word essay submission accompanied by a $125 entry fee.

However, numerous unsuccessful contestants have come forward alleging that the contest was unfairly rigged in favor of the Adamses, citing deceptive advertising practices. The group, known as the Center Lovell Contest Fair Practices Commission, claims that Sage misrepresented the contest as a fair competition when, in reality, she allegedly favored business owners. This assertion is supported by the fact that Sage herself won the inn in a similar contest 22 years ago after submitting a winning essay.

Maine State Police are currently investigating the matter to determine whether the contest violated state law on games of skill, which prohibits operators from partially or completely controlling the outcome. The police investigation is focused specifically on this aspect of the law and does not extend to potential federal laws or civil liabilities. Despite receiving fewer than the targeted 7,500 entries, Sage reportedly secured enough funds for retirement through the contest, which required a $125 entry fee from each participant.

Janice Sage, the contest organizer, declined to comment on the allegations, as did the Adamses, the winners of the contest. However, Kelley Prass Collins, founder of the Center Lovell Contest Fair Practices Commission, expressed outrage over the situation, stating that Sage's actions were tantamount to coercing thousands of people into entering a contest they had little chance of winning. The investigation is expected to conclude within a week, and the contestants are advised to seek private counsel to explore further action.

reference: – Maine State Police are investigating whether an innkeeper violated state law in an essay contest with her 210-year-old country inn as the prize. Maine Department of Public Safety spokesman Steve McCausland said today an investigation was opened into whether the "Win an Inn" contest violated laws governing games of chance. The Boston Globe first reported that some contest losers felt the odds were stacked against them, contending that Center Lovell Inn owner Janice Sage marketed the contest to "dreamers" but instead awarded the prize to a couple with hospitality business experience in the Virgin Islands. "One of the many allegations against Janice Sage and the contest she sponsored is that the advertising of the contest ... was illegally deceptive and violated consumer-rights regulations, intentionally coercing thousands of people to enter a contest that they never had an actual chance of winning," says the founder of the Center Lovell Contest Fair Practices Commission, a group made up of dozens of unhappy contestants. The entry fee was $125. Sage told the Globe she received fewer than the 7,500 entries she sought, but it was still enough to fund her retirement.

final_summary:
The United States and China, the world's two largest economies and emitters of greenhouse gases, have made a joint commitment to tackle climate change by setting ambitious targets to reduce their carbon emissions. Acknowledging that addressing this issue requires collective action, the two countries have recognized that neither nation can single-handedly resolve the problem. Despite accounting for approximately 40% of global emissions, the US and China understand that their combined efforts are crucial to making a meaningful impact.

The US plans to decrease its net greenhouse gas emissions by 26-28% below 2005 levels by 2025, doubling the pace of carbon reductions compared to the previous decade. This target is grounded in an extensive analysis of emission-reducing potential across various sectors, with projected benefits for public health, clean air, and energy security. Building on the Obama administration's 2009 pledge to lower emissions by 17% by 2020, the US is on track to meet this goal while fostering job creation and economic growth through the expansion of the clean energy sector.

China has announced a landmark commitment to peak its carbon dioxide emissions around 2030, with the possibility of reaching this milestone earlier. This represents a significant shift, given that China has been responsible for approximately 60% of global carbon dioxide emissions growth over the past 15 years. To achieve this goal, China aims to increase the proportion of its energy consumption derived from non-polluting sources, such as renewables and nuclear power, to around 20% by 2030. This will necessitate the development of an additional 800-1000 gigawatts of new power generation capacity from sources like wind, solar, and nuclear energy by 2030, equivalent to the current capacity of all coal-fired power plants in China or nearly the entire electricity generation capacity of the US.

By working together, the US and China hope to encourage other nations to adopt similarly ambitious targets, ultimately contributing to a robust global climate agreement in 2015. Their joint commitment serves as a significant step forward in the US-China relationship, marking a collaborative effort to address the pressing issue of climate change and pave the way for a more sustainable future.
