reference: – It passed in the wee hours, and by a wee margin: The Senate passed the biggest tax overhaul in more than 30 years Saturday morning 51-49. Tennessee's Sen. Bob Corker was the only Republican to side against the bill. With the House having passed its bill in November, Congress is just a few steps away from getting something on President Trump's desk. He tweeted in part, "Look forward to signing a final bill before Christmas!" Politico reports the final hours before the vote were filled with "horse trading" and some ridicule from Democrats: Minority Whip Dick Durbin tweeted a photo showing a page of the bill with semi-legible handwriting scrawled in the margin, saying, "Trying to review the #GOPTaxScam but they are making hand-written changes to brand new text as we speak – can anyone else read this?" Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer swung at Republicans for releasing a revamped 479-page bill that senators didn't have time to review. GOP Sen. Ron Johnson's take, per the AP: "You really don't read this kind of legislation." Among the numerous last-minute amendments that were debated: A controversial provision by GOP Sen. Pat Toomey that would have given Michigan's conservative Hillsdale College a pass on a new tax on university endowments was axed. Politico reports the House will on Monday formally assemble the conference committee that will work to get the House and Senate measures to jibe; the Senate hasn't commented on a formal conference yet. The Wall Street Journal offers a chart that illustrates key differences between the two bills.

final_summary:
Celia Barquín Arozamena, a 22-year-old Spanish golfer and Iowa State University student, was found murdered on a golf course in Ames, Iowa, on July 18, 2018. The victim, who had recently won the European Ladies' Amateur Championship, was discovered with multiple stab wounds to her head, neck, and upper body. 

Collin Daniel Richards, a 22-year-old man, was subsequently charged with first-degree murder. Authorities alleged that Richards had committed a random act of violence against Barquín Arozamena, who was described as an "outstanding team player" by the Royal Spanish Golf Federation. 

Richards had a history of run-ins with law enforcement, having previously been charged with domestic abuse, trespassing, public intoxication, and intimidation with a dangerous weapon. He had also served a seven-month prison sentence prior to the incident. 

According to the police complaint, Richards had expressed an "urge to rape and kill a woman" to an acquaintance in recent days. A police dog tracked Barquín Arozamena's scent to a homeless encampment near the golf course, where Richards was found with scratches on his face and a laceration on his left hand. Two pairs of shorts with bloodstains and a knife were recovered from Richards, which he allegedly gave to others after the attack. Richards was arrested after asking two acquaintances to drop him off near the encampment to retrieve his tent. 

Barquín Arozamena was a rising star in the golfing world, having won numerous awards and accolades throughout her career. She was a three-time all-conference selection, earned first-team All-Big 12 academic honors three times, and qualified for NCAA regionals in all four years she competed. In addition to her golf achievements, Barquín Arozamena was a civil engineering major at Iowa State University and had been named the school's female athlete of the year in 2018. 

Instead of being honored at a football game on Saturday, the team wore helmet decals with her initials to pay tribute to her memory. The university and the golfing community were shocked by her sudden and tragic death, with many expressing their condolences to her family and friends. The case remains under investigation, with authorities considering it a random act of violence.

history.draft:
Celia Barquín Arozamena, a 22-year-old Spanish golfer and Iowa State University student, was found murdered on a golf course in Ames, Iowa, on July 18, 2018. The victim, who had recently won the European Ladies' Amateur Championship, was discovered with multiple stab wounds to her head, neck, and upper body. 

Collin Daniel Richards, a 22-year-old man, was subsequently charged with first-degree murder. Authorities alleged that Richards had committed a random act of violence against Barquín Arozamena, who was described as an "outstanding team player" by the Royal Spanish Golf Federation. 

Richards had a history of run-ins with law enforcement, having previously been charged with domestic abuse, trespassing, public intoxication, and intimidation with a dangerous weapon. He had also served a seven-month prison sentence prior to the incident. 

According to the police complaint, Richards had expressed an "urge to rape and kill a woman" to an acquaintance in recent days. A police dog tracked Barquín Arozamena's scent to a homeless encampment near the golf course, where Richards was found with scratches on his face and a laceration on his left hand. Two pairs of shorts with bloodstains and a knife were recovered from Richards, which he allegedly gave to others after the attack. Richards was arrested after asking two acquaintances to drop him off near the encampment to retrieve his tent. 

Barquín Arozamena was a rising star in the golfing world, having won numerous awards and accolades throughout her career. She was a three-time all-conference selection, earned first-team All-Big 12 academic honors three times, and qualified for NCAA regionals in all four years she competed. In addition to her golf achievements, Barquín Arozamena was a civil engineering major at Iowa State University and had been named the school's female athlete of the year in 2018. 

Instead of being honored at a football game on Saturday, the team wore helmet decals with her initials to pay tribute to her memory. The university and the golfing community were shocked by her sudden and tragic death, with many expressing their condolences to her family and friends. The case remains under investigation, with authorities considering it a random act of violence.

reference: – A 22-year-old golfer headed for the height of her sport was instead murdered on the course. Wrapping up a civil engineering degree at Iowa State University, where she'd been named female athlete of the year, Spain's Celia Barquín Arozamena was found dead a short distance from her unattended golf bag on the Coldwater Golf Links in Ames around 10:30am Monday, reports the BBC. After determining Barquín was assaulted, police by evening had charged 22-year-old Collin Daniel Richards of no known address with first-degree murder, per a release. Details weren't provided, though WOI-TV reports Richards has a history of arrests for intimidation with a dangerous weapon, trespassing, and public intoxication dating back to 2015. "This is a tragic and senseless loss of a talented young woman and an acclaimed student athlete," Iowa State president Wendy Wintersteen says, per ESPN. "She was a player who was heading for the very top, without a doubt," adds the technical director of the Spanish Golf Federation, per the BBC. Ranked 69th in the country by Golfweek, Barquín had secured an invite to next year's Women's British Open with a victory at the European Ladies' Amateur championship in July. Considered one of the most accomplished players in Iowa State golf history, the Puente San Miguel native also became the Big 12 women's golf champion and only the third Iowa State women's golfer to compete in the US Women's Open earlier this year, per the Independent.

final_summary:
A recent decision by Transport for London (TFL) has left Uber facing a significant challenge as the city's transport authority declined to renew the ride-hailing giant's operating license. According to TFL, Uber is not a "fit and proper" private car hire operator due to its lack of corporate responsibility regarding public safety and security. The decision cites Uber's approach to handling serious criminal offenses and its use of software, known as Greyball, to block regulatory bodies from accessing its app and conducting regulatory or law enforcement duties.

Uber's current license expires on September 30th, giving the company time to appeal the decision in court. The firm's CEO, Dara Khosrowshahi, has confirmed that Uber will challenge the ruling, acknowledging that the company's reputation has taken a hit. In a letter to staff, Khosrowshahi stated that a bad reputation comes with significant costs, emphasizing the importance of acting with integrity and learning from past mistakes.

London Mayor Sadiq Khan expressed support for TFL's decision, stating that companies operating in the city must adhere to high standards, particularly concerning customer safety and security. Khan emphasized that innovation and new technologies should not compromise these fundamental requirements.

The decision has sparked a range of reactions from various stakeholders. While many Uber users oppose the move, citing the convenience and affordability offered by the service, others welcome the decision, highlighting the importance of addressing concerns over Uber's treatment of drivers and commitment to public safety. Critics of Uber, including traditional black-cab drivers and employment rights campaigners, have long argued that the company has consistently disregarded regulations and mistreated its drivers.

The revocation of Uber's license marks another significant setback for the company, which has faced numerous challenges globally due to concerns over safety and competition from established taxi industries. As Uber prepares to appeal the decision, the company faces pressure to reform its business model and address criticisms surrounding its treatment of drivers and commitment to public safety.

Transport for London says the car-hailing app was not "fit and proper to hold a private hire operator license." It cited Uber's approach to reporting serious criminal offenses and its "approach to explaining the use of Greyball in London"; that's the software Uber reportedly used to block regulatory bodies from gaining full access to the app, preventing "officials from undertaking regulator or law enforcement duties." The AP reports London Mayor Sadiq Khan said he supported the decision, saying any operator of taxi services in the city "needs to play by the rules." He says that "providing an innovative service must not be at the expense of customer safety and security." It can keep operating "until any appeal processes have been exhausted," per Transport for London.

final_summary:
In the days leading up to Black Friday, a group of determined shoppers has already begun camping out in front of Best Buy in Beaumont, California. Among them are Vicky Torres and Juanita Salas, who arrived at the store on the previous Wednesday, securing their spots in anticipation of the holiday shopping event. They plan to wait through the night, taking turns sleeping and keeping each other company, until the store opens its doors on Black Friday. Their goal is to snag a 50-inch high-definition television for a significantly reduced price of $199.

Store manager Eric Tschudy notes that the number of "campers" has increased each year, and the store appreciates the dedication shown by these early birds. For Torres and Salas, camping out is a strategic move, as they are on a fixed income and cannot afford to splurge on expensive gifts during the regular season. By camping out, they aim to secure the best deals available on the items they want.

Some people passing by may view their actions as extreme or foolish, while others express admiration for their dedication. Torres and Salas have received requests from fellow shoppers asking them to save spots in line, which highlights the growing trend of Black Friday camping. Supporting her efforts is Jacob Alva, who provides essential services such as food, phone charging, and bathroom breaks. He feels safe leaving his wife's spot unattended due to the presence of police and security personnel. As the big day approaches, Torres and Salas remain committed to their mission, fueled by hot coffee, snacks, and determination.

reference: – Some stores are trying to resist Black Friday madness by refusing to open on Thanksgiving, but for die-hard shoppers, it's never too early to get ready for the mega-sales. Take Vicky Torres and Juanita Salas, who were already bundled up and camped out in front of the Beaumont, Calif., Best Buy weeks before everyone starts defrosting their turkeys. They've been there since last Wednesday, ABC 7 reports, and they're "good to go" for the duration, Torres says, with warm clothing, hot coffee, and food. "The point is to get the sales, because everybody is on a fixed income and we don't have that kind of money to splurge," says Salas' husband, Jacob Alva. The two women alternate who stays overnight to save their places in line, though there's no indication from reports that anyone else is actually in said line yet. If both women have to leave for a bathroom break or for any other reason, Alva takes over, he tells the San Bernardino County Sun; he also charges their phones and brings them sustenance. He's not particularly worried about his wife's safety, claiming "the cops and security out here are really good." Torres and Salas did the same "fun" thing last year—Best Buy's Beaumont manager tells the Sun "we love Vicky here." So what's their holiday must-have this time around? A 50-inch high-def TV for $199, they tell ABC. (Cyber Monday might be even bigger than Black Friday.)

final_summary:
Karl Lagerfeld, the creative director of French fashion house Chanel, unveiled his latest fall-winter 2014-2015 collection in an unconventional setting – a mock supermarket within the Grand Palais, a venue typically reserved for glamorous fashion shows. The space was transformed into a sprawling supermarket, complete with rows of Chanel-branded products, including pasta, cheese, sauce, detergent, and other everyday essentials, although the event was not meant to resemble a real supermarket. 

Lagerfeld's vision was to blur the lines between high-end fashion and everyday life, drawing inspiration from the idea that luxury should be accessible and relatable. He stated, "For me, the idea of the supermarket is a reflection of our times." The designer's team created over 500 unique labels and displayed more than 100,000 items, some of which will be donated to charity.

Models walked down the catwalk, pushing shopping carts and carrying metallic baskets adorned with Chanel's iconic logo, as they pretended to browse through the supermarket aisles. The collection itself featured oversized tweed jackets paired with skinny pants and sneakers, as well as comfortable-looking jogging suits and bright pink shredded leggings. The soundtrack included loud pop music and public announcements, adding to the immersive experience.

The show was attended by A-list celebrities, including singer Rihanna and actress Keira Knightley, who sat in the front row alongside her husband, Klaxons singer James Righton. After the show, the audience was invited to take home some of the Chanel products, leading to a chaotic scene as attendees rushed to grab items from the shelves. Security guards had to intervene to maintain order.

In contrast to Lagerfeld's supermarket-themed show, other designers showcased their collections in more traditional settings. Iris van Herpen's show featured a dramatic and unsettling theme, with models appearing to suffocate in plastic bags. Meanwhile, Alexander McQueen's Sarah Burton presented a cryptic collection with an 18th-century vibe, featuring A-line skirts and lace-up boots. Valentino's Maria Grazia Chiuri and Pierpaolo Piccioli drew inspiration from the swinging sixties, incorporating diaphanous fabrics and pop art elements into their designs.

Overall, Paris Fashion Week 2014 was marked by innovative and thought-provoking presentations from various designers, each pushing the boundaries of fashion and creativity.

reference: – Chanel's show at Paris Fashion Week yesterday had a somewhat pedestrian theme: The Grand Palais, where the show was held, was turned into a Chanel supermarket. There were rows of food and household items, all with Chanel-branded names like Coco beer and Tweed cola, and models pushed shopping carts or carried baskets through the aisles while music and fake announcements played ("the young Marine is waiting for her parents at the cashiers," for example); signs advertised "sales"—like "1 for the price of 2" and "50% markup." Altogether, more than 100,000 items were displayed with more than 500 different labels; some will be donated to charity, Reuters reports, but others were grabbed by audience members after the show ... and things got so crazy that security guards had to intervene, with the AP noting fashionistas' bags were actually searched upon exit, with stolen goods removed, though one apparently made off with a Chanel doormat. "For me the supermarket is the pop art of today," said designer Karl Lagerfeld ... though of course he also said he himself rarely actually visits supermarkets. Chanel last made headlines for featuring models in sneakers.

final_summary:
Harper Lee, the renowned author of "To Kill a Mockingbird," has filed a trademark infringement lawsuit against the Monroe County Heritage Museum in Monroeville, Alabama, alleging that the museum has exploited her fame and the prestige of her Pulitzer-winning book without offering compensation. The lawsuit, filed in federal court, seeks to prohibit the museum from using the name of Lee's book to promote its activities or sell merchandise.

According to the complaint, the museum has become increasingly brazen in its trademark violations, particularly since Lee suffered a stroke and is now in poor health. reference: – Harper Lee is apparently as litigious as she is reclusive. The To Kill a Mockingbird author has, in the Guardian's words, "shocked" her hometown of Monroeville, Ala., by suing its local museum. The 87-year-old's accusation: that the nonprofit is making use of her fame without compensation. The trademark infringement lawsuit, filed Oct. 15, calls out the Monroe County Heritage Museum's website and gift shop sales of Mockingbird-branded goods. The museum says that shop rakes in a measly $28,000 annually, which funds a few "pitifully paid" employees, and that it has sold such goods for two decades, reports AL.com. But the suit accuses the museum of taking advantage of the book, and cites its web URL as example: tokillamockingbird.com. Just 30,000 visitors head to the museum annually, and it warns that the suit could prove its undoing. Making the story even more contentious: Locals aren't sure if Lee is actually the driver of the suit, and the museum suspects her "handlers" are taking advantage of her declining health. More confusing still, the suit suggests the museum is exploiting her health by committing bolder trademark violations, notes AL.com. Lee is deaf and nearly blind, and currently living in an assisted-living facility in Monroeville. The Guardian doesn't exactly make clear who is believed to be the driver, but floats one name: Tonya Carter, a lawyer married to Truman Capote's cousin (a longtime friend of Lee's). Many believe Lee handed Carter power of attorney, though the Guardian could find no paper trail proving that.

final_summary:
A 19-year-old unarmed black man, Tony Robinson, was fatally shot by Officer Matt Kenny, a 12-year-veteran of the Madison Police Department, in Madison, Wisconsin, on Friday, March 6, 2015. According to authorities, the shooting occurred after Kenny entered Robinson's apartment in response to a disturbance call and was allegedly assaulted by Robinson. The incident sparked widespread outrage and protests in the city, with dozens of people taking to the streets to express their discontent over the latest in a series of high-profile deaths of unarmed black individuals at the hands of police officers.

Madison Police Chief Mike Koval acknowledged the community's anger and frustration, stating that he understood the community's concerns and promised that his department would respect and protect the rights of protesters. He emphasized the importance of peaceful and non-destructive demonstrations, echoing the sentiments of Robinson's family, who had requested calm and respectful gatherings. 

The shooting occurred just days after the US Justice Department announced that it would not bring federal charges against Darren Wilson, the white former Ferguson, Missouri, police officer who fatally shot 18-year-old Michael Brown, an unarmed black teenager, in August 2014. The decision reignited tensions and protests nationwide, highlighting ongoing concerns about systemic racism and police brutality in the United States.

Officer Matt Kenny has been placed on administrative leave pending the outcome of an investigation by the state's Division of Criminal Investigation. Kenny has a history of using lethal force in the line of duty, having shot and killed a suspect in a "suicide by cop-type" situation in 2007. He received commendations for his actions during the incident, which was deemed justified by the authorities.

Tony Robinson's family and friends remembered him as a kind and gentle soul, with his grandmother describing him as "beautiful, beautiful." His mother, Andrea Irwin, expressed her shock and grief, stating that she could not comprehend what had happened to her son. The family has yet to be allowed to view Robinson's body, with investigators citing it as evidence.

As the investigation continues, the community remains on edge, seeking answers and justice for Tony Robinson's untimely death. Madison Mayor Paul Soglin pledged transparency and openness in communicating the findings of the investigation, stating that "our community has many questions, questions that I share." He added that there will be answers.

Robinson's past, including a felony conviction for armed robbery in 2014, has raised questions about his involvement in the incident leading up to his death. However, Police Chief Koval declined to comment on Robinson's character, deeming it "inappropriate" given the circumstances. Neighbors described Robinson as a friendly and outgoing individual who often interacted with those around him, contradicting some of the negative perceptions surrounding his past behavior.

The aftermath of the shooting has highlighted the need for greater understanding and accountability within law enforcement agencies, particularly in regards to the use of force and the treatment of minority communities. As the investigation unfolds, the people of Madison and beyond continue to demand justice and reform, seeking to ensure that such tragedies are prevented in the future.

reference: – Madison, Wis., saw peaceful protests yesterday in the wake of the shooting death of 19-year-old Tony Robinson by a police officer, with both Robinson's family and police urging non-violent demonstrations and the police chief vowing to "defend, facilitate, foster those First Amendment rights of assembly and freedom of speech." Robinson "was unarmed," says chief Mike Koval. "That's going to make this all the more complicated for the investigators, for the public to accept." The police response marks what the AP calls a "stark contrast" to Ferguson. "Our community has many questions, questions that I share. There will be answers," said Madison's mayor. "When the answers come, we will be open and transparent in communicating them." A 2014 state law mandates such cases be investigated by an outside agency, notes the AP. Officer Matt Kenny, a 12-year veteran who Koval said was injured in the struggle that ended in Robinson's death, has been placed on administrative leave pending investigation. He had been involved in a 2007 shooting death of a suspect, reports NBC; that case was later ruled to be "suicide by cop" and he was exonerated. As for Robinson's criminal history, "I'm not here to do a character workup on someone who lost his life less than 24 hours ago," said Koval. The AP, however, checked state records and found that Robinson pleaded guilty in October to felony armed robbery in connection with a home invasion; he was sentenced to three years' probation. A neighbor says she asked him about the robbery, which she deemed out of character. "He felt he was under a lot of pressure from the others to do what he did," she says. "He told me he would never do anything like that again."

final_summary:
A gunman identified as Dr. Henry Bello, a former doctor at the Bronx-Lebanon Hospital in New York City, opened fire with an assault rifle inside the hospital on Friday, killing a woman and wounding at least six other people before taking his own life. According to law enforcement officials, Bello, who wore a white lab coat and carried identification, died from a self-inflicted gunshot wound on the 17th floor of the hospital. 

Bello had previously worked at the hospital for approximately six months before leaving due to allegations of sexual harassment. His past also included a charge of sexual abuse stemming from an incident in Manhattan years prior. The shooting occurred at around 2:50 pm, with reports indicating that Bello entered the hospital with the rifle concealed in his lab coat and began firing, sending staff members scrambling for cover.

Emergency responders were eventually able to enter the hospital after Bello took his own life, allowing them to rush to the aid of the injured. Five of the six wounded individuals were reported to be in serious condition from gunshot wounds sustained during the attack. The identity of the woman killed in the shooting has not been released.

Witnesses described chaotic scenes within the hospital, with medical personnel using a fire hose as a makeshift tourniquet to treat one victim's injuries. Others recalled the harrowing sight of Bello, who set himself on fire before shooting himself in the head, fleeing down a hallway. The attack appears to be a tragic event that resulted in significant loss of life and injury, with Mayor Bill de Blasio stating that it was a senseless act of violence.

final_summary:
The Obama administration announced a significant revision to the Affordable Care Act (ACA) on July 2, 2013, delaying the enforcement of a key provision requiring large employers to provide health insurance to their workers or face a penalty until 2015. The original deadline for compliance was January 1, 2014, but the Treasury Department pushed it back a year due to concerns raised by businesses about the complexity of the reporting requirements. According to the Treasury Department, the delay aims to give employers more time to adjust to the new system and make necessary adaptations to their health benefits, allowing them to test the new reporting systems and simplify the process.

In a White House blog post, senior adviser Valerie Jarrett stated that the delay would enable employers to make health coverage more affordable and accessible for their workers. However, the delay has sparked controversy among lawmakers, with Republicans seizing on the opportunity to criticize the ACA and call for its repeal. Senate Minority Leader Mitch McConnell described the delay as a sign that the law needs to be replaced with "common-sense reforms" that lower costs for Americans. Senator John Barrasso labeled the administration's action a "cynical political ploy" aimed at delaying the "coming train wreck" associated with the ACA until after the 2014 elections.

Some Democrats, including Senator Harry Reid's office, defended the administration's decision, citing a willingness to be flexible and work with stakeholders to ensure successful implementation of the law. However, others, such as Senator Max Baucus, one of the law's primary architects, expressed concerns about the potential impact on small businesses and the overall effectiveness of the law. Employer groups, including the US Chamber of Commerce, welcomed the delay, arguing that it provides much-needed time for clarification of the rules and adaptation of health coverage and reporting systems.

The delay has also raised concerns about the potential consequences for the law's expansion of healthcare coverage, with some experts predicting that it may lead to more people turning to the law's insurance exchanges for coverage. The delay does not affect the individual mandate, which requires most taxpayers to either purchase insurance or pay a penalty, nor does it impact other central provisions of the law, such as the establishment of health insurance marketplaces in the states. Despite the administration's efforts to portray the delay as a positive development, opponents of the law argue that it highlights the complexities and flaws inherent in the legislation.

Senior Adviser Valerie Jarrett stated in a White House blog post that the delay would enable employers to test the new reporting systems and simplify the process, ultimately making health coverage more affordable and accessible for their workers.

However, the delay has sparked controversy among lawmakers, with Republicans seizing on the opportunity to criticize the ACA and call for its repeal. Senate Minority Leader Mitch McConnell described the delay as a sign that the law needs to be replaced with "common-sense reforms" that lower costs for Americans. Senator John Barrasso labeled the administration's action a "cynical political ploy" aimed at delaying the "coming train wreck" associated with the ACA until after the 2014 elections.

Some Democrats, including Senator Harry Reid's office, defended the administration's decision, citing a willingness to be flexible and work with stakeholders to ensure successful implementation of the law. However, others, such as Senator Max Baucus, one of the law's primary architects, expressed concerns about the potential impact on small businesses and the overall effectiveness of the law.

Employer groups, including the US Chamber of Commerce, welcomed the delay, arguing that it provides much-needed time for clarification of the rules and adaptation of health coverage and reporting systems. The delay has also raised concerns about the potential consequences for the law's expansion of healthcare coverage, with some experts predicting that it may lead to more people turning to the law's insurance exchanges for coverage.

The delay does not affect the individual mandate, which requires most taxpayers to either purchase insurance or pay a penalty, nor does it impact other central provisions of the law, such as the establishment of health insurance marketplaces in the states. Despite the administration's efforts to portray the delay as a positive development, opponents of the law argue that it highlights the complexities and flaws inherent in the legislation.

Senator John Barrasso labeled the administration's action a "cynical political ploy" aimed at delaying the "coming train wreck" associated with the ACA until after the 2014 elections.

Some Democrats, including Senator Harry Reid's office, defended the administration's decision, citing a willingness to be flexible and work with stakeholders to ensure successful implementation of the law. However, others, such as Senator Max Baucus, one of the law's primary architects, expressed concerns about the potential impact on small businesses and the overall effectiveness of the law. reference: – ObamaCare is still going into effect in 2014, but one key component of it is being temporarily shelved. The White House today postponed by one year a rule requiring employers to either provide coverage to employees or face hefty fines, reports the Wall Street Journal. The rule will take effect in 2015, instead of 2014. "We have heard concerns about the complexity of the requirements and the need for more time to implement them effectively," writes Treasury official Mark Mazur in a blog post. The Journal explains that while most employers already provide such coverage, restaurants and retail companies in particular—both of which rely on low-wage workers—were resisting. The rule affects businesses with more than 50 workers. The Hill calls the decision "stunning" and says it "represents an enormous victory for businesses that had lobbied against the healthcare law." It also notes that the move pushes back the start of the controversial provision until after the 2014 midterms, which should be good news for vulnerable Democrats. The New York Times reports that other key parts of ObamaCare, including the establishment of "exchanges" where people can buy insurance, will take effect as planned in 2014. But "it will be difficult for officials running the exchanges to know who is entitled to subsidies if they are not able to confirm whether employers are offering insurance to their employees," writes Jackie Calmes.

final_summary:
According to recent announcements, Disney has outlined a comprehensive plan to release multiple Star Wars movies over the next few years. At least five new titles are scheduled for release between 2016 and 2020. 

The first installment, "Rogue One: A Star Wars Story," is set to hit theaters on December 16, 2016, under the direction of Gareth Edwards. The film tells the story of a group of Rebel fighters attempting to steal the plans for the Death Star following the formation of the Galactic Empire. The cast features Felicity Jones, Diego Luna, Riz Ahmed, Ben Mendelsohn, and several other notable actors.

In 2017, "Star Wars Episode VIII" is scheduled to premiere on December 15, marking a change from its initially planned release date of May 2017. Directed and written by Rian Johnson, the film will feature a mix of returning cast members from "The Force Awakens." While the exact plot remains unknown, Lawrence Kasdan has expressed confidence in Johnson's vision, stating that he will create something unique and intriguing.

A spin-off film focused on the character of Han Solo, titled "Solo: A Star Wars Story," is scheduled for release on May 25, 2018. Directed by Phil Lord and Christopher Miller, the movie explores the early life of the iconic smuggler, thief, and scoundrel. Although the cast has yet to be officially announced, the story is expected to delve into Han's formative years, leading up to his encounter with Luke Skywalker and Obi-Wan Kenobi in the Mos Eisley cantina.

"Star Wars Episode IX" is slated for release in 2019, with director J.J. Abrams taking over from Colin Trevorrow, although Trevorrow was originally assigned to direct. Although details about the plot are scarce, Chris Terrio is credited as the writer, suggesting a continuation of the narrative established in "Episode VIII." 

Lastly, a spin-off film centered around Boba Fett, another beloved character from the franchise, is reportedly due for release in 2020. Unfortunately, little information is currently available regarding the project, including the identity of the director or writer.

This ambitious schedule represents a significant departure from the previous pace of Star Wars releases, which spanned nearly four decades without such frequency. If all goes according to plan, fans can expect to witness five new Star Wars films within a four-year period, a prospect that has generated excitement among enthusiasts worldwide.

As the Independent puts it, "Start sourcing the ultimate Star Wars costume now, because less than a year is not long to wait." (See how George Lucas walked back his "epic slam" on Disney.)

final_summary:
A former CIA officer, Sabrina De Sousa, is set to be extradited to Italy to serve a four-year prison sentence for her alleged role in the 2003 CIA-led kidnapping of Egyptian cleric Hassan Mustafa Osama Nasr, also known as Abu Omar. However, Abu Omar himself has disputed the charges, claiming that De Sousa is a scapegoat and that the true culprits are more senior officials who have enjoyed immunity. According to Abu Omar, De Sousa was not directly involved in the operation, and the US administration sacrificed her to save its own interests. 

Abu Omar's statement marks a significant development in the case, which has been shrouded in controversy since its exposure in 2005. De Sousa, who was working undercover for the CIA in Italy at the time of the kidnapping, denies personal involvement in the abduction and claims she was only a translator in an early-stage discussion of the operation. Despite her denials, De Sousa has been convicted in absentia by an Italian court alongside 25 other Americans. 

De Sousa's lawyers have attempted to persuade the US government to intervene on her behalf, but the administration has remained silent. In a surprising move, De Sousa has written an open letter to Pope Francis, appealing for his intervention in the matter. She argues that her extradition will not lead to real accountability for the abuses she suffered and that the US government should take responsibility for its actions. 

Meanwhile, Abu Omar has expressed sympathy for De Sousa, stating that she should be pardoned and that he does not wish for her to suffer the same fate as him. The case has become increasingly complex, with the Italian government poised to extradite De Sousa despite the US government's reluctance to intervene. Experts believe that Italy may use the situation to extract concessions from the US, particularly in light of the recent tensions between the two countries over the murder of Italian citizen Giulio Regeni in Cairo. 

As De Sousa prepares to face her fate, she remains resolute, stating that she hopes to counter the charges against her in Italy rather than being silenced. Italian President Sergio Mattarella has granted clemency to other defendants in this case, raising hopes that De Sousa may also receive similar treatment.

reference: – A former CIA officer says she's days away from being sent to an Italian prison and blames the US government for not doing more to help her, ABC News reports. In 2003, the CIA abducted Egyptian cleric and terror suspect Osama Mustafa Hassan Nasr from Milan, Italy, under its "extraordinary rendition" program. Nasr says he was transferred to Egypt, where he was tortured. At the time, Sabrina de Sousa was working undercover for the CIA in Italy but tells NPR she was 150 miles away on a ski trip with her son during the week Nasr was kidnapped. Nevertheless, de Sousa was one of 26 Americans convicted of violating Italian law by abducting Nasr. She faces four years in prison, the Guardian reports. De Sousa has been living in Portugal and fighting extradition—without any apparent help from the US government. "The US government won't intervene because they don't want me implicating anyone else as I try to counter the charges against me," de Sousa tells ABC. She says she feels "abandoned and betrayed" by her own country. De Sousa appealed her extradition to Portugal's highest court but lost that appeal Wednesday. She believes her next stop is an Italian prison and has asked Pope Francis for help. In a strange twist, while the US government isn't speaking up for de Sousa, the man she is said to have helped kidnap is. “The US administration sacrificed them," Nasr tells the Guardian. "All of those higher up in the hierarchy are enjoying their immunity." He says de Sousa is a "scapegoat." So far, none of the 26 Americans convicted by Italy have seen jail time.

final_summary:
On Sunday, thousands of Native Americans and their allies gathered outside the University of Minnesota's TCF Bank Stadium, protesting the Washington Redskins' name ahead of the team's game against the Minnesota Vikings. The protest drew an estimated 3,500 to 4,000 people, though organizers claimed the turnout was higher. The protesters, including students and activists, chanted slogans such as "Change the Name Now" and "Stop Racism in the NFL," while waving banners and beating drums. Speakers from across the country addressed the crowd, criticizing the Redskins' moniker as a racial slur and advocating for its removal. Representative Betty McCollum (D-Minn.) highlighted the painful history of Native American genocide and called on the NFL to reconsider the team's name, stating, "If there is any decency in the NFL, the time is now to change the name." 

Some Redskins fans showed support for the team's name, claiming it honored Native American warrior culture. Others dismissed the protest, with one Vikings fan speculating that the controversy might spread to other teams. The University of Minnesota had requested that the Redskins refrain from using their name and logo at the stadium, citing the institution's affirmative action, diversity, and equal opportunity policy. The Shakopee Mdewakanton Sioux Community, which donated $10 million toward the stadium's construction, also opposed the Redskins' name. Despite this, a Vikings spokesperson stated that the team would allow the Redskins to use their name and logo at the game. The decision sparked criticism from the university and Native American advocates, who argued that the use of the name perpetuates racism and disrespect toward Native Americans. Meanwhile, a traffic incident involving two buses carrying the Redskins team occurred on a highway exit ramp en route to the stadium, but fortunately, no serious injuries were reported, and the team announced that the accident would not impact the game.

The protesters, including students and activists, chanted slogans such as "Change the Name Now" and "Stop Racism in the NFL," while waving banners and beating drums. 

Speakers from across the country addressed the crowd, decrying the Redskins' moniker as a racial slur and calling for its removal. In contrast, some Redskins fans expressed support for the team's name, arguing that it honored Native American warrior culture. However, others were dismissive of the protest, with one Vikings fan suggesting that the controversy could spread to other teams, such as the Vikings themselves.

The university's concerns were echoed by the Shakopee Mdewakanton Sioux Community, which donated $10 million towards the construction of the stadium and opposes the Redskins' name. 

A Vikings spokesperson stated that the team was considering the university's request but ultimately decided to allow the Redskins to use their name and logo at the game. The team's decision was met with criticism from the university and Native American advocates, who argued that the use of the name perpetuated racism and disrespect towards Native Americans.

Meanwhile, a separate incident occurred when two buses carrying the Redskins team collided on a highway exit ramp en route to the stadium. Fortunately, no serious injuries were reported, and the team announced that the accident would not impact the game.

history.draft:
On Sunday, thousands of Native Americans and their allies gathered outside the University of Minnesota's TCF Bank Stadium, protesting the Washington Redskins' name ahead of the team's game against the Minnesota Vikings. The protest drew an estimated 3,500 to 4,000 people, though organizers claimed the turnout was higher. The protesters, including students and activists, chanted slogans such as "Change the Name Now" and "Stop Racism in the NFL," while waving banners and beating drums. Speakers from across the country addressed the crowd, criticizing the Redskins' moniker as a racial slur and advocating for its removal. Representative Betty McCollum (D-Minn.) highlighted the painful history of Native American genocide and called on the NFL to reconsider the team's name, stating, "If there is any decency in the NFL, the time is now to change the name." 

Some Redskins fans showed support for the team's name, claiming it honored Native American warrior culture. Others dismissed the protest, with one Vikings fan speculating that the controversy might spread to other teams. The University of Minnesota had requested that the Redskins refrain from using their name and logo at the stadium, citing the institution's affirmative action, diversity, and equal opportunity policy. The Shakopee Mdewakanton Sioux Community, which donated $10 million toward the stadium's construction, also opposed the Redskins' name. Despite this, a Vikings spokesperson stated that the team would allow the Redskins to use their name and logo at the game. The decision sparked criticism from the university and Native American advocates, who argued that the use of the name perpetuates racism and disrespect toward Native Americans. Meanwhile, a traffic incident involving two buses carrying the Redskins team occurred on a highway exit ramp en route to the stadium, but fortunately, no serious injuries were reported, and the team announced that the accident would not impact the game.

reference: – The Washington Redskins are having a rough time of it today: Things started with a crash this morning involving two of the team's buses, which were en route to play the Vikings in Minneapolis, reports the AP. A police escort in Minnesota crashed into a guardrail around 8:30am; the team bus immediately behind it braked to avoid hitting the cop car, but second bus rear-ended the first. No injuries. Arriving in Minneapolis, the team was met with chants of "Who are we? Not your mascot!" from some 3,200 protesting Native Americans who were none too happy with the "Redskins" moniker. "My Hubby Did Not Fight in Iraq To Be Called A 'Redskin,' read one sign. The protest raised familiar arguments, with one Redskins fan calling the name a "respectful" one in which Native Americans should "take pride." The name is nothing more than "a racial slur," counters one Democratic rep at the protest, citing Minnesota's "11 proud tribal nations. This was a government-funded policy of genocide." Indeed, as the Washington Post reports, Minneapolis is home to a sizable Native American presence, perhaps contributing to the size of the demonstration. The issue is compounded by the University of Minnesota, which currently leases its football stadium to the Vikings for a hefty $300,000 per game. With 1,100 students who identify as Native American, the university had in August demanded that the Redskins limit use of the mascot, logo, and team name during today's game.
